kids.question: Where did Solomon go to worship God at the beginning of his reign?
kids.explanation: 2 Chronicles 1:3 — "So Solomon, and all the congregation with him, went to the high place that was at Gibeon."

kids.question: What did Solomon ask God to give him?
kids.explanation: 2 Chronicles 1:10 — "Give me now wisdom and knowledge, that I may go out and come in before this people."

kids.question: Because Solomon asked for wisdom, what did God also give him?
kids.explanation: 2 Chronicles 1:12 — "Wisdom and knowledge is granted unto thee; and I will give thee riches, and wealth, and honour, such as none of the kings have had that have been before thee."

teens.question: What was kept at Gibeon that Solomon went there to worship?
teens.option_a: The tabernacle of the congregation and the brasen altar that Moses had made
teens.option_b: The ark of the covenant
teens.option_c: The original stone tablets
teens.option_d: The altar of incense
teens.explanation: 2 Chronicles 1:5 — "Moreover the brasen altar, that Bezaleel the son of Uri...had made, he put before the tabernacle of the LORD."

teens.question: How many burnt offerings did Solomon offer at Gibeon?
teens.option_a: A thousand burnt offerings
teens.option_b: Five hundred burnt offerings
teens.option_c: Three thousand burnt offerings
teens.option_d: Seven burnt offerings
teens.explanation: 2 Chronicles 1:6 — "And Solomon went up thither to the brasen altar before the LORD...and offered a thousand burnt offerings upon it."

teens.question: What did God say to Solomon in the night at Gibeon?
teens.option_a: Ask what I shall give thee
teens.option_b: I am the LORD thy God, fear not
teens.option_c: Write down all my statutes
teens.option_d: Build me a house in Jerusalem
teens.explanation: 2 Chronicles 1:7 — "In that night did God appear unto Solomon, and said unto him, Ask what I shall give thee."

teens.question: What did Solomon say he had NOT asked for when God offered him anything?
teens.option_a: Riches, wealth, honour, the life of his enemies, or long life
teens.option_b: Victory in war
teens.option_c: A great kingdom
teens.option_d: Fame among all nations
teens.explanation: 2 Chronicles 1:11 — "Thou hast not asked riches, wealth, or honour, nor the life of thine enemies, neither yet hast asked long life."

teens.question: What did Solomon import in large numbers to build up his military strength?
teens.option_a: Horses and chariots from Egypt and from all other countries
teens.option_b: Iron weapons from the Philistines
teens.option_c: War elephants from Arabia
teens.option_d: Bronze armour from Tyre
teens.explanation: 2 Chronicles 1:16 — "And Solomon had horses brought out of Egypt, and linen yarn: the king's merchants received the linen yarn at a price."

adults.question: Where was the ark of God kept when Solomon went to Gibeon, and who had brought it there?
adults.option_a: In Jerusalem, in the tent that David had pitched for it
adults.option_b: In Shiloh where it had always been
adults.option_c: At Gibeon inside the tabernacle
adults.option_d: At Bethel with the Levites
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 1:4 — "But the ark of God had David brought up from Kirjath-jearim to the place which David had prepared for it: for he had pitched a tent for it at Jerusalem."

adults.question: On what basis did Solomon frame his request for wisdom — what role did he acknowledge he was called to fill?
adults.option_a: He was made king over a people like the dust of the earth in multitude, and needed wisdom to lead them
adults.option_b: He was the youngest of David's sons and felt unqualified
adults.option_c: He had been charged by David to build the temple and needed knowledge for it
adults.option_d: He was surrounded by hostile nations and needed military counsel
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 1:10 — "Give me now wisdom and knowledge...for who can judge this thy people, that is so great?"

adults.question: What does God's statement "wisdom and knowledge is granted unto thee" reveal about the nature of Solomon's reign from its outset?
adults.option_a: That God himself confirmed and equipped Solomon's rule, making his wisdom a divine gift rather than human achievement
adults.option_b: That Solomon had studied all available scrolls diligently
adults.option_c: That the priests at Gibeon had trained him from childhood
adults.option_d: That the gift was conditional upon his completing the temple
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 1:12 — "Wisdom and knowledge is granted unto thee; and I will give thee riches, and wealth, and honour," showing God's direct bestowal.

adults.question: What was the scale of Solomon's commercial activity in horses and chariots according to this chapter?
adults.option_a: He gathered chariots and horsemen so that he had 1,400 chariots and 12,000 horsemen, buying at fixed prices for Hittite and Syrian kings
adults.option_b: He imported only from Egypt
adults.option_c: He relied entirely on tribute from conquered nations
adults.option_d: He inherited David's army without expansion
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 1:14 — "And Solomon gathered chariots and horsemen: and he had a thousand and four hundred chariots, and twelve thousand horsemen."

adults.question: What economic detail shows the abundance of Solomon's kingdom regarding silver and gold?
adults.option_a: The king made silver and gold as plenteous in Jerusalem as stones, and cedar as sycamore trees
adults.option_b: Gold was stored only in the temple treasury
adults.option_c: Silver was rationed among the twelve tribes
adults.option_d: Taxes were paid only in cedar timber
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 1:15 — "And the king made silver and gold at Jerusalem as plenteous as stones, and cedar trees made he as the sycamore trees that are in the vale for abundance."

adults.question: What was the price of a chariot from Egypt and of a horse, revealing the trade economics of Solomon's time?
adults.option_a: A chariot cost six hundred shekels of silver and a horse one hundred and fifty shekels
adults.option_b: A chariot cost one hundred shekels and a horse fifty shekels
adults.option_c: A chariot cost a talent of gold and a horse ten shekels
adults.option_d: Prices varied by season and were not fixed
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 1:17 — "And they fetched up, and brought forth out of Egypt a chariot for six hundred shekels of silver, and an horse for an hundred and fifty."

adults.question: What role did Solomon's merchants play in the regional horse trade?
adults.option_a: They exported horses and chariots to all the kings of the Hittites and of Syria
adults.option_b: They only supplied Solomon's own army
adults.option_c: They operated under Egyptian license
adults.option_d: They sold only within Israel's borders
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 1:17 — "And so brought they out horses for all the kings of the Hittites, and for the kings of Syria, by their means."

adults.question: How does the Chronicler's account of Solomon's accession differ in emphasis from Kings by starting with the Gibeon episode?
adults.option_a: The Chronicler highlights Solomon's wholehearted seeking of God as the foundation of his blessed reign
adults.option_b: The Chronicler is more interested in military history
adults.option_c: The Chronicler omits the covenant with Pharaoh
adults.option_d: The Chronicler focuses on the tax system first
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 1:1 — "And Solomon the son of David was strengthened in his kingdom, and the LORD his God was with him, and magnified him exceedingly," setting a theological tone for the reign.

kids.question: What building project did Solomon plan and prepare for?
kids.option_a: A house for the name of the LORD and a house for his kingdom
kids.option_b: A great city wall around Jerusalem
kids.explanation: 2 Chronicles 2:1 — "And Solomon determined to build an house for the name of the LORD, and an house for his kingdom."

kids.question: What did Solomon ask the king of Tyre to send him for the building project?
kids.option_a: A man cunning to work in gold, silver, brass, and other materials
kids.option_b: A thousand soldiers to guard the temple
kids.option_c: Ships to carry cedar from Lebanon
kids.option_d: Silver enough to pay all the workers
kids.explanation: 2 Chronicles 2:7 — "Send me now therefore a man cunning to work in gold, and in silver, and in brass, and in iron."

kids.question: What did Solomon promise to send King Huram of Tyre in exchange for the timber and skilled workers?
kids.explanation: 2 Chronicles 2:15 — "Now therefore the wheat, and the barley, the oil, and the wine, which my lord hath spoken of, let him send unto his servants."

teens.question: How many labourers did Solomon conscript to carry burdens for the building project?
teens.option_a: Seventy thousand to bear burdens and eighty thousand hewers in the mountain
teens.option_b: Twenty thousand to carry and forty thousand to cut stone
teens.option_c: Thirty thousand men total sent in shifts
teens.option_d: One hundred thousand men from the tribes of Israel
teens.explanation: 2 Chronicles 2:2 — "And Solomon told out threescore and ten thousand men to bear burdens, and fourscore thousand to hew in the mountain."

teens.question: What did Solomon say about the temple, acknowledging God cannot be contained?
teens.option_a: The heaven and heaven of heavens cannot contain him, so who is he to build him a house
teens.option_b: The temple would be the greatest wonder of all nations
teens.option_c: No house could be worthy, so only the best cedar must be used
teens.option_d: The temple must stand forever because God lives there
teens.explanation: 2 Chronicles 2:6 — "But who is able to build him an house, seeing the heaven and heaven of heavens cannot contain him?"

teens.question: What was the name of the craftsman King Huram sent to Solomon?
teens.option_a: Huram-abi, skilled in all manner of craft
teens.option_b: Adoniram, master of bronze
teens.option_c: Bezaleel, the son of Uri
teens.option_d: Hiram, the chief architect
teens.explanation: 2 Chronicles 2:13 — "And now I have sent a cunning man, endued with understanding, of Huram my father's."

teens.question: What was Huram-abi's mother's tribal background according to the text?
teens.option_a: She was of the daughters of Dan
teens.option_b: She was of the tribe of Levi
teens.option_c: She was a woman of Tyre
teens.option_d: She was of the tribe of Judah
teens.explanation: 2 Chronicles 2:14 — "The son of a woman of the daughters of Dan, and his father was a man of Tyre."

teens.question: How many overseers did Solomon appoint to set the people to work?
teens.option_a: Three thousand and six hundred
teens.option_b: One thousand two hundred
teens.option_c: Five hundred and fifty
teens.option_d: Two thousand four hundred
teens.explanation: 2 Chronicles 2:18 — "And he set threescore and ten thousand of them to be bearers of burdens, and fourscore thousand to be hewers in the mountain, and three thousand and six hundred overseers to set the people a work."

adults.question: What theological reason did Solomon give Huram for why the temple must be great and wonderful?
adults.option_a: For great is our God above all gods, and no one can build him a house since the heavens cannot contain him, yet Solomon would burn incense before him
adults.option_b: Because Israel was the greatest nation and deserved the greatest temple
adults.option_c: To show all surrounding nations the power of the God of Israel
adults.option_d: Because David had already sworn an oath that the temple would surpass all others
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 2:5 — "And the house which I build is great: for great is our God above all gods."

adults.question: What four categories of material did Solomon request that Huram's craftsman be skilled to work in?
adults.option_a: Gold, silver, brass, and iron — plus purple, crimson, and blue materials, and to engrave
adults.option_b: Only gold and silver for the holy vessels
adults.option_c: Cedar, fir, and algum timbers only
adults.option_d: Stone, clay, mortar, and hewn rock
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 2:7 — "Send me now therefore a man cunning to work in gold, and in silver, and in brass, and in iron, and in purple, and crimson, and blue."

adults.question: What was the annual payment Solomon promised to Huram's servants who cut timber in Lebanon?
adults.option_a: Twenty thousand measures of beaten wheat, twenty thousand measures of barley, twenty thousand baths of wine, and twenty thousand baths of oil
adults.option_b: A talent of gold for every hundred logs
adults.option_c: Silver paid by weight each month
adults.option_d: A fixed tribute of spices and livestock
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 2:10 — "And, behold, I will give to thy servants, the hewers that cut timber, twenty thousand measures of beaten wheat, and twenty thousand measures of barley, and twenty thousand baths of wine, and twenty thousand baths of oil."

adults.question: How did Huram describe the God of Israel in his reply to Solomon, showing his recognition of Israel's God?
adults.option_a: Blessed be the LORD God of Israel, that made heaven and earth, who hath given to David a wise son endued with prudence and understanding
adults.option_b: The God of Israel is mighty in battle and none can stand against him
adults.option_c: The God of Israel has given Solomon wisdom greater than Pharaoh's
adults.option_d: Truly the LORD God is above the gods of Tyre
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 2:12 — "Huram said moreover, Blessed be the LORD God of Israel, that made heaven and earth, who hath given to David the king a wise son, endued with prudence and understanding."

adults.question: What specific crafts was Huram-abi said to be skilled in beyond metalwork?
adults.option_a: To find out every device which shall be put to him — skilled in all crafts including purple, blue, fine linen, and carving
adults.option_b: He was only a metalworker with no other skills
adults.option_c: He specialized in the cutting of precious stones
adults.option_d: He was an architect who designed buildings
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 2:14 — "Skilful to work in gold, and in silver, in brass, in iron, in stone, and in timber, in purple, in blue, and in fine linen, and in crimson; also to grave any manner of graving, and to find out every device."

adults.question: How many non-Israelite strangers did Solomon number as the labour force, and what had previously been done to count them?
adults.option_a: 153,600 strangers, whom David had numbered
adults.option_b: 100,000 strangers whom Solomon himself counted
adults.option_c: 200,000 who were counted by the tribal chiefs
adults.option_d: 70,000 counted by the Levites
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 2:17 — "And Solomon numbered all the strangers that were in the land of Israel, after the numbering wherewith David his father had numbered them; and they were found an hundred and fifty and three thousand and six hundred."

adults.question: What type of timber did Solomon specifically request from Lebanon beside cedar?
adults.option_a: Fir trees and algum trees
adults.option_b: Oak and acacia
adults.option_c: Cypress and pine
adults.option_d: Ebony and sandalwood
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 2:8 — "Send me also cedar trees, fir trees, and algum trees, out of Lebanon: for I know that thy servants can skill to cut timber in Lebanon."

adults.question: What was Solomon's stated purpose for the algum timber from Lebanon?
adults.option_a: To make stairs for the house of God and for the king's house, and harps and psalteries for singers
adults.option_b: To build ships for his navy
adults.option_c: To construct the doors of the temple sanctuary
adults.option_d: To fuel the altar fires perpetually
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 2:8 — "And the algum trees to make algum trees in abundance: for they were not common," used later per 2 Chronicles 9:11 for stairs and instruments.

kids.question: On what mountain did Solomon build the temple?
kids.explanation: 2 Chronicles 3:1 — "Then Solomon began to build the house of the LORD at Jerusalem in mount Moriah."

kids.question: What were the names of the two pillars Solomon built at the porch of the temple?
kids.explanation: 2 Chronicles 3:17 — "And he reared up the pillars before the temple, one on the right hand, and the other on the left; and called the name of that on the right hand Jachin, and the name of that on the left Boaz."

kids.question: What precious material did Solomon use to overlay the inside of the temple?
kids.explanation: 2 Chronicles 3:4 — "And the porch that was in the front of the house...he overlaid it within with pure gold."

teens.question: What was the historical significance of Mount Moriah mentioned in the text?
teens.option_a: It was where the LORD appeared to David his father and where David had prepared the threshingfloor of Ornan the Jebusite
teens.option_b: It was the mountain where Moses received the law
teens.option_c: It was the site of Joshua's first altar in Canaan
teens.option_d: It was where Abraham first built an altar to God
teens.explanation: 2 Chronicles 3:1 — "In the place that David had prepared in the threshingfloor of Ornan the Jebusite."

teens.question: What was the length and breadth of the porch of the temple?
teens.option_a: The length twenty cubits and the height one hundred and twenty cubits
teens.option_b: Sixty cubits long and twenty cubits wide
teens.option_c: Thirty cubits long and ten cubits high
teens.option_d: Forty cubits long and twenty cubits wide
teens.explanation: 2 Chronicles 3:4 — "And the porch that was in the front of the house, the length of it was according to the breadth of the house, twenty cubits, and the height was an hundred and twenty."

teens.question: What covered the walls and beams of the most holy house?
teens.option_a: Fine gold, and the gold was of Parvaim
teens.option_b: Pure silver overlaid on cedar
teens.option_c: Carved cherubim and palm trees in cedar
teens.option_d: Bronze plates and linen hangings
teens.explanation: 2 Chronicles 3:6 — "And he garnished the house with precious stones for beauty: and the gold was gold of Parvaim."

teens.question: What was the wingspan of each of the two cherubim Solomon made for the most holy house?
teens.option_a: Ten cubits each, so their wings together spanned twenty cubits
teens.option_b: Five cubits each
teens.option_c: Fifteen cubits each
teens.option_d: Eight cubits each
teens.explanation: 2 Chronicles 3:11 — "And the wings of the cherubims were twenty cubits long: one wing of the one cherub was five cubits...and the other wing was likewise five cubits."

teens.question: What material was used to make the veil that separated the most holy place?
teens.option_a: Blue, purple, crimson, and fine linen with cherubims worked on it
teens.option_b: Pure white linen only
teens.option_c: Gold thread woven into cedar planks
teens.option_d: Purple silk embroidered with the law
teens.explanation: 2 Chronicles 3:14 — "And he made the vail of blue, and purple, and crimson, and fine linen, and wrought cherubims thereon."

adults.question: On what day of which month in which year of Solomon's reign did construction begin?
adults.option_a: The second day of the second month of the fourth year of his reign
adults.option_b: The first day of the first month of the third year
adults.option_c: The seventh month of the seventh year
adults.option_d: The first day of the fourth year at the new moon
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 3:2 — "And he began to build in the second day of the second month, in the fourth year of his reign."

adults.question: What were the dimensions of the house of God according to the old measure?
adults.option_a: The length sixty cubits and the breadth twenty cubits
adults.option_b: One hundred cubits long and fifty cubits wide
adults.option_c: Forty cubits by twenty cubits
adults.option_d: Thirty cubits high and twenty cubits wide
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 3:3 — "Now these are the things wherein Solomon was instructed for the building of the house of God. The length by cubits after the first measure was threescore cubits, and the breadth twenty cubits."

adults.question: How much gold was used to overlay the upper chambers and the great house?
adults.option_a: Six hundred talents of fine gold for the upper chambers and twenty talents for each story
adults.option_b: A thousand talents in the most holy place alone
adults.option_c: Three hundred talents for the outer house
adults.option_d: All the gold of David's treasury without a stated weight
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 3:8 — "And he made the most holy house...and he overlaid it with fine gold, amounting to six hundred talents."

adults.question: What was the height of the cherubims standing in the most holy place?
adults.option_a: Each cherub was ten cubits high
adults.option_b: Each cherub was five cubits high
adults.option_c: Each cherub was fifteen cubits high
adults.option_d: Each cherub was twenty cubits high
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 3:13 — "The wings of these cherubims spread themselves forth twenty cubits: and they stood on their feet, and their faces were inward."

adults.question: How were the cherubim oriented in the most holy house?
adults.option_a: Their faces turned inward toward the house, wings stretching to the walls on each side and touching in the middle
adults.option_b: They faced the entrance of the temple
adults.option_c: They faced outward toward Jerusalem
adults.option_d: They faced each other with wings folded
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 3:13 — "And they stood on their feet, and their faces were inward," with wings spanning the full twenty cubits of the room.

adults.question: How high were the pillars Jachin and Boaz, and what was on top of them?
adults.option_a: Thirty-five cubits high with chapiters of five cubits on top
adults.option_b: Twenty cubits high with carved pomegranates
adults.option_c: Forty cubits high with bronze bowls on top
adults.option_d: Eighteen cubits high with lily-work chapiters
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 3:15 — "Also he made before the house two pillars of thirty and five cubits high, and the chapiter that was on the top of each of them was five cubits."

adults.question: What decorative element adorned the chains placed on the pillars?
adults.option_a: An hundred pomegranates
adults.option_b: Golden lilies
adults.option_c: Bronze rings and hooks
adults.option_d: Carved palm trees
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 3:16 — "And he made chains, as in the oracle, and put them on the heads of the pillars; and made an hundred pomegranates, and put them on the chains."

adults.question: What is the theological significance of building the temple on Mount Moriah, the site where Abraham offered Isaac?
adults.option_a: It connected the temple to the covenant of sacrifice and redemption established with Abraham, showing continuity of God's covenant purposes
adults.option_b: It was simply the highest available ground in Jerusalem
adults.option_c: It was chosen because Ornan paid the highest price for his threshingfloor
adults.option_d: It was selected by lot among the Levites
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 3:1 — "Then Solomon began to build the house of the LORD at Jerusalem in mount Moriah, where the LORD appeared unto David his father," linking temple worship to divine encounter and Abrahamic covenant.

kids.question: What large basin did Solomon make for the priests to wash in?
kids.explanation: 2 Chronicles 4:2 — "Also he made a molten sea of ten cubits from brim to brim, round in compass."

kids.question: How many candlesticks did Solomon make for the temple?
kids.explanation: 2 Chronicles 4:7 — "And he made ten candlesticks of gold according to their form, and set them in the temple, five on the right hand, and five on the left."

kids.question: How many tables did Solomon put in the temple?
kids.explanation: 2 Chronicles 4:8 — "He made also ten tables, and placed them in the temple, five on the right side, and five on the left."

teens.question: What were the dimensions of the brasen altar Solomon made?
teens.option_a: Twenty cubits long, twenty cubits broad, and ten cubits high
teens.option_b: Ten cubits long, ten cubits broad, and five cubits high
teens.option_c: Thirty cubits long and fifteen cubits wide
teens.option_d: Fifteen cubits in each direction
teens.explanation: 2 Chronicles 4:1 — "Moreover he made an altar of brass, twenty cubits the length thereof, and twenty cubits the breadth thereof, and ten cubits the height thereof."

teens.question: What figures were under the molten sea, supporting it?
teens.option_a: Twelve oxen, three looking toward each of the four points of the compass
teens.option_b: Four bronze lions on a pedestal
teens.option_c: Twenty-four cherubim in two rows
teens.option_d: Twelve carved palm trees
teens.explanation: 2 Chronicles 4:4 — "It stood upon twelve oxen, three looking toward the north, and three looking toward the west, and three looking toward the south, and three looking toward the east."

teens.question: How many lavers did Solomon make, and what were they used for?
teens.option_a: Ten lavers to wash in, for rinsing the burnt offering
teens.option_b: Seven lavers for the priests' daily washing
teens.option_c: Twelve lavers for the twelve tribes
teens.option_d: Four lavers for the four cardinal points
teens.explanation: 2 Chronicles 4:6 — "He made also ten lavers, and put five on the right hand, and five on the left, to wash in them: such things as they offered for the burnt offering they washed in them."

teens.question: What was the circumference and depth of the molten sea, and how many baths did it hold?
teens.option_a: Thirty cubits around, five cubits deep, holding three thousand baths
teens.option_b: Twenty cubits around, three cubits deep, one thousand baths
teens.option_c: Forty cubits around, eight cubits deep, five thousand baths
teens.option_d: Fifteen cubits around, four cubits deep, two thousand baths
teens.explanation: 2 Chronicles 4:5 — "And the thickness of it was an handbreadth, and the brim of it like the work of the brim of a cup...and it received and held three thousand baths."

teens.question: What was made of fine gold for placing inside the most holy house?
teens.option_a: An hundred basons of gold
teens.option_b: The altar of incense and the table of shewbread
teens.option_c: The seven-branched menorah
teens.option_d: The ark overlaid with pure gold
teens.explanation: 2 Chronicles 4:8 — "He made also an hundred basons of gold," along with the golden altar and tables.

adults.question: What metal was used to cast all the vessels Huram made, and in what region were they cast?
adults.option_a: Brass, cast in the plain of Jordan between Succoth and Zeredathah in clay ground
adults.option_b: Refined silver at Jerusalem in the royal workshops
adults.option_c: Bronze in the furnaces of Tyre
adults.option_d: Gold mixed with bronze at Gibeon
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 4:17 — "In the plain of Jordan did the king cast them, in the clay ground between Succoth and Zeredathah."

adults.question: What decorative motif ran below the brim of the molten sea?
adults.option_a: Flowers, resembling the work of a lily blossom
adults.option_b: Cherubim and palm trees alternating
adults.option_c: Pomegranates in two rows
adults.option_d: Oxen heads in a continuous band
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 4:5 — "And the brim of it like the work of the brim of a cup, with flowers of lilies; and it received and held three thousand baths."

adults.question: What was the distinction between the use of the molten sea and the lavers?
adults.option_a: The sea was for the priests to wash in while the lavers were for washing what was used in the burnt offering
adults.option_b: The sea was for ritually purifying new vessels and the lavers for daily priestly washing
adults.option_c: The sea held water for drinking and lavers for ceremonial sprinkling
adults.option_d: The sea served the inner court and lavers served the outer court only
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 4:6 — "The sea was for the priests to wash in...and such things as they offered for the burnt offering they washed in them."

adults.question: What summary statement describes the quantity of brass used in all these furnishings?
adults.option_a: The weight of the brass could not be found out, it was so great
adults.option_b: Three hundred talents of brass in total
adults.option_c: More brass than any king had ever used
adults.option_d: The brass was weighed and recorded by the Levites
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 4:18 — "Thus Solomon made all these vessels in great abundance: for the weight of the brass could not be found out."

adults.question: What were the four categories of golden items Solomon made for inside the temple?
adults.option_a: The golden altar, the tables with the shewbread, the candlesticks with their lamps, and the flowers, lamps, and tongs of pure gold
adults.option_b: Ark, altar, table, and menorah
adults.option_c: Basons, cups, snuffers, and bowls
adults.option_d: Doors, veil hooks, cherubim, and pillars
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 4:19 — "And Solomon made all the vessels that were for the house of God, the golden altar also, and the tables whereon the shewbread was set."

adults.question: What courts did Solomon make for the temple complex?
adults.option_a: The court of the priests and the great court, with doors overlaid with brass
adults.option_b: The inner court and the outer court of the Gentiles
adults.option_c: The court of women and the court of Israel
adults.option_d: The upper court and the lower court by the gate
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 4:9 — "Furthermore he made the court of the priests, and the great court, and doors for the court, and overlaid the doors of them with brass."

adults.question: In addition to the major furnishings, what smaller golden items did Huram complete for the temple?
adults.option_a: The pots, shovels, and flesh-hooks, and all their instruments
adults.option_b: The rings on the staves of the ark
adults.option_c: The curtain hooks and wall fittings
adults.option_d: The incense pans and oil containers
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 4:16 — "The pots also, and the shovels, and the fleshhooks, and all their instruments, did Huram his father make to king Solomon for the house of the LORD of bright brass."

adults.question: What does the phrase "Solomon made all these vessels in great abundance" (4:18) communicate about the temple economy?
adults.option_a: It shows that God's house was furnished with extravagant generosity, echoing the principle that nothing should be withheld from the LORD
adults.option_b: It was a display of political power to impress foreign kings
adults.option_c: It was necessary to replace Mosaic tabernacle vessels which had worn out
adults.option_d: It reflected the surplus of Solomon's treasury that needed to be spent
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 4:18 — "Thus Solomon made all these vessels in great abundance: for the weight of the brass could not be found out," signifying lavish devotion to God's worship.

kids.question: What was brought into the temple when it was finished?
kids.option_a: The ark of the covenant of the LORD
kids.explanation: 2 Chronicles 5:2 — "Then Solomon assembled the elders of Israel...to bring up the ark of the covenant of the LORD out of the city of David."

kids.question: What happened when the trumpeters and singers praised God at the dedication?
kids.option_a: A cloud filled the house of the LORD
kids.option_c: A great earthquake shook the temple
kids.option_d: All the people fell on their faces
kids.explanation: 2 Chronicles 5:13-14 — "Then the house was filled with a cloud, even the house of the LORD."

kids.question: What were inside the ark when it was placed in the most holy place?
kids.option_a: The two tables of stone which Moses put therein at Horeb
kids.option_b: The pot of manna and Aaron's rod
kids.explanation: 2 Chronicles 5:10 — "There was nothing in the ark save the two tables which Moses put therein at Horeb."

teens.question: At what feast was the ark brought into the temple?
teens.option_a: The feast of the seventh month
teens.option_b: The feast of Passover
teens.option_c: The feast of Pentecost
teens.option_d: The feast of Tabernacles
teens.explanation: 2 Chronicles 5:3 — "Wherefore all the men of Israel assembled themselves unto the king in the feast which was in the seventh month."

teens.question: Who carried the ark into the temple?
teens.option_a: The priests
teens.option_b: The Levites with staves
teens.option_c: Solomon and the elders together
teens.option_d: The sons of Asaph
teens.explanation: 2 Chronicles 5:7 — "And the priests brought in the ark of the covenant of the LORD unto his place, to the oracle of the house, into the most holy place."

teens.question: How many sheep and oxen were sacrificed when the ark was brought up, so great that they could not be counted?
teens.option_a: Sheep and oxen that could not be told nor numbered for multitude
teens.option_b: One thousand oxen and ten thousand sheep
teens.option_c: Seven bulls and seven rams
teens.option_d: Twenty-two thousand cattle and one hundred and twenty thousand sheep
teens.explanation: 2 Chronicles 5:6 — "Also king Solomon, and all the congregation of Israel that were assembled unto him before the ark, sacrificed sheep and oxen, which could not be told nor numbered for multitude."

teens.question: What song did the singers and musicians sing when the cloud filled the temple?
teens.option_a: For he is good; for his mercy endureth for ever
teens.option_b: Blessed is the name of the LORD forever
teens.option_c: The LORD reigneth, let the earth rejoice
teens.option_d: How amiable are thy tabernacles, O LORD of hosts
teens.explanation: 2 Chronicles 5:13 — "They lifted up their voice...and praised the LORD, saying, For he is good; for his mercy endureth for ever."

teens.question: Why could the priests not stand to minister after the ark was brought in?
teens.option_a: The glory of the LORD had filled the house of God
teens.option_b: They were overcome with the weight of the offering
teens.option_c: The king commanded them to stand aside
teens.option_d: The smoke of the incense blinded their eyes
teens.explanation: 2 Chronicles 5:14 — "So that the priests could not stand to minister by reason of the cloud: for the glory of the LORD had filled the house of God."

adults.question: What were the staves of the ark and where could they be seen when the ark rested in the most holy place?
adults.option_a: The staves were so long that the ends of them were seen from the holy place before the oracle, but not outward, and they are there unto this day
adults.option_b: The staves were removed once the ark was in place
adults.option_c: The staves extended outside the temple veil
adults.option_d: The staves were kept separately in the priests' chambers
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 5:9 — "And the staves were so long, that the ends of the staves were seen from the ark before the oracle; but they were not seen without. And there it is unto this day."

adults.question: What was the exact assembly and who were brought together for the dedication of the ark?
adults.option_a: Solomon assembled all the elders of Israel, all the heads of the tribes, and the chief of the fathers of the children of Israel unto Jerusalem
adults.option_b: Only the priests and Levites were summoned
adults.option_c: All fighting men from twenty years old and upward
adults.option_d: The tribal representatives and their households
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 5:2 — "Then Solomon assembled the elders of Israel, and all the heads of the tribes, the chief of the fathers of the children of Israel, unto Jerusalem."

adults.question: What was the arrangement of the Levitical musicians who sang and played at the dedication?
adults.option_a: All the Levitical singers — Asaph, Heman, Jeduthun and their sons and brethren — clothed in white linen with cymbals, psalteries, and harps, with 120 priests sounding trumpets
adults.option_b: Only the sons of Asaph played while the priests stood silent
adults.option_c: Seven priests with seven trumpets led the procession
adults.option_d: The singers stood in the inner court while priests played outside
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 5:12 — "Also the Levites which were the singers...clothed in white linen, having cymbals and psalteries and harps, stood at the east end of the altar, and with them an hundred and twenty priests sounding with trumpets."

adults.question: What theological statement is made by the cloud filling the temple after the music of praise?
adults.option_a: The divine glory manifestly took up residence in the house, confirming that God accepted the worship and the dwelling place
adults.option_b: It was a natural weather phenomenon at that season of the year
adults.option_c: It was a warning to the priests to be more careful in their service
adults.option_d: It symbolised the hidden mystery of God that cannot be revealed
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 5:13-14 — "The house was filled with a cloud, even the house of the LORD; So that the priests could not stand to minister by reason of the cloud: for the glory of the LORD had filled the house of God."

adults.question: The absence of any contents in the ark except the two tablets echoes what about Israel's covenant identity?
adults.option_a: That the covenant law of God, not ritual objects, was the heart of Israel's relationship with the LORD
adults.option_b: That the manna and rod had decayed over time and were removed
adults.option_c: That the tablets were the only items that survived the wilderness
adults.option_d: That the Levites had stored the other items elsewhere for safekeeping
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 5:10 — "There was nothing in the ark save the two tables which Moses put therein at Horeb, when the LORD made a covenant with the children of Israel."

adults.question: What does the bringing of the tabernacle along with the ark indicate about this dedication event?
adults.option_a: All the holy vessels of the tabernacle were also gathered and deposited, signifying the transfer of all Mosaic worship heritage into the permanent temple
adults.option_b: The tabernacle was destroyed once the ark was removed
adults.option_c: The tabernacle was kept in a separate shrine outside Jerusalem
adults.option_d: The tabernacle was sent to Gibeon to remain there permanently
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 5:5 — "And they brought up the ark, and the tabernacle of the congregation, and all the holy vessels that were in the tabernacle, these did the priests and the Levites bring up."

adults.question: What position in the temple did the ark occupy, and what was placed above it?
adults.option_a: It was set under the wings of the cherubims in the most holy place, with the cherubims covering the ark and its staves
adults.option_b: It stood in the centre of the holy place facing the altar
adults.option_c: It was placed directly before the golden altar of incense
adults.option_d: It was set upon the rock of Moriah at the base of the altar
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 5:7-8 — "The priests brought in the ark...to the oracle...And the cherubims spread forth their wings over the place of the ark, and the cherubims covered the ark and the staves thereof above."

adults.question: What is the typological significance of the cloud of glory filling the temple in relation to the Mosaic tabernacle?
adults.option_a: It exactly repeats the pattern of Exodus 40 where the cloud filled the tabernacle after its completion, showing God's faithful continuity
adults.option_b: It was a unique miracle never seen before or since
adults.option_c: It replaced the fire that had appeared at Sinai
adults.option_d: It was prophesied by David in the Psalms
adults.explanation: Just as in Exodus 40:34 — "Then a cloud covered the tent of the congregation," so in 2 Chronicles 5:13-14 the cloud of the LORD's glory filled the completed temple, confirming divine acceptance.

kids.question: What did Solomon say the LORD chose to dwell in?
kids.explanation: 2 Chronicles 6:1 — "Then said Solomon, The LORD hath said that he would dwell in the thick darkness."

kids.question: What did Solomon do before all the people when he prayed?
kids.option_a: He kneeled down upon his knees and spread forth his hands toward heaven
kids.option_b: He stood on a high mountain
kids.option_c: He lay face down on the ground
kids.explanation: 2 Chronicles 6:13 — "And kneeled down upon his knees before all the congregation of Israel, and spread forth his hands toward heaven."

kids.question: Solomon said the heaven of heavens cannot contain God. What could the temple do even less?
kids.option_a: How much less this house which I have built
kids.option_b: Not even the whole earth could contain God
kids.option_c: The ark itself could not hold him
kids.option_d: No building of stone or wood could contain him
kids.explanation: 2 Chronicles 6:18 — "But will God in very deed dwell with men on the earth? behold, heaven and the heaven of heavens cannot contain thee; how much less this house which I have built!"

teens.question: What was the scaffold that Solomon made, and what were its dimensions?
teens.option_a: A brasen scaffold five cubits long, five broad, and three cubits high, set in the court
teens.option_b: A wooden platform of ten cubits on every side
teens.option_c: A stone pulpit at the entrance to the temple
teens.option_d: A raised altar of brass in the inner court
teens.explanation: 2 Chronicles 6:13 — "For Solomon had made a brasen scaffold, of five cubits long, and five cubits broad, and three cubits high, and had set it in the midst of the court."

teens.question: What had God told David about who would build the temple?
teens.option_a: Thy son which shall come forth out of thy loins, he shall build the house for my name
teens.option_b: David himself would build it if he was worthy
teens.option_c: The whole nation together would build it
teens.option_d: No man but God himself would build his house
teens.explanation: 2 Chronicles 6:9 — "Notwithstanding thou shalt not build the house; but thy son which shall come forth out of thy loins, he shall build the house for my name."

teens.question: In his prayer, what did Solomon ask God to do when Israel was defeated in battle because of sin and then returned to God?
teens.option_a: Hear from heaven and forgive the sin of thy people Israel and bring them again unto the land
teens.option_b: Give them a sign to show they were forgiven
teens.option_c: Send an angel to fight for them
teens.option_d: Restore their crops and remove the famine
teens.explanation: 2 Chronicles 6:24-25 — "Then hear thou from the heavens, and forgive the sin of thy people Israel, and bring them again unto the land which thou gavest to them."

teens.question: What does Solomon ask God to do regarding the foreigner who is not of Israel when he prays toward the temple?
teens.option_a: Hear from heaven and do according to all that the stranger calleth to thee for, that all people may know thy name
teens.option_b: Send the stranger away unless he converts to Israel
teens.option_c: Answer his prayer only if a priest intercedes for him
teens.option_d: Grant him the same blessing as the children of Israel
teens.explanation: 2 Chronicles 6:33 — "Hear thou from the heavens, even from thy dwelling place, and do according to all that the stranger calleth to thee for; that all people of the earth may know thy name."

teens.question: What petition did Solomon make about Israel's armies when they go to battle and pray toward the city and temple?
teens.option_a: Hear from heaven and maintain their cause
teens.option_b: Give them victory over every enemy
teens.option_c: Send angels to fight before them
teens.option_d: Pour out thy wrath on their enemies
teens.explanation: 2 Chronicles 6:34-35 — "If thy people go out to war against their enemies by the way that thou shalt send them, and they pray unto thee...then hear thou from the heavens their prayer and their supplication, and maintain their cause."

adults.question: What was the foundation of Solomon's prayer — what covenant did he invoke at the opening of his supplication?
adults.option_a: The covenant with David that there would never fail a man to sit before God upon the throne of Israel, if his children take heed to walk in God's law
adults.option_b: The Mosaic covenant at Sinai given to all Israel
adults.option_c: The covenant with Abraham that his seed would inherit the land
adults.option_d: The covenant of peace made with Phinehas
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 6:16 — "Now therefore, O LORD God of Israel, keep with thy servant David my father that which thou hast promised him."

adults.question: What was the precise condition stated in the Davidic covenant that Solomon recited to God?
adults.option_a: There shall not fail thee a man in my sight to sit upon the throne of Israel — yet so that thy children take heed to their way to walk in my law as thou hast walked before me
adults.option_b: The throne would stand forever with no conditions attached
adults.option_c: The covenant required only that sacrifices never cease
adults.option_d: God promised to keep the covenant regardless of their behavior
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 6:16 — "Yet so that thy children take heed to their way to walk in my law, as thou hast walked before me."

adults.question: How many distinct petitions or situations does Solomon address in his prayer, showing its comprehensive scope?
adults.option_a: Seven petitions covering oath-taking, defeat in battle, drought, famine or plague, the foreigner, battle, and exile
adults.option_b: Three petitions for sin, battle, and foreign nations
adults.option_c: Ten petitions covering all areas of the law
adults.option_d: Five petitions for the five types of sacrifice
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 6:22-39 — Solomon addresses seven situations: oath disputes (v.22), military defeat (v.24), drought (v.26), famine or pestilence (v.28), the foreigner (v.32), battle (v.34), and captivity (v.36).

adults.question: What universal theological statement does Solomon make about human sinfulness in his prayer?
adults.option_a: There is no man which sinneth not
adults.option_b: All have sinned and fallen short of God's glory
adults.option_c: Man's heart is desperately wicked above all things
adults.option_d: The imagination of man's heart is evil from his youth
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 6:36 — "For there is no man which sinneth not," acknowledging universal human sinfulness as the premise for the need of divine forgiveness.

adults.question: What specific act of forgiveness did Solomon request for the captives who returned to God in the land of their enemies?
adults.option_a: Forgive thy people, have compassion upon them who carried them captive, and maintain their cause
adults.option_b: Destroy the nations that took them captive
adults.option_c: Send a deliverer to bring them home immediately
adults.option_d: Restore their property and honour among the nations
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 6:39 — "Then hear thou from the heavens, even from thy dwelling place, their prayer and their supplications, and maintain their cause, and forgive thy people which have sinned against thee."

adults.question: What does Solomon's statement "heaven and the heaven of heavens cannot contain thee" (6:18) reveal about Solomon's theology?
adults.option_a: He held a transcendent view of God — God cannot be confined to any place — making the temple a place for prayer toward God rather than God's geographic prison
adults.option_b: He was questioning whether the temple project was worthwhile
adults.option_c: He was responding to pagan critics who said Israel's God lived in a box
adults.option_d: He was citing a Mosaic teaching about God's omnipresence
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 6:18 — "But will God in very deed dwell with men on the earth? behold, heaven and the heaven of heavens cannot contain thee; how much less this house which I have built!"

adults.question: What was the posture and platform arrangement during Solomon's prayer, and what does it signify?
adults.option_a: He stood upon a brasen scaffold in the court, then kneeled with hands spread toward heaven before all the congregation
adults.option_b: He prayed privately inside the most holy place
adults.option_c: He stood at the altar of burnt offering with the priests around him
adults.option_d: He prayed from his throne in the palace overlooking the temple
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 6:12-13 — "And he stood before the altar of the LORD in the presence of all the congregation of Israel, and spread forth his hands...kneeled down upon his knees before all the congregation of Israel."

adults.question: What does Solomon's petition for the foreigner (6:32-33) reveal about the intended scope of the temple's ministry?
adults.option_a: The temple was intended as a house of prayer for all peoples of the earth, not just Israel, "that all people of the earth may know thy name"
adults.option_b: Foreigners were welcome only if they converted to Judaism first
adults.option_c: The petition was a diplomatic courtesy with no theological significance
adults.option_d: Solomon was concerned only with trade partners from Tyre and Egypt
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 6:33 — "That all people of the earth may know thy name, and fear thee, as doth thy people Israel, and may know that this house which I have built is called by thy name."

kids.question: What came down from heaven when Solomon finished praying?
kids.explanation: 2 Chronicles 7:1 — "Now when Solomon had made an end of praying, the fire came down from heaven, and consumed the burnt offering and the sacrifices."

kids.question: What did all the people do when they saw the fire come down from heaven?
kids.option_a: They bowed themselves with their faces to the ground upon the pavement and worshipped
kids.option_c: They shouted and blew their trumpets
kids.explanation: 2 Chronicles 7:3 — "And when all the children of Israel saw how the fire came down...they bowed themselves with their faces to the ground upon the pavement, and worshipped."

kids.question: What is the most famous verse from this chapter about what God's people should do when things go wrong?
kids.option_a: If my people, which are called by my name, shall humble themselves, and pray, and seek my face, and turn from their wicked ways, then will I hear from heaven
kids.option_b: Seek the LORD while he may be found
kids.option_c: Repent and do the first works
kids.option_d: Draw near to God and he will draw near to you
kids.explanation: 2 Chronicles 7:14 — "If my people, which are called by my name, shall humble themselves, and pray, and seek my face, and turn from their wicked ways; then will I hear from heaven, and will forgive their sin, and will heal their land."

teens.question: How many animals were sacrificed at the dedication feast that Solomon and all Israel kept?
teens.option_a: Twenty and two thousand oxen, and an hundred and twenty thousand sheep
teens.option_b: A thousand oxen and ten thousand sheep
teens.option_c: Seven bulls and seven rams per day for seven days
teens.option_d: Fifty thousand oxen and one hundred thousand sheep
teens.explanation: 2 Chronicles 7:5 — "And king Solomon offered a sacrifice of twenty and two thousand oxen, and an hundred and twenty thousand sheep: so the king and all the people dedicated the house of God."

teens.question: For how many days was the dedication feast celebrated in total?
teens.option_a: Fourteen days and then on the twenty-third day he sent the people away
teens.option_b: Seven days only
teens.option_c: Twenty-one days
teens.option_d: Thirty days
teens.explanation: 2 Chronicles 7:9 — "And in the eighth day they made a solemn assembly: for they kept the dedication of the altar seven days, and the feast seven days. And on the three and twentieth day of the seventh month he sent the people away."

teens.question: When did God appear to Solomon the second time in this chapter?
teens.option_a: At night
teens.option_b: At noon when the sun was highest
teens.option_c: During the morning sacrifice
teens.option_d: On the last day of the feast
teens.explanation: 2 Chronicles 7:12 — "And the LORD appeared to Solomon by night, and said unto him, I have heard thy prayer, and have chosen this place to myself for an house of sacrifice."

teens.question: What three things does God promise to do if his people humble themselves, pray, seek his face, and turn from wicked ways?
teens.option_a: Hear from heaven, forgive their sin, and heal their land
teens.option_b: Give them rain, peace, and victory over enemies
teens.option_c: Send his spirit, restore their crops, and bless their children
teens.option_d: Open the windows of heaven, rebuke the devourer, and enlarge their borders
teens.explanation: 2 Chronicles 7:14 — "Then will I hear from heaven, and will forgive their sin, and will heal their land."

teens.question: What warning did God give Solomon about what would happen if Israel turned away and served other gods?
teens.option_a: He would pluck them up by the roots from the land, cast off the house, and make it a proverb and a byword among all nations
teens.option_b: He would send foreign armies immediately
teens.option_c: He would stop the rain for three years
teens.option_d: He would remove the king from the throne at once
teens.explanation: 2 Chronicles 7:20 — "Then will I pluck them up by the roots out of my land which I have given them; and this house, which I have sanctified for my name, will I cast out of my sight, and will make it to be a proverb and a byword among all nations."

adults.question: What did the glory of the LORD do after the fire consumed the offering, and what was the implication for the priests?
adults.option_a: The glory of the LORD filled the house so that the priests could not enter into the house of the LORD
adults.option_b: The glory departed after confirming the sacrifice
adults.option_c: The glory appeared as a pillar of fire above the altar
adults.option_d: The glory dwelt in the ark and the priests carried it in procession
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 7:2 — "And the priests could not enter into the house of the LORD, because the glory of the LORD had filled the LORD's house."

adults.question: What role did the Levitical musicians play during the dedication, and what instruments were specified?
adults.option_a: All the Levites with instruments of music praised the LORD, and the priests sounded trumpets opposite them while all Israel stood
adults.option_b: Only the sons of Asaph played with harps and lyres
adults.option_c: The priests used only the silver trumpets Moses had made
adults.option_d: The people sang without instruments
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 7:6 — "And the priests waited on their offices: the Levites also with instruments of musick of the LORD, which David the king had made to praise the LORD...and the priests sounded trumpets before them."

adults.question: What caused the brasen altar to be insufficient for the dedication sacrifices, and how was the problem solved?
adults.option_a: The altar was too small for all the burnt offerings, so Solomon hallowed the middle of the court before the house and offered burnt offerings there
adults.option_b: The fire on the altar went out and could not be relit
adults.option_c: The altar broke under the weight of the offerings
adults.option_d: The priests were too few to service the altar alone
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 7:7 — "Moreover Solomon hallowed the middle of the court that was before the house of the LORD: for there he offered burnt offerings, and the fat of the peace offerings, because the brasen altar which Solomon had made was not able to receive the burnt offerings."

adults.question: What was the full extent of the territory from which the great congregation came for the dedication feast?
adults.option_a: From the entering in of Hamath unto the river of Egypt
adults.option_b: From Dan to Beersheba
adults.option_c: From Egypt to the Euphrates river
adults.option_d: From the wilderness of Sinai to the sea of the Philistines
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 7:8 — "Also at the same time Solomon kept the feast seven days, and all Israel with him, a very great congregation, from the entering in of Hamath unto the river of Egypt."

adults.question: In God's second appearance to Solomon, what did God say he had chosen and sanctified?
adults.option_a: This house that Solomon had built, for God's name to be there forever, and his eyes and his heart would be there perpetually
adults.option_b: Jerusalem as the eternal capital of the kingdom
adults.option_c: The Davidic line to be kings forever
adults.option_d: The altar as the place where prayer would be heard
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 7:16 — "For now have I chosen and sanctified this house, that my name may be there for ever: and mine eyes and mine heart shall be there perpetually."

adults.question: What condition does God set for Solomon's personal dynasty as distinct from the national covenant of 7:14?
adults.option_a: If Solomon walks before God as David walked, doing all that God commanded, and keeping statutes and judgments, then God will establish the throne of his kingdom as covenanted with David
adults.option_b: The dynasty would continue automatically regardless of obedience
adults.option_c: Only the building of the temple determined the dynasty's future
adults.option_d: The covenant would be renewed with each successive king
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 7:17-18 — "If thou wilt walk before me...then will I establish the throne of thy kingdom, according as I have covenanted with David thy father."

adults.question: What would the nations say about the destroyed temple, and what would be the divine answer that gives the reason?
adults.option_a: Why hath the LORD done thus unto this land, and unto this house? And it shall be answered: Because they forsook the LORD God of their fathers
adults.option_b: That the temple was weaker than the gods of the nations
adults.option_c: That Israel had no God to protect them
adults.option_d: That Solomon's wisdom failed in the end
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 7:21-22 — "And this house, which is high, shall be an astonishment to every one that passeth by it...And it shall be answered, Because they forsook the LORD God of their fathers."

adults.question: What is the theological structure of 2 Chronicles 7:14 as a covenantal formula, identifying each element?
adults.option_a: Condition (humble, pray, seek, turn) → Promise (hear, forgive, heal) — a classic covenant if-then structure requiring human response for divine action
adults.option_b: An unconditional promise of healing regardless of behavior
adults.option_c: A priestly formula for the Day of Atonement ritual
adults.option_d: A prophecy about the end times only, not for Solomon's generation
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 7:14 — "If my people...shall humble themselves, and pray, and seek my face, and turn from their wicked ways; THEN will I hear from heaven, and will forgive their sin, and will heal their land," showing the conditional nature of covenant blessing.

kids.question: How long did it take Solomon to build his two great projects?
kids.explanation: 2 Chronicles 8:1 — "And it came to pass at the end of twenty years, wherein Solomon had built the house of the LORD, and his own house."

kids.question: What did Solomon do to the cities that Huram had given him?
kids.option_a: He built them, and caused the children of Israel to dwell there
kids.option_c: He gave them to the Levites
kids.option_d: He sold them back to Huram
kids.explanation: 2 Chronicles 8:2 — "That the cities which Huram had restored to Solomon, Solomon built them, and caused the children of Israel to dwell there."

kids.question: Where did Solomon build a fleet of ships?
kids.option_a: Ezion-geber, beside Eloth at the Red Sea
kids.explanation: 2 Chronicles 8:17 — "Then went Solomon to Ezion-geber, and to Eloth, at the sea side in the land of Edom."

teens.question: What did Solomon do with the non-Israelite peoples who remained in the land?
teens.option_a: He made them pay tribute and serve as bond labourers, but did not make Israelites servants for the work
teens.option_b: He drove them all out of the land
teens.option_c: He incorporated them into the twelve tribes
teens.option_d: He put them in charge of the temple service
teens.explanation: 2 Chronicles 8:8-9 — "But of their children, who were left after them in the land, whom the children of Israel consumed not, them did Solomon make to pay tribute...But of the children of Israel did Solomon make no servants for his work."

teens.question: Why did Pharaoh's daughter move from the city of David to her own house that Solomon built for her?
teens.option_a: Because Solomon said no wife shall dwell in the house of David king of Israel, because the places the ark had come to are holy
teens.option_b: Because she preferred a larger residence
teens.option_c: Because the city of David was too crowded
teens.option_d: Because David's palace was given to the priests
teens.explanation: 2 Chronicles 8:11 — "My wife shall not dwell in the house of David king of Israel, because the places are holy, whereunto the ark of the LORD hath come."

teens.question: What did Solomon build at Baalath and Tadmor in connection with his defense strategy?
teens.option_a: Storage cities, chariot cities, and cities for his horsemen throughout his territory
teens.option_b: Watchtowers on the borders of Israel
teens.option_c: Temples to secure the loyalty of border peoples
teens.option_d: Treasuries to store tribute from surrounding nations
teens.explanation: 2 Chronicles 8:4-6 — "And he built Tadmor in the wilderness, and all the store cities, which he built in Hamath. Also he built Beth-horon...And Baalath, and all the store cities that Solomon had."

teens.question: How did Solomon organize the priests and Levites according to David's commandment?
teens.option_a: The priests in their courses and the Levites in their courses for every duty of service, and the porters by their courses at every gate
teens.option_b: He appointed new priests to replace those who had supported Adonijah
teens.option_c: He organized them by tribe rather than by course
teens.option_d: He put the eldest Levites in charge of each department
teens.explanation: 2 Chronicles 8:14 — "And he appointed, according to the order of David his father, the courses of the priests to their service, and the Levites to their charges...for so had David the man of God commanded."

teens.question: What ships did Huram's servants provide for Solomon's fleet, and what expertise did they contribute?
teens.option_a: Huram's servants were expert shipmen of the sea who sailed with Solomon's servants to Ophir for gold
teens.option_b: Egyptian sailors who knew the Red Sea routes
teens.option_c: Phoenician craftsmen who built the ships at Ezion-geber
teens.option_d: Levitical guides who led the navigation by scripture
teens.explanation: 2 Chronicles 8:18 — "And Huram sent him by the hands of his servants ships, and servants that had knowledge of the sea; and they went with the servants of Solomon to Ophir, and took thence four hundred and fifty talents of gold, and brought them to king Solomon."

adults.question: What was the amount of gold brought back from Ophir by Solomon's fleet?
adults.option_a: Four hundred and fifty talents of gold
adults.option_b: One hundred and twenty talents
adults.option_c: Six hundred and sixty-six talents
adults.option_d: Three hundred talents per voyage
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 8:18 — "And they went with the servants of Solomon to Ophir, and took thence four hundred and fifty talents of gold, and brought them to king Solomon."

adults.question: What does Solomon's statement about Pharaoh's daughter reveal about his theology of holiness and sacred space?
adults.option_a: He recognized that the holiness attached to the ark and David's house meant a non-Israelite royal wife should not dwell in a consecrated space
adults.option_b: He was simply following Egyptian court custom about housing royal wives
adults.option_c: He was ashamed of the marriage and wanted to hide her
adults.option_d: He feared Pharaoh's daughter would defile the temple worship directly
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 8:11 — "My wife shall not dwell in the house of David king of Israel, because the places are holy, whereunto the ark of the LORD hath come."

adults.question: What range of territory did Solomon's administrative control cover in this chapter?
adults.option_a: From Hamath-zobah in the north to Ezion-geber at the Red Sea in the south, including Lebanon and the wilderness
adults.option_b: Only the twelve tribes' original territories
adults.option_c: From Dan to Beersheba without expansion
adults.option_d: From the Euphrates to the borders of Egypt
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 8:3-4 — "And Solomon went to Hamath-zobah...And he built Tadmor in the wilderness," showing the full extent of his empire.

adults.question: What was the theological basis Solomon cited for maintaining David's prescribed order of worship?
adults.option_a: David the man of God had commanded it, and Solomon did not decline from anything David commanded concerning priests and Levites and treasuries
adults.option_b: The Mosaic law required the same arrangement
adults.option_c: An oracle given at Gibeon specified the worship order
adults.option_d: The high priest Zadok had maintained the order from David's time
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 8:14-15 — "For so had David the man of God commanded. And they departed not from the commandment of the king unto the priests and Levites concerning any matter."

adults.question: What significance did Solomon attach to the three annual feasts in organizing temple worship?
adults.option_a: He offered burnt offerings to the LORD in the feasts of unleavened bread, weeks, and tabernacles according to the commandment of Moses
adults.option_b: He added four additional feast days to those commanded by Moses
adults.option_c: He replaced the feasts with monthly offerings instead
adults.option_d: He made the feasts optional for those living far from Jerusalem
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 8:13 — "Even after a certain rate every day, offering according to the commandment of Moses, on the sabbaths, and on the new moons, and on the solemn feasts, three times in the year."

adults.question: What was the administrative arrangement for the gatekeepers of the temple?
adults.option_a: The porters were assigned by their courses at every gate, for so had David the man of God commanded
adults.option_b: A single chief gatekeeper managed all entrances
adults.option_c: The gatekeepers were chosen by lot each month
adults.option_d: The Levitical singers also served as gatekeepers
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 8:14 — "And he appointed...the porters also by their courses at every gate: for so had David the man of God commanded."

adults.question: What does the Chronicler affirm about the completeness of Solomon's obedience at the conclusion of the building phase?
adults.option_a: Solomon's work was prepared from the day of the foundation until it was finished and the house of the LORD was perfected
adults.option_b: Some items remained incomplete until Rehoboam's reign
adults.option_c: The outer courts were not finished in Solomon's lifetime
adults.option_d: The temple was completed but the palace took another ten years
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 8:16 — "Now all the work of Solomon was prepared unto the day of the foundation of the house of the LORD, and until it was finished. So the house of the LORD was perfected."

adults.question: What is the Chronicler's purpose in emphasizing Solomon's administrative and building achievements in chapter 8?
adults.option_a: To show that Solomon fulfilled his divinely appointed role as the builder-king whose obedience to David's instructions secured the kingdom and worship of Israel
adults.option_b: To provide a historical record for future builders
adults.option_c: To justify the heavy taxation and forced labour of his reign
adults.option_d: To compare Solomon favourably with the achievements of foreign kings
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 8:16 — "So the house of the LORD was perfected," and 8:14-15 show Solomon's complete fidelity to David's order, the Chronicler's theological emphasis on the continuity of proper worship.

kids.question: Who came from far away to hear Solomon's wisdom and see his riches?
kids.explanation: 2 Chronicles 9:1 — "And when the queen of Sheba heard of the fame of Solomon, she came to prove Solomon with hard questions at Jerusalem."

kids.question: What did the queen of Sheba say about Solomon after she heard his wisdom and saw his house?
kids.option_a: The half of the greatness of thy wisdom was not told me, for thou exceedest the fame that I heard
kids.option_b: You are truly the wisest man on earth
kids.option_c: Your temple is greater than any I have seen
kids.option_d: I came doubting but now I believe
kids.explanation: 2 Chronicles 9:6 — "Howbeit I believed not their words, until I came, and mine eyes had seen it: and, behold, the one half of the greatness of thy wisdom was not told me: for thou exceedest the fame that I heard."

kids.question: How many talents of gold did Solomon receive every year?
kids.option_a: Six hundred threescore and six talents of gold
kids.explanation: 2 Chronicles 9:13 — "Now the weight of gold that came to Solomon in one year was six hundred and threescore and six talents of gold."

teens.question: What gifts did the queen of Sheba give to Solomon?
teens.option_a: An hundred and twenty talents of gold, spices in great abundance, and precious stones
teens.option_b: Fifty talents of gold and ten camels of spices
teens.option_c: A thousand servants and a hundred horses
teens.option_d: Cedar and cypress timber and purple cloth
teens.explanation: 2 Chronicles 9:9 — "And she gave the king an hundred and twenty talents of gold, and of spices great abundance, and precious stones."

teens.question: What throne did Solomon make, and what animals were on it?
teens.option_a: A great throne of ivory overlaid with gold, with six steps, a footstool, and lions standing by the stays
teens.option_b: A cedar throne with golden eagles on each arm
teens.option_c: A silver throne with carved cherubim at the back
teens.option_d: A marble throne with twelve carved oxen beneath it
teens.explanation: 2 Chronicles 9:17-18 — "Moreover the king made a great throne of ivory, and overlaid it with pure gold...And there were six steps to the throne, with a footstool of gold...and two lions standing by the stays."

teens.question: What did Solomon make two hundred of in gold for his house in the forest of Lebanon?
teens.option_a: Large shields of beaten gold, three hundred shekels of gold in each shield
teens.option_b: Golden trumpets for the Levites
teens.option_c: Candlesticks of pure gold
teens.option_d: Drinking vessels for the feast days
teens.explanation: 2 Chronicles 9:15 — "And king Solomon made two hundred targets of beaten gold: six hundred shekels of beaten gold went to one target."

teens.question: What was the drinking vessels of king Solomon made of, and why was silver not used?
teens.option_a: All the drinking vessels were of gold, and none were of silver, for silver was accounted as nothing in Solomon's days
teens.option_b: Gold mixed with ivory
teens.option_c: Pure silver with gold trim
teens.option_d: Bronze overlaid with gold leaf
teens.explanation: 2 Chronicles 9:20 — "And all the drinking vessels of king Solomon were of gold...for silver was not any thing accounted of in the days of Solomon."

teens.question: How long did Solomon reign in Jerusalem over all Israel?
teens.option_a: Forty years
teens.option_b: Thirty years
teens.option_c: Twenty years
teens.option_d: Fifty years
teens.explanation: 2 Chronicles 9:30 — "And Solomon reigned in Jerusalem over all Israel forty years."

adults.question: What was the theological observation that the queen of Sheba made about the source of Solomon's blessing?
adults.option_a: Blessed be the LORD thy God, which delighted in thee to set thee on his throne, to be king for the LORD thy God: because thy God loved Israel, he made thee king over them
adults.option_b: Solomon's wisdom came from his diligent study of the law
adults.option_c: The gods of Israel were greater than all other gods
adults.option_d: Israel's success proved the superiority of the Davidic lineage
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 9:8 — "Blessed be the LORD thy God, which delighted in thee to set thee on his throne, to be king for the LORD thy God: because thy God loved Israel, to establish them for ever, therefore made he thee king over them."

adults.question: What did the queen of Sheba's happiness imply about the relationship between Solomon's wisdom and his attendants?
adults.option_a: Happy are thy men, and happy are these thy servants, which stand continually before thee, and hear thy wisdom — proximity to wisdom brings blessing
adults.option_b: The servants were happy because of their generous wages
adults.option_c: The servants were content because of Solomon's just governance
adults.option_d: The attendants rejoiced because of the grandeur of the temple
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 9:7 — "Happy are thy men, and happy are these thy servants, which stand continually before thee, and hear thy wisdom."

adults.question: What was the fleet of Huram and Solomon's servants bringing along with gold from Ophir?
adults.option_a: Almug trees and precious stones
adults.option_b: Spices and ivory and peacocks
adults.option_c: Purple cloth and fine linen
adults.option_d: Horses and chariots
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 9:10-11 — "And the servants also of Huram, and the servants of Solomon, which brought gold from Ophir, brought algum trees and precious stones...And the king made of the algum trees terraces to the house of the LORD."

adults.question: How does the Chronicler describe the scope of Solomon's wisdom — what did all the kings of the earth seek?
adults.option_a: All the kings of the earth sought the presence of Solomon, to hear his wisdom that God had put in his heart
adults.option_b: The kings of the east and west sent ambassadors to study Israel's law
adults.option_c: All kings feared Solomon and sent tribute to avoid war
adults.option_d: The kings sought alliance with Solomon for his military strength
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 9:23 — "And all the kings of the earth sought the presence of Solomon, to hear his wisdom, that God had put in his heart."

adults.question: What was the tribute system that supported Solomon's annual income of 666 talents, beyond direct gold?
adults.option_a: All the kings of Arabia and the governors of the country brought gold and silver in addition to merchants and traders
adults.option_b: Taxation on the twelve tribes alone
adults.option_c: Income from the temple treasury offerings
adults.option_d: Trade monopoly from the Ophir fleet exclusively
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 9:14 — "Beside that which chapmen and merchants brought. And all the kings of Arabia and governors of the country brought gold and silver to Solomon."

adults.question: What was unique about the steps and the throne of Solomon among all kingdoms?
adults.option_a: There was no kingdom where the like was made — the throne had twelve lions standing on six steps with no such throne in any kingdom
adults.option_b: It was made of a single piece of ivory from a giant elephant
adults.option_c: It was designed by the craftsman Huram-abi himself
adults.option_d: It could be dismantled and transported when the king travelled
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 9:19 — "And twelve lions stood there on the one side and on the other upon the six steps. There was not the like made in any kingdom."

adults.question: What does the record of Solomon's death formula tell us, and where can more details be found?
adults.option_a: His acts, first and last, are written in the book of Nathan the prophet, and in the prophecy of Ahijah the Shilonite, and in the visions of Iddo the seer
adults.option_b: They are only recorded in 1 Kings with no additional sources
adults.option_c: A separate royal chronicle kept by the high priest
adults.option_d: The book of the chronicles of the kings of Judah
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 9:29 — "Now the rest of the acts of Solomon, first and last, are they not written in the book of Nathan the prophet, and in the prophecy of Ahijah the Shilonite, and in the visions of Iddo the seer?"

adults.question: Why is the Chronicler's account of Solomon notably different from 1 Kings in what it omits?
adults.option_a: The Chronicler omits Solomon's apostasy, foreign wives, and idolatry, focusing instead on his role as temple builder and wise king — a deliberately positive portrait for post-exilic Israel
adults.option_b: The Chronicler had access to different source documents that lacked the negative material
adults.option_c: The negative material was added to Kings much later by an editor
adults.option_d: The Chronicler wrote before Solomon's apostasy was known to the scribes
adults.explanation: Comparison of 2 Chronicles 9 with 1 Kings 11 shows the Chronicler ends with Solomon's death without mentioning apostasy, reflecting his theological purpose of presenting the ideal Davidic king for the restoration community.

kids.question: Who asked King Rehoboam to make the people's work easier?
kids.explanation: 2 Chronicles 10:3-4 — "And they sent and called him. And Jeroboam and all Israel came and spake to Rehoboam, saying, Thy father made our yoke grievous: now therefore ease thou somewhat the grievous servitude of thy father."

kids.question: Who did Rehoboam listen to instead of the wise old men?
kids.option_a: The young men who had grown up with him
kids.option_c: His own servants in the palace
kids.explanation: 2 Chronicles 10:8 — "But he forsook the counsel of the old men, and took counsel with the young men that were brought up with him."

kids.question: What did Rehoboam say his little finger was compared to his father's whole body?
kids.option_a: My little finger shall be thicker than my father's loins
kids.option_b: My little finger is stronger than my father's hand
kids.option_c: My little finger rules more than my father's arm
kids.option_d: My little finger weighs more than my father's back
kids.explanation: 2 Chronicles 10:10 — "My little finger shall be thicker than my father's loins."

teens.question: How long did Rehoboam ask for before giving his answer to the people's request?
teens.option_a: Three days
teens.option_b: Seven days
teens.option_c: One day
teens.option_d: Thirty days
teens.explanation: 2 Chronicles 10:5 — "And he said unto them, Come again unto me after three days. And the people departed."

teens.question: What was the counsel of the old men who had stood before Solomon?
teens.option_a: If thou be kind to this people and please them and speak good words to them they will be thy servants for ever
teens.option_b: Be strong and show them who is king
teens.option_c: Reduce the taxes but increase the tribute
teens.option_d: Send them away without a direct answer
teens.explanation: 2 Chronicles 10:7 — "If thou be kind to this people, and please them, and speak good words to them, they will be thy servants for ever."

teens.question: What did Rehoboam threaten to use on the people instead of whips?
teens.option_a: Scorpions
teens.option_b: Iron rods
teens.option_c: Chains
teens.option_d: Fire
