teens.explanation: 2 Chronicles 10:11 — "For whereas my father put a heavy yoke upon you, I will put more to your yoke: my father chastised you with whips, but I will chastise you with scorpions."

teens.question: What was the rallying cry of the ten tribes when they rejected Rehoboam?
teens.option_a: To your tents, O Israel: and now, David, see to thine own house
teens.option_b: We will have no king but God alone
teens.option_c: Down with the house of Solomon
teens.option_d: Let Jeroboam be our king by the choice of the people
teens.explanation: 2 Chronicles 10:16 — "And when all Israel saw that the king would not hearken unto them...saying, What portion have we in David?...to your tents, O Israel."

teens.question: What did the Chronicler say was the cause of Rehoboam's refusal to listen to the people?
teens.option_a: The cause was of God, that the LORD might perform his word which he spake by the hand of Ahijah the Shilonite to Jeroboam
teens.option_b: Rehoboam was too proud and would not take counsel
teens.option_c: The young advisors had bribed Rehoboam against the elders
teens.option_d: The devil hardened his heart as God had hardened Pharaoh's
teens.explanation: 2 Chronicles 10:15 — "So the king hearkened not unto the people: for the cause was of God, that the LORD might perform his word, which he spake by the hand of Ahijah the Shilonite to Jeroboam."

adults.question: Where did all Israel gather to make Rehoboam king, and why was this location significant?
adults.option_a: Shechem, a historically significant city of assembly associated with the covenant renewals of Joshua's era
adults.option_b: Jerusalem, the seat of David's dynasty
adults.option_c: Gibeon, where Solomon had received wisdom from God
adults.option_d: Bethel, the site of Jacob's dream
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 10:1 — "And Rehoboam went to Shechem: for to Shechem were all Israel come to make him king," echoing Joshua 24:1 and earlier covenant assemblies.

adults.question: What specific contrast did Rehoboam communicate between his governance style and his father's regarding punishment?
adults.option_a: My father chastised you with whips but I will chastise you with scorpions — implying a far more severe and cruel form of discipline
adults.option_b: My father taxed one tenth but I will tax one fifth
adults.option_c: My father drafted one man in ten but I will draft five in ten
adults.option_d: My father ruled forty years but I will rule eighty years with double the demands
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 10:11 — "My father chastised you with whips, but I will chastise you with scorpions."

adults.question: What was the prophecy of Ahijah the Shilonite that the kingdom division fulfilled?
adults.option_a: The LORD had told Jeroboam through Ahijah that he would be given ten tribes of Israel because Solomon had forsaken God and gone after other gods
adults.option_b: Ahijah had predicted a civil war after Solomon's death
adults.option_c: The division was foretold because of David's sin with Bathsheba
adults.option_d: Nathan the prophet had predicted the division in David's time
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 10:15 — "The cause was of God, that the LORD might perform his word, which he spake by the hand of Ahijah the Shilonite to Jeroboam," referring to 1 Kings 11:29-39.

adults.question: What happened to Adoram (Hadoram), whom Rehoboam sent to the northern tribes after their revolt?
adults.option_a: All Israel stoned him with stones that he died, and king Rehoboam made speed to get him up to his chariot to flee to Jerusalem
adults.option_b: He successfully collected the tribute
adults.option_c: He was taken hostage by Jeroboam
adults.option_d: He escaped but was later killed in battle
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 10:18 — "Then king Rehoboam sent Hadoram that was over the tribute; and the children of Israel stoned him with stones, that he died."

adults.question: What does the contrast between the old men's advice and the young men's advice reveal about two competing theories of leadership?
adults.option_a: The old men counselled servant-leadership (be kind, earn loyalty), while the young men counselled authoritarian dominance (show strength, increase burden)
adults.option_b: The old men were simply more cautious while the young men were more ambitious
adults.option_c: The difference was only in communication style, not in policy
adults.option_d: The young men's advice was strategically sound but poorly timed
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 10:7 — "If thou be kind to this people...they will be thy servants for ever," versus 10:10-11 where the young men counselled harsher rule.

adults.question: After the division, what territory remained loyal to the house of David?
adults.option_a: Only Israel that dwelt in the cities of Judah did Rehoboam reign over
adults.option_b: The tribes of Judah, Benjamin, and Simeon
adults.option_c: All of the southern tribes plus the Levites
adults.option_d: Judah alone, since Benjamin joined the north
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 10:17 — "But as for the children of Israel that dwelt in the cities of Judah, Rehoboam reigned over them."

adults.question: What does the division of the kingdom under Rehoboam represent theologically in the Chronicler's narrative?
adults.option_a: The inevitable consequence of Solomon's apostasy working itself out in the next generation, fulfilling God's prophetic word
adults.option_b: Simply a political failure of Rehoboam's inexperience
adults.option_c: A natural economic conflict between the north and south
adults.option_d: God's abandonment of the Davidic covenant
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 10:15 — "The cause was of God, that the LORD might perform his word," showing the division as divine judgment and covenant fulfillment, not mere political accident.

adults.question: What is the literary and theological significance of the phrase "What portion have we in David?" in verse 16?
adults.option_a: It echoes the exact words of Sheba's rebellion in 2 Samuel 20:1, showing the recurring nature of northern tribal resistance to Davidic rule
adults.option_b: It is a liturgical phrase used at covenant assemblies
adults.option_c: It was a legal formula for dissolving a royal contract
adults.option_d: It was coined by Jeroboam to rally the people emotionally
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 10:16 — "What portion have we in David?" mirrors 2 Samuel 20:1, "We have no part in David," connecting Rehoboam's crisis to earlier fault lines in the united monarchy.

kids.question: When Rehoboam wanted to fight the northern tribes, what did God say through the prophet Shemaiah?
kids.option_a: Return every man to his house, for this thing is done of me
kids.option_b: Go up and fight, I will give you victory
kids.option_c: Wait three years and then go to battle
kids.option_d: Send an army of priests instead of soldiers
kids.explanation: 2 Chronicles 11:4 — "Thus saith the LORD, Ye shall not go up, nor fight against your brethren: return every man to his house: for this thing is done of me."

kids.question: What did Rehoboam build throughout the land of Judah to make the kingdom strong?
kids.explanation: 2 Chronicles 11:5 — "And Rehoboam dwelt in Jerusalem, and built cities for defence in Judah."

kids.question: Who came from all the northern tribes to live in Judah and Jerusalem to support Rehoboam?
kids.explanation: 2 Chronicles 11:13 — "And the priests and the Levites that were in all Israel resorted to him out of all their coasts."

teens.question: How many chosen warriors did Rehoboam assemble to fight against Israel?
teens.option_a: An hundred and fourscore thousand chosen men
teens.option_b: Three hundred thousand fighting men
teens.option_c: Fifty thousand horsemen
teens.option_d: One million soldiers from all Judah
teens.explanation: 2 Chronicles 11:1 — "And when Rehoboam was come to Jerusalem, he gathered of the house of Judah and Benjamin an hundred and fourscore thousand chosen men."

teens.question: Why did the Levites leave their pastures and possessions and come to Judah and Jerusalem?
teens.option_a: Because Jeroboam and his sons had cast them off from executing the priest's office unto the LORD
teens.option_b: Because the northern kingdom taxed them more heavily
teens.option_c: Because Jerusalem had better temple facilities
teens.option_d: Because God commanded them all to return in a vision
teens.explanation: 2 Chronicles 11:14 — "For the Levites left their suburbs and their possession, and came to Judah and Jerusalem: for Jeroboam and his sons had cast them off from executing the priest's office unto the LORD."

teens.question: What false gods did Jeroboam appoint priests for in the north?
teens.option_a: He ordained him priests for the high places, and for the devils, and for the calves which he had made
teens.option_b: He set up Baal worship in every city
teens.option_c: He brought back the gods of Egypt
teens.option_d: He appointed priests of Molech and Ashtaroth
teens.explanation: 2 Chronicles 11:15 — "And he ordained him priests for the high places, and for the devils, and for the calves which he had made."

teens.question: How many wives and concubines did Rehoboam take?
teens.option_a: Eighteen wives and sixty concubines, and begat twenty-eight sons and sixty daughters
teens.option_b: Fourteen wives and thirty concubines
teens.option_c: Seven wives and twelve concubines
teens.option_d: Twenty wives and forty concubines
teens.explanation: 2 Chronicles 11:21 — "And Rehoboam loved Maachah the daughter of Absalom above all his wives and his concubines: (for he took eighteen wives, and threescore concubines; and begat twenty and eight sons, and threescore daughters.)"

teens.question: Whom did Rehoboam appoint as the leader among his sons, to be the successor to the throne?
teens.option_a: Abijah the son of Maachah
teens.option_b: His firstborn Jeush
teens.option_c: Attai his third son
teens.option_d: Ziza the son of Shelomith
teens.explanation: 2 Chronicles 11:22 — "And Rehoboam made Abijah the son of Maachah the chief, to be ruler among his brethren: for he thought to make him king."

adults.question: List the cities Rehoboam fortified as a defensive network, and what strategic pattern do they reveal?
adults.option_a: Bethlehem, Etam, Tekoa, Beth-zur, Soco, Adullam, Gath, Mareshah, Ziph, Adoraim, Lachish, Azekah, Zorah, Aijalon, and Hebron — forming a ring of forts around Judah especially on the western and southern approaches
adults.option_b: Only northern border towns facing Israel
adults.option_c: Cities along the Jordan River as a defensive line
adults.option_d: Coastal cities to protect against Philistine raids
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 11:6-10 — "He built even Beth-lehem, and Etam, and Tekoa, And Beth-zur, and Shoco, and Adullam...These are the fenced cities of Judah and Benjamin," forming a defensive perimeter.

adults.question: What did Rehoboam provision the fortified cities with, showing his logistical planning?
adults.option_a: Shields, spears, victuals, oil, and wine, and officers over every city
adults.option_b: Only weapons and soldiers without food stores
adults.option_c: Gold and silver for paying mercenaries
adults.option_d: Temple vessels moved from Jerusalem for safekeeping
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 11:11-12 — "And he fortified the strong holds, and put captains in them, and store of victual, and of oil and wine. And in every several city he put shields and spears."

adults.question: What spiritual dynamic does the Chronicler highlight about those who came from the north to Judah under Rehoboam?
adults.option_a: All those from every tribe of Israel who set their hearts to seek the LORD God of Israel came to Jerusalem to sacrifice, strengthening the kingdom for three years
adults.option_b: Only the Levites came south, ordinary Israelites stayed north
adults.option_c: The northern emigrants were mostly merchants and craftsmen
adults.option_d: The priests came only temporarily for the annual feasts
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 11:16 — "And after them out of all the tribes of Israel such as set their hearts to seek the LORD God of Israel came to Jerusalem, to sacrifice unto the LORD God of their fathers."

adults.question: What does the statement "they strengthened the kingdom of Judah and made Rehoboam the son of Solomon strong, three years" tell us about the nature of spiritual faithfulness and political stability?
adults.option_a: Faithfulness to God's worship brought national strength, but only lasted three years because Rehoboam eventually forsook the law
adults.option_b: Three years was the standard probationary period for new kings
adults.option_c: Military fortifications alone secured the kingdom for three years
adults.option_d: The northern defection actually made Judah stronger long-term
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 11:17 — "So they strengthened the kingdom of Judah, and made Rehoboam the son of Solomon strong, three years: for three years they walked in the way of David and Solomon."

adults.question: Why did Rehoboam choose Maachah as his most beloved wife when she was not his first wife?
adults.option_a: He loved Maachah the daughter of Absalom above all his wives and concubines and made her son Abijah his crown prince
adults.option_b: Maachah was the daughter of a foreign king and brought a political alliance
adults.option_c: She was the most beautiful of his wives
adults.option_d: She was the eldest and held by custom the first rank
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 11:21 — "And Rehoboam loved Maachah the daughter of Absalom above all his wives and his concubines," making her son heir apparent.

adults.question: What does Rehoboam's wise practice of distributing his sons among the fortified cities teach us about his administration?
adults.option_a: He dealt wisely, distributing his many sons to all the countries of Judah and Benjamin to all the fenced cities, giving them abundant victuals — a strategy to prevent a coup and secure loyal governors
adults.option_b: He exiled potential rivals to keep them from Jerusalem
adults.option_c: He was following the Mosaic law about royal inheritance
adults.option_d: He stationed his sons where they could learn military skills
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 11:23 — "And he dealt wisely, and dispersed of all his children throughout all the countries of Judah and Benjamin, unto every fenced city: and he gave them victual in abundance."

adults.question: What is the significance of Jeroboam's priests for "the devils" (shedim, goat-demons)?
adults.option_a: It indicates he instituted a syncretistic religion involving demonic cult worship, not merely a reformed Yahwism, showing the spiritual depravity of the northern kingdom from its foundation
adults.option_b: It was a misunderstanding by the Chronicler of ordinary high-place worship
adults.option_c: Jeroboam worshipped demons openly before all Israel
adults.option_d: The shedim were minor household gods not considered dangerous
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 11:15 — "And he ordained him priests for the high places, and for the devils (shedim), and for the calves which he had made," showing the northern cult's departure from pure Yahwism.

adults.question: What does the three-year period of faithfulness under Rehoboam (v.17) reveal about the Chronicler's retribution theology?
adults.option_a: The Chronicler consistently links obedience to blessing and disobedience to punishment — Rehoboam's faithfulness brought military strength and the kingdom was strengthened, before he later forsook the law and suffered invasion
adults.option_b: Three years was a fixed period of divine testing before judgment
adults.option_c: The Chronicler is uncertain about the length of Rehoboam's good period
adults.option_d: Military strength came from David's legacy, not from Rehoboam's personal faithfulness
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 11:17 — "Three years they walked in the way of David and Solomon," connected directly to their being strengthened, anticipating the contrast with chapter 12 when Rehoboam forsook the law and suffered Shishak's invasion.

kids.question: What did Rehoboam and all Israel do that caused God to let a foreign king attack them?
kids.option_a: They forsook the law of the LORD
kids.option_b: They stopped going to the temple
kids.option_c: They refused to pay their tithes
kids.explanation: 2 Chronicles 12:1 — "And it came to pass, when Rehoboam had established the kingdom, and had strengthened himself, he forsook the law of the LORD, and all Israel with him."

kids.question: Who was the foreign king who came up against Jerusalem?
kids.explanation: 2 Chronicles 12:2 — "And it came to pass, that in the fifth year of king Rehoboam Shishak king of Egypt came up against Jerusalem."

kids.question: What did Rehoboam and the princes do when the prophet Shemaiah told them God was punishing them?
kids.option_a: They humbled themselves and said, The LORD is righteous
kids.option_b: They went out to fight the Egyptian army
kids.option_c: They prayed all night in the temple
kids.option_d: They sent gifts to Shishak to stop him
kids.explanation: 2 Chronicles 12:6 — "Whereupon the princes of Israel and the king humbled themselves; and they said, The LORD is righteous."

teens.question: How large was Shishak's army that came against Jerusalem?
teens.option_a: Twelve hundred chariots, and threescore thousand horsemen, and people without number out of Egypt
teens.option_b: Five hundred chariots and twenty thousand infantry
teens.option_c: A million soldiers from Cush and Egypt
teens.option_d: Three thousand chariots and ninety thousand foot soldiers
teens.explanation: 2 Chronicles 12:3 — "With twelve hundred chariots, and threescore thousand horsemen: and the people were without number that came with him out of Egypt; the Lubims, the Sukkiims, and the Ethiopians."

teens.question: What did God say he would do differently because they had humbled themselves?
teens.option_a: He would not destroy them but grant them some deliverance, and not pour his wrath on Jerusalem by the hand of Shishak
teens.option_b: He would drive out Shishak's army in one night
teens.option_c: He would send an angel to protect the temple treasury
teens.option_d: He would restore all that Shishak had taken
teens.explanation: 2 Chronicles 12:7 — "And when the LORD saw that they humbled themselves, the word of the LORD came to Shemaiah, saying, They have humbled themselves; therefore I will not destroy them, but I will grant them some deliverance."

teens.question: What did Shishak take when he came to Jerusalem?
teens.option_a: The treasures of the house of the LORD and the treasures of the king's house, and the shields of gold Solomon had made
teens.option_b: Only the gold from the temple altar
teens.option_c: The ark of the covenant
teens.option_d: The king's throne and royal treasury
teens.explanation: 2 Chronicles 12:9 — "So Shishak king of Egypt came up against Jerusalem, and took away the treasures of the house of the LORD, and the treasures of the king's house; he took all: he carried away also the shields of gold which Solomon had made."

teens.question: What did Rehoboam make to replace the golden shields that Shishak had taken?
teens.option_a: Shields of brass
teens.option_b: Shields of silver
teens.option_c: Shields of iron
teens.option_d: Shields of wood overlaid with bronze
teens.explanation: 2 Chronicles 12:10 — "Instead of which king Rehoboam made shields of brass, and committed them to the hands of the chief of the guard."

teens.question: What was the overall moral summary the Chronicler gave about Rehoboam's character?
teens.option_a: He did evil, because he prepared not his heart to seek the LORD
teens.option_b: He was a good king who made one great mistake
teens.option_c: He was neither righteous nor wicked but walked in the middle way
teens.option_d: He started evil but repented and ended well
teens.explanation: 2 Chronicles 12:14 — "And he did evil, because he prepared not his heart to seek the LORD."

adults.question: What specific word from God did the prophet Shemaiah deliver to explain why Shishak had been permitted to succeed?
adults.option_a: Ye have forsaken me, and therefore have I also left you in the hand of Shishak
adults.option_b: Because thou hast not walked in the way of thy father Solomon
adults.option_c: The covenant is broken and I will no longer fight for you
adults.option_d: This calamity comes because thou hast made Egypt an ally
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 12:5 — "Thus saith the LORD, Ye have forsaken me, and therefore have I also left you in the hand of Shishak."

adults.question: What is the theological structure of "forsake-abandon" that 12:5 demonstrates in the Chronicler's pattern?
adults.option_a: When Israel forsook God, God in turn forsook (abandoned/handed over) Israel — a precise reciprocal covenant consequence showing divine justice
adults.option_b: God acted arbitrarily without regard to Israel's behavior
adults.option_c: God used Egypt as a neutral instrument with no covenantal significance
adults.option_d: Shishak invaded because of Solomon's sins, not Rehoboam's
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 12:5 — "Ye have forsaken me, and therefore have I also left you," perfectly mirroring 7:22's warning and establishing the Chronicler's consistent pattern of immediate retribution.

adults.question: What was the precise condition that led God to modify his judgment from destruction to partial deliverance?
adults.option_a: Their humbling of themselves — the LORD saw that they humbled themselves and therefore granted some deliverance and would not pour out his wrath by Shishak
adults.option_b: Their offering of burnt offerings at the temple
adults.option_c: The intercession of the priest Shemaiah on their behalf
adults.option_d: The partial faithfulness of the Levites who had not forsaken God
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 12:7 — "When the LORD saw that they humbled themselves...I will not destroy them, but I will grant them some deliverance; and my wrath shall not be poured out upon Jerusalem by the hand of Shishak."

adults.question: What theological lesson does the replacement of golden shields with bronze ones symbolize?
adults.option_a: It visually represented the diminished glory of the kingdom under God's discipline — gold replaced by an inferior metal symbolizes the loss that comes from spiritual compromise
adults.option_b: It was a purely practical economic decision after the treasury was emptied
adults.option_c: Bronze was harder and more practical for ceremonial use
adults.option_d: It fulfilled a prophecy about the kingdoms declining metals as in Daniel's statue
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 12:10 — "Instead of which king Rehoboam made shields of brass," the substitution of brass for gold visually embodying the spiritual and material decline caused by Rehoboam's unfaithfulness.

adults.question: What was Shishak's full strategic achievement in this campaign, beyond taking Jerusalem's treasuries?
adults.option_a: He took the fenced cities of Judah and came to Jerusalem, meaning Rehoboam's entire fortification network had been breached before the capital was threatened
adults.option_b: He only attacked Jerusalem without touching other cities
adults.option_c: He set up a permanent garrison in Jerusalem
adults.option_d: He destroyed the temple and carried off the priests
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 12:4 — "And he took the fenced cities which pertained to Judah, and came to Jerusalem," showing Rehoboam's entire defensive ring (built in ch. 11) had fallen.

adults.question: What does the Chronicler say about the condition of Judah after the humbling before Shishak?
adults.option_a: They were Shishak's servants, that they might know my service and the service of the kingdoms of the countries
adults.option_b: They were restored to their former strength within a year
adults.option_c: They paid annual tribute to Egypt for forty years
adults.option_d: They remained under Egyptian occupation until Solomon's death
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 12:8 — "Nevertheless they shall be his servants; that they may know my service, and the service of the kingdoms of the countries," a profoundly ironic lesson about the choice between serving God and serving foreign kings.

adults.question: What does God's statement "that they may know my service, and the service of the kingdoms of the countries" (12:8) reveal about his pedagogical purpose in judgment?
adults.option_a: God used Shishak's conquest as a teaching moment — Israel would learn through comparison that serving God was far lighter than serving pagan kings, making discipline a form of instruction rather than mere punishment
adults.option_b: God was abandoning Israel permanently to foreign domination
adults.option_c: The statement was ironic: God was mocking Israel for their choice
adults.option_d: It was a one-time lesson without broader application
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 12:8 — "That they may know my service, and the service of the kingdoms of the countries," showing divine discipline as educative, designed to restore proper perspective on the relative demands of serving God versus serving human masters.

adults.question: How does the Chronicler's summary assessment of Rehoboam — "he prepared not his heart to seek the LORD" (12:14) — function as the key to understanding all of chapters 10-12?
adults.option_a: It identifies the root failure as not a single act but a character disposition — a heart not oriented toward God — which explains both his foolish political decision in chapter 10 and his spiritual apostasy in chapter 12
adults.option_b: It refers only to the specific incident of forsaking the law in 12:1
adults.option_c: It is a formulaic condemnation the Chronicler applies to all bad kings without specific meaning
adults.option_d: It means Rehoboam never attended temple services
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 12:14 — "He did evil, because he prepared not his heart to seek the LORD," linking his political failures (ch. 10), fortification successes (ch. 11), and ultimate spiritual failure (ch. 12) to a single underlying issue of the heart.

kids.question: Who was the king of Judah who fought against Jeroboam?
kids.explanation: 2 Chronicles 13:1 — "Now in the eighteenth year of king Jeroboam began Abijah to reign over Judah."

kids.question: What kind of covenant did Abijah say God had made with David and his sons?
kids.explanation: 2 Chronicles 13:5 — "Ought ye not to know that the LORD God of Israel gave the kingdom over Israel to David for ever, even to him and to his sons by a covenant of salt?"

kids.question: Who did Abijah say was fighting for Judah against Jeroboam?
kids.explanation: 2 Chronicles 13:12 — "And, behold, God himself is with us for our captain, and his priests with sounding trumpets to cry alarm against you."

teens.question: How many soldiers did Abijah have compared to Jeroboam in their battle?
teens.option_a: Abijah had 400,000 and Jeroboam had 800,000 chosen men
teens.option_b: Abijah had 500,000 and Jeroboam had 500,000 men
teens.option_c: Abijah had 300,000 and Jeroboam had 600,000 men
teens.option_d: Abijah had 200,000 and Jeroboam had 800,000 men
teens.explanation: 2 Chronicles 13:3 — "And Abijah set the battle in array with an army of valiant men of war, even four hundred thousand chosen men: Jeroboam also set the battle in array against him with eight hundred thousand chosen men."

teens.question: On what mountain did Abijah stand and give his speech to Jeroboam?
teens.option_a: Mount Zemaraim
teens.option_b: Mount Carmel
teens.option_c: Mount Moriah
teens.option_d: Mount Ephraim
teens.explanation: 2 Chronicles 13:4 — "And Abijah stood up upon mount Zemaraim, which is in mount Ephraim, and said, Hear me, thou Jeroboam, and all Israel."

teens.question: What was Jeroboam's military tactic against Abijah in the battle?
teens.option_a: He set an ambushment behind Judah while the front army faced them
teens.option_b: He attacked at night while Judah was sleeping
teens.option_c: He cut off Judah's water supply first
teens.option_d: He sent spies to cause confusion in Judah's camp
teens.explanation: 2 Chronicles 13:13 — "But Jeroboam caused an ambushment to come about behind them: so they were before Judah, and the ambushment was behind them."

teens.question: What did Judah do when they found themselves surrounded by Jeroboam's army?
teens.option_a: They cried unto the LORD and the priests sounded the trumpets
teens.option_b: They retreated to the mountains
teens.option_c: They sent messengers to make peace
teens.option_d: They charged the enemy without fear
teens.explanation: 2 Chronicles 13:14 — "And when Judah looked back, behold, the battle was before and behind: and they cried unto the LORD, and the priests sounded with the trumpets."

teens.question: How many casualties did Israel suffer when God delivered them into Abijah's hand?
teens.option_a: Five hundred thousand chosen men fell slain
teens.option_b: Two hundred thousand men were killed
teens.option_c: Eight hundred thousand men perished
teens.option_d: One hundred thousand men were slain
teens.explanation: 2 Chronicles 13:17 — "And Abijah and his people slew them with a great slaughter: so there fell down slain of Israel five hundred thousand chosen men."

adults.question: What accusation did Abijah make against Jeroboam regarding the priesthood and worship of Israel?
adults.option_a: Jeroboam had driven out the sons of Aaron and the Levites and made priests of the lowest of the people for golden calves
adults.option_b: Jeroboam had stolen the temple treasures from Jerusalem
adults.option_c: Jeroboam had made a treaty with Egypt against Israel's God
adults.option_d: Jeroboam had burned the law of Moses in public
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 13:9 — "Have ye not cast out the priests of the LORD, the sons of Aaron, and the Levites, and have made you priests after the manner of the nations of other lands?"

adults.question: What did Abijah say distinguished Judah from Israel in terms of their religious practice?
adults.option_a: Judah had not forsaken the LORD — they had priests of Aaron ministering and Levites in their work, offering morning and evening burnt offerings and incense
adults.option_b: Judah had built a new temple surpassing Solomon's
adults.option_c: Judah's army was stronger because of their obedience
adults.option_d: Judah paid tithes to all the Levites faithfully
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 13:10-11 — "But as for us, the LORD is our God, and we have not forsaken him; and the priests, which minister unto the LORD, are the sons of Aaron, and the Levites wait upon their business."

adults.question: What cities did Abijah capture from Jeroboam during and after the battle?
adults.option_a: Bethel, Jeshanah, and Ephrain with their surrounding villages
adults.option_b: Samaria, Dan, and Beersheba
adults.option_c: Shechem, Tirzah, and Penuel
adults.option_d: Ramah, Gibeah, and Mizpah
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 13:19 — "And Abijah pursued after Jeroboam, and took cities from him, Bethel with the towns thereof, and Jeshanah with the towns thereof, and Ephrain with the towns thereof."

adults.question: What was the theological explanation the Chronicler gave for Judah's victory over the numerically superior force?
adults.option_a: The children of Israel were brought under at that time and the children of Judah prevailed because they relied upon the LORD God of their fathers
adults.option_b: Judah had a superior military strategy devised by Abijah
adults.option_c: God sent an angel to fight for Judah directly
adults.option_d: Jeroboam's soldiers refused to fight fellow Israelites
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 13:18 — "Thus the children of Israel were brought under at that time, and the children of Judah prevailed, because they relied upon the LORD God of their fathers."

adults.question: What happened to Jeroboam personally as a result of his opposition to Abijah and Judah?
adults.option_a: The LORD struck him and he died
adults.option_b: He was captured and held prisoner in Jerusalem
adults.option_c: He fled to Egypt and never returned
adults.option_d: He lost his throne to his son immediately
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 13:20 — "Neither did Jeroboam recover strength again in the days of Abijah: and the LORD struck him, and he died."

adults.question: How many wives and children did Abijah have, showing the extent of his dynasty?
adults.option_a: Fourteen wives, twenty-two sons, and sixteen daughters
adults.option_b: Ten wives, twelve sons, and eight daughters
adults.option_c: Seven wives, thirty sons, and twenty daughters
adults.option_d: Eighteen wives, forty sons, and thirty daughters
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 13:21 — "But Abijah waxed mighty, and married fourteen wives, and begat twenty and two sons, and sixteen daughters."

adults.question: Where are further details of Abijah's acts, words, and ways recorded according to the Chronicler?
adults.option_a: In the story of the prophet Iddo
adults.option_b: In the book of the kings of Israel
adults.option_c: In the annals of the palace scribes
adults.option_d: In the records of the high priest
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 13:22 — "And the rest of the acts of Abijah, and his ways, and his sayings, are written in the story of the prophet Iddo."

adults.question: What does Abijah's speech from Mount Zemaraim reveal about the Chronicler's theological perspective on the division of the kingdom?
adults.option_a: The Chronicler presents the north as in apostasy while the Davidic covenant and Levitical worship in Judah remain the legitimate expression of Israel's religion
adults.option_b: The Chronicler sees both kingdoms as equally sinful before God
adults.option_c: The Chronicler blames Solomon rather than Jeroboam for the division
adults.option_d: The Chronicler views the division as permanent and irreversible in God's plan
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 13:5 — "Ought ye not to know that the LORD God of Israel gave the kingdom over Israel to David for ever, even to him and to his sons by a covenant of salt?" showing covenant legitimacy resting with Judah.

kids.question: Who was the king of Judah who had peace and rest during his early reign?
kids.explanation: 2 Chronicles 14:1 — "So Abijah slept with his fathers...and Asa his son reigned in his stead. In his days the land was quiet ten years."

kids.question: What did Asa ask God to give his people when a huge army came against him?
kids.option_a: Help and rest against the mighty
kids.option_d: A dream to tell him what to do
kids.explanation: 2 Chronicles 14:11 — "And Asa cried unto the LORD his God, and said, LORD, it is nothing with thee to help, whether with many, or with them that have no power: help us, O LORD our God."

kids.question: Who led the huge army of a million men that came against Asa?
kids.explanation: 2 Chronicles 14:9 — "And there came out against them Zerah the Ethiopian with an host of a thousand thousand, and three hundred chariots."

teens.question: What religious reforms did Asa carry out in the land during his years of peace?
teens.option_a: He removed the foreign altars and high places, broke the images, and cut down the groves
teens.option_b: He rebuilt the temple walls and repaired the bronze altar
teens.option_c: He drove out all the foreign traders from Jerusalem
teens.option_d: He reinstated the Passover feast that had been neglected
teens.explanation: 2 Chronicles 14:3 — "For he took away the altars of the strange gods, and the high places, and brake down the images, and cut down the groves."

teens.question: What did Asa tell Judah to seek and do during the years of peace?
teens.option_a: Seek the LORD God of their fathers and do the law and the commandment
teens.option_b: Build fortified cities and store up supplies
teens.option_c: Make alliances with neighboring nations
teens.option_d: Increase their army and train more soldiers
teens.explanation: 2 Chronicles 14:4 — "And commanded Judah to seek the LORD God of their fathers, and to do the law and the commandment."

teens.question: What did Asa build during the years of peace, and what did he say to Judah about it?
teens.option_a: Fenced cities with walls, towers, gates, and bars; he said the land was theirs because they had sought the LORD
teens.option_b: A new palace larger than Solomon's
teens.option_c: A fleet of ships for trade
teens.option_d: A great road connecting all the cities of Judah
teens.explanation: 2 Chronicles 14:7 — "Therefore he said unto Judah, Let us build these cities, and make about them walls, and towers, gates, and bars, while the land is yet before us; because we have sought the LORD our God."

teens.question: Where did the battle between Asa and Zerah the Ethiopian take place?
teens.option_a: In the valley of Zephathah at Mareshah
teens.option_b: At the Jordan River crossing
teens.option_c: Near Jerusalem at the Kidron valley
teens.option_d: At Megiddo in the plain of Jezreel
teens.explanation: 2 Chronicles 14:10 — "Then Asa went out against him, and they set the battle in array in the valley of Zephathah at Mareshah."

teens.question: What did God do in response to Asa's prayer against Zerah?
teens.option_a: The LORD smote the Ethiopians before Asa and before Judah and they fled
teens.option_b: He sent fire from heaven to consume their chariots
teens.option_c: He caused an earthquake to swallow the enemy army
teens.option_d: He sent hornets to drive away the Ethiopian army
teens.explanation: 2 Chronicles 14:12 — "So the LORD smote the Ethiopians before Asa, and before Judah; and the Ethiopians fled."

adults.question: What was the precise size of Asa's army when he faced Zerah the Ethiopian?
adults.option_a: Three hundred thousand men from Judah bearing shields and spears, and two hundred and fourscore thousand from Benjamin bearing shields and drawing bows
adults.option_b: Five hundred thousand men from all the tribes
adults.option_c: Two hundred thousand chosen warriors
adults.option_d: Four hundred thousand men armed with swords
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 14:8 — "And Asa had an army of men that bare targets and spears, out of Judah three hundred thousand; and out of Benjamin...two hundred and fourscore thousand."

adults.question: What was the full content of Asa's prayer before battle, and what theological principle did it express?
adults.option_a: He said it is nothing for the LORD to help with many or with those of no power, and asked God to rest on them so that men would not prevail against God
adults.option_b: He asked for a sign before committing to battle
adults.option_c: He recited all of God's past victories for Israel
adults.option_d: He promised to build a new altar if God gave victory
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 14:11 — "LORD, it is nothing with thee to help, whether with many, or with them that have no power: help us, O LORD our God; for we rest on thee, and in thy name we go against this multitude."

adults.question: What cities were spoiled after the Ethiopian defeat, and what was found there?
adults.option_a: They smote all the cities round about Gerar for the fear of the LORD was upon them, and they spoiled all the cities and carried away much spoil
adults.option_b: Only Gerar itself was spoiled and emptied
adults.option_c: They took three specific named cities along the trade route
adults.option_d: They seized Egyptian trading posts along the Nile delta
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 14:14 — "And they smote all the cities round about Gerar; for the fear of the LORD came upon them: and they spoiled all the cities; for there was exceeding much spoil in them."

adults.question: What livestock did Judah carry away from the battle's aftermath?
adults.option_a: Sheep and camels in abundance
adults.option_b: Horses and mules from the Ethiopian cavalry
adults.option_c: Cattle and donkeys for agriculture
adults.option_d: Goats and oxen as temple offerings
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 14:15 — "They smote also the tents of cattle, and carried away sheep and camels in abundance, and returned to Jerusalem."

adults.question: How does the Chronicler's account of Asa's reign in chapter 14 establish a pattern for his overall theology of history?
adults.option_a: Seeking God brings peace and victory; Asa's prosperity is explicitly linked to his obedience, establishing a retribution theology the Chronicler applies throughout
adults.option_b: The Chronicler shows that military strength alone determines outcome
adults.option_c: The Chronicler demonstrates that alliances with foreign powers are always blessed
adults.option_d: The Chronicler indicates that reform is only needed after military defeat
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 14:7 — "Because we have sought the LORD our God, we have sought him, and he hath given us rest on every side."

adults.question: What does the description of Asa's fortified cities reveal about his administrative capabilities?
adults.option_a: He was a strategic builder who took advantage of peace to strengthen Judah's defenses with walls, towers, gates, and bars — demonstrating prudent governance
adults.option_b: He relied entirely on God and built nothing for national defense
adults.option_c: His building projects were condemned by the prophets as a lack of faith
adults.option_d: He copied Solomon's building projects without original strategy
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 14:6-7 — "And he built fenced cities in Judah: for the land had rest...and he had no war in those years."

adults.question: What does the phrase "the LORD thy God is with thee" imply about the relationship between divine presence and human seeking in this chapter?
adults.option_a: The divine presence is conditioned upon and responsive to wholehearted seeking — "because thou hast sought the LORD thy God, he is with thee"
adults.option_b: God's presence is unconditional regardless of human behavior
adults.option_c: God's presence was guaranteed only to the Davidic kings
adults.option_d: The phrase refers only to military victory and has no spiritual dimension
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 15:2 — "The LORD is with you, while ye be with him; and if ye seek him, he will be found of you."

adults.question: What does Zerah's force of a million men and three hundred chariots against Asa's 580,000 suggest about the theological point the Chronicler is making?
adults.option_a: That numerical and material superiority are irrelevant when God fights for those who trust him — Asa's smaller force defeats the larger through prayer
adults.option_b: That Judah had grown complacent and needed a wake-up call
adults.option_c: That God only helps when the odds are even
adults.option_d: That Asa's military reforms had secretly made Judah stronger than the numbers suggest
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 14:11 — "LORD, it is nothing with thee to help, whether with many, or with them that have no power," making the disparity in size the very point.

kids.question: What prophet came to King Asa with a message from God?
kids.explanation: 2 Chronicles 15:1 — "And the Spirit of God came upon Azariah the son of Oded."

kids.question: What did Azariah tell Asa that God would do if they sought him?
kids.option_a: He would be found by them
kids.option_b: He would give them gold and silver
kids.option_c: He would destroy all their enemies
kids.option_d: He would give them a long life
kids.explanation: 2 Chronicles 15:2 — "The LORD is with you, while ye be with him; and if ye seek him, he will be found of you."

kids.question: What did Asa do to his grandmother Maachah because of her idol?
kids.option_a: He removed her from being queen and destroyed her idol
kids.option_b: He sent her to live in another country
kids.option_c: He put her in prison for her sin
kids.option_d: He made her pay a fine for the idol
kids.explanation: 2 Chronicles 15:16 — "And also concerning Maachah the mother of Asa the king, he removed her from being queen, because she had made an idol in a grove."

teens.question: What warning did Azariah give about forsaking God based on Israel's past history?
teens.option_a: If they forsook God he would forsake them, and for a long time Israel had been without the true God, without a teaching priest, and without law
teens.option_b: God would send a foreign king to conquer them
teens.option_c: The land would suffer drought and famine for seven years
teens.option_d: The Davidic dynasty would come to an end
teens.explanation: 2 Chronicles 15:2-3 — "But if ye forsake him, he will forsake you...Now for a long season Israel hath been without the true God, and without a teaching priest, and without law."

teens.question: What did Asa do to the temple as a result of Azariah's prophecy?
teens.option_a: He put away the abominable idols from all the land of Judah and Benjamin and renewed the altar of the LORD
teens.option_b: He tore down and rebuilt the entire temple
teens.option_c: He hired new priests to replace the corrupt ones
teens.option_d: He moved the ark of the covenant back to its original position
teens.explanation: 2 Chronicles 15:8 — "And when Asa heard these words...he took courage, and put away the abominable idols out of all the land of Judah and Benjamin...and renewed the altar of the LORD."

teens.question: Who gathered to Jerusalem for the great assembly and covenant renewal under Asa?
teens.option_a: People from Judah, Benjamin, and strangers from Ephraim, Manasseh, and Simeon who had fallen to him out of Israel
teens.option_b: Only the people of Judah and Benjamin
teens.option_c: Representatives from all twelve tribes equally
teens.option_d: Levites from all parts of the country
teens.explanation: 2 Chronicles 15:9 — "And he gathered all Judah and Benjamin, and the strangers with them out of Ephraim and Manasseh, and out of Simeon: for they fell to him out of Israel in abundance, when they saw that the LORD his God was with him."

teens.question: What happened at the great assembly — what sacrifices were offered?
teens.option_a: Seven hundred oxen and seven thousand sheep from the spoil they had brought
teens.option_b: Three thousand bulls and ten thousand sheep
teens.option_c: One thousand oxen and five thousand sheep as firstfruits
teens.option_d: The usual daily offerings plus a hundred special sacrifices
teens.explanation: 2 Chronicles 15:11 — "And they offered unto the LORD the same time, of the spoil which they had brought, seven hundred oxen and seven thousand sheep."

teens.question: What was the penalty established in the covenant renewal for anyone who would not seek the LORD God of Israel?
teens.option_a: He should be put to death, whether small or great, man or woman
teens.option_b: He should be banished from the land
teens.option_c: He should pay a heavy fine to the temple
teens.option_d: He should lose all his property
teens.explanation: 2 Chronicles 15:13 — "That whosoever would not seek the LORD God of Israel should be put to death, whether small or great, whether man or woman."

adults.question: What specific idol did Maachah make that caused Asa to remove her as queen mother, and what did he do to it?
adults.option_a: She made an idol in a grove and Asa cut down her idol and stamped it and burnt it at the brook Kidron
adults.option_b: She set up a golden calf and Asa melted it down
adults.option_c: She built a high place for Baal and Asa demolished it
adults.option_d: She brought Asherah poles into the temple and Asa removed them
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 15:16 — "He removed her from being queen, because she had made an idol in a grove: and Asa cut down her idol, and stamped it, and burnt it at the brook Kidron."

adults.question: What remained even after Asa's reforms, and what does this reveal about the limits of his reformation?
adults.option_a: The high places were not taken away out of Israel, though Asa's heart was perfect all his days
adults.option_b: Asa failed to remove the golden calves from Bethel and Dan
adults.option_c: The Asherah poles remained in Samaria throughout his reign
adults.option_d: The foreign priests were not expelled despite the reform decree
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 15:17 — "But the high places were not taken away out of Israel: nevertheless the heart of Asa was perfect all his days."

adults.question: What did Asa bring into the house of God, and from where did these things come?
adults.option_a: The silver and gold and vessels that he and his father had dedicated
adults.option_b: Spoil from the Ethiopian war including captured weapons
adults.option_c: Tribute payments from all the nations around Judah
adults.option_d: New furnishings crafted by the artisans of Tyre
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 15:18 — "And he brought into the house of God the things that his father had dedicated, and that he himself had dedicated, silver, and gold, and vessels."

adults.question: What does the phrase "they sware unto the LORD with a loud voice, and with shouting, and with trumpets, and with cornets" reveal about this covenant renewal ceremony?
adults.option_a: It was a full national liturgical event combining solemn vow with joyful public worship — the community's wholehearted commitment expressed through both word and music
adults.option_b: It was a military victory celebration that included a vow
adults.option_c: It was a private ceremony for the priests and Levites only
adults.option_d: It was modeled on the Sinai covenant ceremony described in Exodus
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 15:14 — "And they sware unto the LORD with a loud voice, and with shouting, and with trumpets, and with cornets."

adults.question: What was the result of the covenant renewal — what did God give Judah?
adults.option_a: The LORD gave them rest round about
adults.option_b: God sent rain after three years of drought
adults.option_c: God doubled their harvests for ten years
adults.option_d: God gave them victory over three surrounding nations
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 15:15 — "And all Judah rejoiced at the oath: for they had sworn with all their heart, and sought him with their whole desire; and he was found of them: and the LORD gave them rest round about."

adults.question: How long had it been since there was "no peace to him that went out, nor to him that came in" according to Azariah's historical review?
adults.option_a: During those ancient troubled times when nation was destroyed of nation and city of city, before Asa's time of peace
adults.option_b: During the reign of Rehoboam specifically
adults.option_c: For the forty years of wilderness wandering
adults.option_d: During the period of the judges before the monarchy
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 15:5-6 — "And in those times there was no peace to him that went out, nor to him that came in, but great vexations were upon all the inhabitants of the countries. And nation was destroyed of nation, and city of city."

adults.question: What year of Asa's reign did the great assembly and covenant renewal take place?
adults.option_a: The fifteenth year of Asa's reign
adults.option_b: The tenth year of his reign
adults.option_c: The twenty-fifth year of his reign
adults.option_d: The first year of his reign
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 15:10 — "So they gathered themselves together at Jerusalem in the third month, in the fifteenth year of the reign of Asa."

adults.question: What does Azariah's statement "if ye seek him, he will be found of you; but if ye forsake him, he will forsake you" establish as a governing principle throughout Chronicles?
adults.option_a: A conditional covenant relationship: divine blessing and presence are contingent on human seeking and faithfulness, not merely ethnic or dynastic privilege
adults.option_b: An unconditional promise based solely on David's covenant
adults.option_c: A principle that applies only to kings, not ordinary Israelites
adults.option_d: A temporary arrangement until the building of the temple
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 15:2 — "The LORD is with you, while ye be with him; and if ye seek him, he will be found of you; but if ye forsake him, he will forsake you."

kids.question: Instead of trusting God when Baasha king of Israel threatened him, what did Asa do?
kids.option_a: He sent silver and gold to the king of Syria for help
kids.option_b: He prayed and fasted for three days
kids.option_c: He built a bigger army for Judah
kids.option_d: He asked the priests what to do
kids.explanation: 2 Chronicles 16:2-3 — "Then Asa brought out silver and gold out of the treasures of the house of the LORD...and sent to Benhadad king of Syria."

kids.question: What prophet came to rebuke Asa for trusting Syria instead of God?
kids.explanation: 2 Chronicles 16:7 — "And at that time Hanani the seer came to Asa king of Judah."

kids.question: What sickness did God send upon Asa near the end of his life?
kids.option_d: Fever that would not go away
kids.explanation: 2 Chronicles 16:12 — "And Asa in the thirty and ninth year of his reign was diseased in his feet."

teens.question: What had God done for Asa earlier that Hanani referenced to rebuke his failure to trust?
teens.option_a: God had delivered the Ethiopians and the Lubims and a great host into his hand because he relied on the LORD
teens.option_b: God had sent prophets to warn him about Syria three times
teens.option_c: God had blessed his building projects abundantly
teens.option_d: God had made him the most powerful king since Solomon
teens.explanation: 2 Chronicles 16:8 — "Were not the Ethiopians and the Lubims a huge host, with very many chariots and horsemen? yet, because thou didst rely on the LORD, he delivered them into thine hand."

teens.question: What did Hanani say about those whose heart is perfect toward God?
teens.option_a: The eyes of the LORD run to and fro throughout the whole earth to show himself strong on their behalf
teens.option_b: God will give them rest from all their enemies
teens.option_c: Their children will reign after them in peace
teens.option_d: God will send angels to guard them
teens.explanation: 2 Chronicles 16:9 — "For the eyes of the LORD run to and fro throughout the whole earth, to shew himself strong in the behalf of them whose heart is perfect toward him."

teens.question: What did Asa do to Hanani after the prophet rebuked him?
teens.option_a: He put him in prison and oppressed some of the people at the same time
teens.option_b: He sent him away from Jerusalem in exile
teens.option_c: He fined him a large amount of silver
teens.option_d: He accepted the rebuke and repented publicly
teens.explanation: 2 Chronicles 16:10 — "Then Asa was wroth with the seer, and put him in a prison house; for he was in a rage with him because of this thing. And Asa oppressed some of the people the same time."

teens.question: What did Asa do in his illness that showed his continued failure to trust God?
teens.option_a: He sought not the LORD but only the physicians
teens.option_b: He offered sacrifices to foreign gods
teens.option_c: He refused to let the Levites pray for him
teens.option_d: He fled to Egypt to find medicine
teens.explanation: 2 Chronicles 16:12 — "And Asa in the thirty and ninth year of his reign was diseased in his feet, until his disease was exceeding great: yet in his disease he sought not to the LORD, but to the physicians."

teens.question: What did Hanani say would happen to Asa because he relied on Syria rather than on God?
teens.option_a: From henceforth he would have wars
teens.option_b: His kingdom would be divided among his sons
teens.option_c: Syria would eventually conquer Jerusalem
teens.option_d: The tribute he paid would bankrupt the kingdom
teens.explanation: 2 Chronicles 16:9 — "Herein thou hast done foolishly: therefore from henceforth thou shalt have wars."

adults.question: What was the political situation that led Asa to seek Syrian help — what had Baasha done?
adults.option_a: Baasha built Ramah to prevent anyone going out or coming in to Asa king of Judah
adults.option_b: Baasha invaded southern Judah and captured three cities
adults.option_c: Baasha made an alliance with Egypt against Judah
adults.option_d: Baasha cut off Judah's access to the sea ports
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 16:1 — "In the six and thirtieth year of the reign of Asa Baasha king of Israel came up against Judah, and built Ramah, to the intent that he might let none go out or come in to Asa king of Judah."

adults.question: What was the precise agreement Asa made with Benhadad of Syria and what did it involve breaking?
adults.option_a: He sent silver and gold from the treasures of the house of the LORD and the king's house and asked Benhadad to break his league with Baasha
adults.option_b: He gave Syrian merchants trading rights in Jerusalem
adults.option_c: He surrendered five cities to Syria in exchange for military help
adults.option_d: He gave Benhadad hostages from the royal family
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 16:3 — "There is a league between me and thee, as there was between my father and thy father: behold, I have sent thee silver and gold; go, break thy league with Baasha king of Israel."

adults.question: What did Benhadad do militarily after receiving Asa's silver and gold?
adults.option_a: He smote Ijon, Dan, Abelmaim, and the store cities of Naphtali
adults.option_b: He attacked Samaria and forced Baasha to withdraw
adults.option_c: He occupied the Jordan valley as a buffer zone
adults.option_d: He besieged Tirzah, capital of northern Israel
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 16:4 — "And Benhadad hearkened unto king Asa, and sent the captains of his armies against the cities of Israel; and they smote Ijon, and Dan, and Abelmaim, and all the store cities of Naphtali."

adults.question: What did Asa do with the stone and timber Baasha had been using to build Ramah?
adults.option_a: He took them and built Geba and Mizpah with them
adults.option_b: He destroyed all of it so Baasha could not finish
adults.option_c: He used it to repair the walls of Jerusalem
adults.option_d: He stored the materials in the temple precincts
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 16:6 — "Then Asa the king took all Judah; and they carried away the stones of Ramah, and the timber thereof, wherewith Baasha was building; and he built therewith Geba and Mizpah."

adults.question: What contrast does Hanani draw between what Asa could have had versus what he now has?
adults.option_a: Had he relied on God the host of Syria would have been delivered into his hand, but now he has wars instead
adults.option_b: He could have had peace with all nations but now only Syria is his enemy
adults.option_c: He could have been wealthier than Solomon but now faces poverty
adults.option_d: He could have conquered Baasha completely but now Baasha is stronger
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 16:7 — "Because thou hast relied on the king of Syria, and not relied on the LORD thy God, therefore is the host of the king of Syria escaped out of thine hand."

adults.question: What was the manner of Asa's burial and the honors given to him at his death?
adults.option_a: He was buried in his own sepulchres in the city of David and they laid him on a bed of spices with a very great burning of fire made in his honour
adults.option_b: He was buried without ceremony because of his failures
adults.option_c: He was buried in the valley of Kidron outside the city
adults.option_d: He was buried alongside his father Abijah in a shared tomb
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 16:14 — "And they buried him in his own sepulchres, which he had made for himself in the city of David, and laid him in the bed which was filled with sweet odours and divers kinds of spices prepared by the apothecaries' art: and they made a very great burning for him."

adults.question: How does the structure of Asa's reign in chapters 14-16 demonstrate the Chronicler's theological pattern of testing?
adults.option_a: Asa's early seeking of God brings victory and peace; his later failure to seek God brings prophet rebuke, war, and disease — showing blessing follows faithfulness and failure follows self-reliance
adults.option_b: The Chronicler shows Asa as uniformly righteous throughout his reign
adults.option_c: The Chronicler focuses only on the political failures without theological commentary
adults.option_d: The Chronicler blames Baasha for all of Asa's problems
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 14:11 compared to 16:7 — the same verb "rely on the LORD" used positively in ch.14 and negatively in ch.16 creates a deliberate contrast.

adults.question: What does Asa's imprisonment of Hanani suggest about the spiritual decline of his later years?
adults.option_a: Asa had moved from a king who sought God and accepted divine guidance to one who silenced prophetic critique, showing hardening of heart — a pattern the Chronicler shows leads to judgment
adults.option_b: Asa was testing the prophet's credentials before accepting his message
adults.option_c: The imprisonment was later rescinded showing Asa's eventual repentance
adults.option_d: Hanani had spoken presumptuously without proper credentials
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 16:10 — "Then Asa was wroth with the seer, and put him in a prison house; for he was in a rage with him," contrasting with his earlier reception of Azariah's prophecy in chapter 15.

kids.question: Who became king of Judah after Asa?
kids.explanation: 2 Chronicles 17:1 — "And Jehoshaphat his son reigned in his stead, and strengthened himself against Israel."

kids.question: What did Jehoshaphat send throughout all the cities of Judah to teach the people?
kids.option_a: Princes and Levites and priests with the book of the law of the LORD
kids.option_b: Soldiers to collect taxes for the temple
kids.option_c: Builders to repair the city walls
kids.option_d: Singers to lead worship in every town
kids.explanation: 2 Chronicles 17:7-9 — "He sent to his princes...And with them he sent Levites...And they taught in Judah, and had the book of the law of the LORD with them."

kids.question: Why did God establish Jehoshaphat's kingdom?
kids.option_a: Because he walked in the first ways of his father David and sought the LORD
kids.option_b: Because he had the biggest army in all of Judah
kids.option_c: Because he rebuilt the temple from the ground up
kids.option_d: Because he made peace with all the surrounding nations
kids.explanation: 2 Chronicles 17:3-4 — "And the LORD was with Jehoshaphat, because he walked in the first ways of his father David...but sought to the LORD God of his father."

teens.question: What did Jehoshaphat remove from Judah in his religious reforms?
teens.option_a: The high places and groves out of Judah
teens.option_b: All the foreign merchants from Jerusalem
teens.option_c: The Baal temples that Asa had not destroyed
teens.option_d: The non-Levitical priests from the temple service
teens.explanation: 2 Chronicles 17:6 — "And his heart was lifted up in the ways of the LORD: moreover he took away the high places and groves out of Judah."

teens.question: What was the purpose of sending the teaching mission throughout Judah in the third year of Jehoshaphat's reign?
teens.option_a: To teach the people the book of the law of the LORD in all the cities of Judah
teens.option_b: To collect the temple tax from every household
teens.option_c: To recruit soldiers for the expanding army
teens.option_d: To register all the priests and Levites in a census
teens.explanation: 2 Chronicles 17:9 — "And they taught in Judah, and had the book of the law of the LORD with them, and went about throughout all the cities of Judah, and taught the people."

teens.question: What effect did Jehoshaphat's early reign have on the surrounding nations?
teens.option_a: The fear of the LORD fell upon all the kingdoms of the lands round about Judah and they made no war against him
teens.option_b: They all paid tribute to Jehoshaphat after his first military campaign
teens.option_c: They formed an alliance against him but were defeated
teens.option_d: They sent ambassadors asking to learn about Israel's God
teens.explanation: 2 Chronicles 17:10 — "And the fear of the LORD fell upon all the kingdoms of the lands that were round about Judah, so that they made no war against Jehoshaphat."

teens.question: What did the Philistines and Arabians bring to Jehoshaphat as tribute?
teens.option_a: The Philistines brought presents and silver and the Arabians brought him rams and he-goats
teens.option_b: Both nations sent cedar timber and spices
teens.option_c: Gold and precious stones from their mines
teens.option_d: Grain and oil from their harvests
teens.explanation: 2 Chronicles 17:11 — "Also some of the Philistines brought Jehoshaphat presents, and tribute silver; and the Arabians brought him flocks, seven thousand and seven hundred rams, and seven thousand and seven hundred he goats."

teens.question: How large was Jehoshaphat's army according to the detailed military census in this chapter?
teens.option_a: Over one million forty thousand armed men from Judah and Benjamin combined under five commanders
teens.option_b: Five hundred thousand men in total
teens.option_c: Three hundred thousand chosen warriors
teens.option_d: Eight hundred thousand men ready for battle

adults.question: What was the specific contrast the Chronicler drew between Jehoshaphat and his father Asa in terms of religious practice?
adults.option_a: Jehoshaphat walked in the first ways of his father David and sought not Baalim but the LORD God of his father, not walking after Israel's doings
adults.option_b: Jehoshaphat completed the reforms Asa had begun but failed to finish
adults.option_c: Jehoshaphat surpassed Asa by removing all the high places
adults.option_d: Jehoshaphat restored the Levitical music that Asa had neglected
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 17:3-4 — "He walked in the first ways of his father David...For he sought to the LORD God of his father, and walked in his commandments, and not after the doings of Israel."

adults.question: Who were the five princes sent on the teaching mission and how many Levites and priests accompanied them?
adults.option_a: Five princes (Benhail, Obadiah, Zechariah, Nethaneel, Michaiah) with nine Levites and two priests — Elishama and Jehoram
adults.option_b: Three princes with twelve Levites and four priests
adults.option_c: Seven princes with twenty Levites and the high priest
adults.option_d: Ten princes with thirty Levites and six priests
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 17:7-8 — "Also in the third year of his reign he sent to his princes, even to Benhail, and to Obadiah, and to Zechariah, and to Nethaneel, and to Michaiah, to teach in the cities of Judah. And with them he sent Levites...and with them Elishama and Jehoram, priests."

adults.question: What does the sending of the Book of the Law with the teaching mission suggest about Jehoshaphat's view of Torah in national life?
adults.option_a: He regarded the written law as the authoritative basis for national reform — teaching Scripture systematically to all citizens, not just religious specialists
adults.option_b: The book was sent primarily for the priests to study privately
adults.option_c: The law was used mainly for legal disputes and property cases
adults.option_d: The teaching mission was primarily about temple rituals and sacrifices
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 17:9 — "And they taught in Judah, and had the book of the law of the LORD with them, and went about throughout all the cities of Judah, and taught the people."

adults.question: What was the administrative structure of Jehoshaphat's military, and what does the detail reveal about his reign?
adults.option_a: Five commanders over five divisions from Judah (Adnah, Jehohanan, Amasiah) and Benjamin (Eliada, Jehozabad) with specific troop numbers for each — showing a well-organized professional military
adults.option_b: A single general over an undifferentiated mass army
adults.option_c: A rotating command system changing every three years
adults.option_d: Tribal militias without central command structure
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 17:14-18 — the five named commanders with precise troop totals shows systematic military organization.

adults.question: What did it mean that Amasiah "willingly offered himself unto the LORD" according to verse 16?
adults.option_a: He volunteered his service as an act of devotion rather than being conscripted — indicating a class of dedicated military volunteers within Jehoshaphat's forces
adults.option_b: He offered sacrifices at the temple before each battle
adults.option_c: He pledged his personal wealth to fund the army
adults.option_d: He had taken a Nazirite vow of military dedication
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 17:16 — "And next him was Amasiah the son of Zichri, who willingly offered himself unto the LORD; and with him two hundred thousand mighty men of valour."

adults.question: What was the theological significance of the fear of the LORD falling on surrounding nations rather than Jehoshaphat winning military campaigns?
adults.option_a: God protected Judah supernaturally through his own reputation, making wars unnecessary — showing divine sovereignty working through awe rather than requiring human military action
adults.option_b: The surrounding nations were simply too weak to challenge Judah
adults.option_c: Jehoshaphat's diplomacy rather than God's action kept the peace
adults.option_d: The peace was fragile and would soon end
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 17:10 — "And the fear of the LORD fell upon all the kingdoms of the lands that were round about Judah, so that they made no war against Jehoshaphat."

adults.question: How does the Chronicler's portrait of Jehoshaphat in chapter 17 set up the contrast with his alliance with Ahab in chapter 18?
adults.option_a: Chapter 17 establishes Jehoshaphat as a faithful, prosperous king separated from Israel's corruption — making his alliance with the apostate Ahab in chapter 18 a surprising and theologically significant failure
adults.option_b: Chapter 17 shows Jehoshaphat was already inclined toward compromise
adults.option_c: The Chronicler presents chapters 17-18 as unrelated events in Jehoshaphat's reign
adults.option_d: Chapter 17 is included only for its military census data
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 17:6 — "His heart was lifted up in the ways of the LORD," contrasting sharply with his behavior in 18:3: "I am as thou art, and my people as thy people."

adults.question: What does it mean that Jehoshaphat's heart was "lifted up in the ways of the LORD" — how does this phrase function in Chronicles?
adults.option_a: It is a positive description meaning his heart was exalted by and directed toward God's ways, indicating wholehearted devotion — contrasting with kings whose hearts were lifted up in pride against God
adults.option_b: It is a warning sign of approaching pride and failure
adults.option_c: It refers specifically to his elevation of temple worship
adults.option_d: It is a formula meaning he achieved military success
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 17:6 — "And his heart was lifted up in the ways of the LORD: moreover he took away the high places and groves out of Judah," showing the phrase as motivational for reform.

kids.question: Which king of Israel did Jehoshaphat make an alliance with by marriage?
kids.explanation: 2 Chronicles 18:1 — "Now Jehoshaphat had riches and honour in abundance, and joined affinity with Ahab."

kids.question: What did the prophet Micaiah say he would only speak?
kids.option_b: Only good things about the king
kids.option_c: The words of the other prophets
kids.option_d: Nothing at all to the king
kids.explanation: 2 Chronicles 18:13 — "And Micaiah said, As the LORD liveth, even what my God saith, that will I speak."

kids.question: What happened to Ahab in the battle against Syria even though he disguised himself?
kids.option_a: A man shot an arrow at random and it struck him between the joints of his armour
kids.option_b: He was captured by the Syrian king
kids.option_c: He fell off his chariot and was trampled
kids.option_d: He was killed by one of his own soldiers by mistake
kids.explanation: 2 Chronicles 18:33 — "And a certain man drew a bow at a venture, and smote the king of Israel between the joints of the harness."

teens.question: What did the four hundred prophets of Ahab tell him about going to battle at Ramoth-gilead?
teens.option_a: Go up, for God will deliver it into the king's hand
teens.option_b: Do not go — you will be defeated and killed
teens.option_c: Wait for a sign from heaven before attacking
teens.option_d: Make peace with Syria first
teens.explanation: 2 Chronicles 18:5 — "And the prophets said, Go up; for God will deliver it into the king's hand."

teens.question: What vision did Micaiah describe of Israel after the battle?
teens.option_a: All Israel scattered upon the mountains as sheep that have no shepherd
teens.option_b: Jerusalem in flames and the temple destroyed
teens.option_c: The king's body lying in the valley of Jezreel
teens.option_d: The armies of Israel fleeing to Egypt
teens.explanation: 2 Chronicles 18:16 — "Then he said, I did see all Israel scattered upon the mountains, as sheep that have no shepherd."

teens.question: What did Micaiah see in his vision of the heavenly court regarding the false prophets?
teens.option_a: A lying spirit in the mouth of all Ahab's prophets sent by God to entice Ahab
teens.option_b: Angels of God filling the prophets with confusion
teens.option_c: The spirit of Jezebel controlling the court prophets
teens.option_d: Satan himself speaking through the false prophets
teens.explanation: 2 Chronicles 18:21-22 — "And the LORD said unto him, Wherewith? And he said, I will go out, and be a lying spirit in the mouth of all his prophets...the LORD hath put a lying spirit in the mouth of these thy prophets."

teens.question: What did Zedekiah son of Chenaanah do to Micaiah after his prophecy?
teens.option_a: He smote him on the cheek and asked which way the spirit of the LORD went
teens.option_b: He called him a false prophet and demanded his execution
teens.option_c: He challenged him to a public debate before the king
teens.option_d: He pushed him out of the king's presence
teens.explanation: 2 Chronicles 18:23 — "Then Zedekiah the son of Chenaanah came near, and smote Micaiah upon the cheek, and said, Which way went the Spirit of the LORD from me to speak unto thee?"

teens.question: What was Ahab's strategy with Jehoshaphat for the battle — what disguise did they use?
teens.option_a: Ahab disguised himself but told Jehoshaphat to wear his royal robes
teens.option_b: Both kings wore disguises so the enemy could not target them
teens.option_c: Jehoshaphat led the main army while Ahab commanded from the rear
teens.option_d: Ahab dressed as a common soldier and gave Jehoshaphat his chariot
teens.explanation: 2 Chronicles 18:29 — "And the king of Israel said unto Jehoshaphat, I will disguise myself, and will enter into the battle; but put thou on thy robes."

adults.question: What was the occasion that brought Jehoshaphat and Ahab together, and what did Ahab propose?
adults.option_a: Ahab held a great feast for Jehoshaphat and proposed they go together to recover Ramoth-gilead from Syria
adults.option_b: Jehoshaphat visited Samaria to negotiate a trade agreement
adults.option_c: Ahab came to Jerusalem seeking military assistance against Moab
adults.option_d: Both kings gathered to divide the spoil from a previous battle
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 18:2 — "And after certain years he went down to Ahab to Samaria. And Ahab killed sheep and oxen for him in abundance, and for the people that he had with him, and persuaded him to go up with him to Ramoth-gilead."

adults.question: What was the precise content of Micaiah's first ironic answer to Ahab, and why did Ahab not believe it?
adults.option_a: He said "Go ye up, and prosper" ironically — Ahab recognized this as sarcasm matching what the other prophets said and demanded he speak the truth
adults.option_b: Micaiah refused to answer at first
adults.option_c: Micaiah predicted defeat from the beginning without irony
adults.option_d: Micaiah spoke in parable and Ahab could not understand
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 18:14-15 — "And when he was come to the king, the king said unto him, Micaiah, shall we go to Ramoth-gilead to battle?...And he said, Go ye up, and prosper...And the king said to him, How many times shall I adjure thee that thou say nothing but the truth to me?"

adults.question: What specifically was Micaiah's message from the LORD about Ahab's fate?
adults.option_a: The LORD said these have no master, let them return every man to his house in peace — indicating Ahab would die in battle
adults.option_b: God said Ahab would be wounded but recover if he repented
adults.option_c: The LORD told Micaiah Ahab would be captured and ransomed
adults.option_d: God decreed Ahab would reign ten more years as punishment for Israel
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 18:16 — "Then he said, I did see all Israel scattered upon the mountains, as sheep that have no shepherd: and the LORD said, These have no master; let them return therefore every man to his house in peace."

adults.question: What was Ahab's response to Micaiah's true prophecy, and what does it reveal about his character?
adults.option_a: He had Micaiah put in prison on bread and water until his safe return — showing he rejected truth and trusted his own plans over God's word
adults.option_b: He cancelled the battle after hearing the prophecy
adults.option_c: He publicly debated Micaiah's credentials before the court
adults.option_d: He sent Micaiah to Jehoshaphat for a second opinion
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 18:25-26 — "Then the king of Israel said...Put this fellow in the prison, and feed him with bread of affliction and with water of affliction, until I return in peace."

adults.question: What was Micaiah's parting word that showed his confidence in his prophecy?
adults.option_a: If thou certainly return in peace, then hath not the LORD spoken by me — affirming God's word over the king's optimism
adults.option_b: He said he would be proven right within three days
adults.option_c: He warned the prison guards they would be judged for obeying the king
adults.option_d: He called the whole court to witness that his words were true
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 18:27 — "And Micaiah said, If thou certainly return in peace, then hath not the LORD spoken by me. And he said, Hearken, all ye people."

adults.question: Why did the Syrian commanders initially turn away from Jehoshaphat during the battle, and what saved him?
adults.option_a: They thought he was the king of Israel but turned away when they realized he was not; Jehoshaphat cried out and the LORD helped him
adults.option_b: Jehoshaphat's bodyguards fought off the Syrian commanders successfully
adults.option_c: God sent confusion into the Syrian camp at that moment
adults.option_d: The Syrian king called off the attack to pursue Ahab instead
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 18:31-32 — "And it came to pass...the captains of the chariots saw Jehoshaphat...Therefore they compassed about him to fight: but Jehoshaphat cried out, and the LORD helped him; and God moved them to depart from him."

adults.question: How did Ahab die, and what happened to his blood?
adults.option_a: He was wounded by the random arrow, propped up in his chariot until evening, and died at sunset; his blood was washed from the chariot in Samaria
adults.option_b: He died immediately when struck and was carried from the field
adults.option_c: He survived the battle but died of his wounds a week later
adults.option_d: His blood was carried back to Ramoth-gilead for burial
