adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 18:33-34 — "And a certain man drew a bow at a venture, and smote the king of Israel between the joints of the harness...and about the time of the sun going down he died."

adults.question: What does the story of Micaiah in chapter 18 contribute to the Chronicler's broader theology of prophecy and kingship?
adults.option_a: It establishes that true prophets speak God's word regardless of royal displeasure, and that ignoring true prophecy leads to death — Ahab's fate becoming a warning for all kings who prefer flattery over truth
adults.option_b: The story is included purely for its historical interest in synchronizing Judah and Israel's histories
adults.option_c: The Chronicler uses it to show that Jehoshaphat was innocent of Ahab's sins
adults.option_d: The story is primarily about the mechanics of divine deception in battle
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 18:22 — "The LORD hath put a lying spirit in the mouth of these thy prophets, and the LORD hath spoken evil against thee," showing the deadly consequence of rejecting prophetic truth.

kids.question: Who came to meet Jehoshaphat when he returned home from the battle with Ahab?
kids.option_a: Jehu the son of Hanani the seer
kids.explanation: 2 Chronicles 19:2 — "And Jehu the son of Hanani the seer went out to meet him."

kids.question: What did Jehoshaphat do among the people after being rebuked, going from Beersheba to Mount Ephraim?
kids.option_a: He brought them back unto the LORD God of their fathers
kids.option_b: He collected taxes to rebuild the army
kids.option_c: He built new cities in the wilderness
kids.option_d: He made peace treaties with all the kings
kids.explanation: 2 Chronicles 19:4 — "And Jehoshaphat dwelt at Jerusalem: and he went out again through the people from Beersheba to mount Ephraim, and brought them back unto the LORD God of their fathers."

kids.question: What did Jehoshaphat tell the judges he appointed — whose judgment were they carrying out?
kids.explanation: 2 Chronicles 19:6 — "And said to the judges, Take heed what ye do: for ye judge not for man, but for the LORD, who is with you in the judgment."

teens.question: What rebuke did Jehu give Jehoshaphat about his alliance with Ahab?
teens.option_a: Shouldest thou help the ungodly and love them that hate the LORD? — wrath of the LORD was upon him for this
teens.option_b: He said Jehoshaphat had broken the covenant of salt with David's house
teens.option_c: He warned Jehoshaphat that Syria would now attack Judah
teens.option_d: He told him the temple treasury must pay a penalty for the sin
teens.explanation: 2 Chronicles 19:2 — "Shouldest thou help the ungodly, and love them that hate the LORD? therefore is wrath upon thee from before the LORD."

teens.question: What saved Jehoshaphat from complete condemnation despite the rebuke?
teens.option_a: There were good things found in him — he had taken away the groves from the land and prepared his heart to seek God
teens.option_b: He immediately tore his garments and fasted for seven days
teens.option_c: He paid a large offering to the temple to atone for his sin
teens.option_d: He sent Jehu away without accepting the rebuke
teens.explanation: 2 Chronicles 19:3 — "Nevertheless there are good things found in thee, in that thou hast taken away the groves out of the land, and hast prepared thine heart to seek God."

teens.question: What specific warning about corruption did Jehoshaphat give the judges he appointed?
teens.option_a: Take heed and do it, for there is no iniquity with the LORD, no respect of persons, and no taking of gifts
teens.option_b: Never accept a bribe no matter how small
teens.option_c: Always seek the king's counsel before making major decisions
teens.option_d: Record every judgment in writing for the king's review
teens.explanation: 2 Chronicles 19:7 — "Wherefore now let the fear of the LORD be upon you; take heed and do it: for there is no iniquity with the LORD our God, nor respect of persons, nor taking of gifts."

teens.question: What court did Jehoshaphat set up in Jerusalem and who presided over it?
teens.option_a: A court for controversies with Amariah the chief priest over matters of the LORD and Zebadiah over the king's matters
teens.option_b: A supreme court of twelve elders from each tribe
teens.option_c: A military tribunal under the chief commander
teens.option_d: A priestly council that met only on feast days
teens.explanation: 2 Chronicles 19:8,11 — "Moreover in Jerusalem did Jehoshaphat set of the Levites, and of the priests...And, behold, Amariah the chief priest is over you in all matters of the LORD; and Zebadiah...for all the king's matters."

teens.question: What did Jehoshaphat charge those serving in the Jerusalem court to do as his closing exhortation?
teens.option_a: Deal courageously and the LORD shall be with the good
teens.option_b: Study the law day and night without ceasing
teens.option_c: Teach the people the statutes every sabbath
teens.option_d: Report all wrongdoers directly to the king
teens.explanation: 2 Chronicles 19:11 — "Deal courageously, and the LORD shall be with the good."

adults.question: What is the theological significance of Jehu's rebuke — "shouldest thou help the ungodly, and love them that hate the LORD?" — for the Chronicler's view of foreign alliances?
adults.option_a: It establishes the principle that political alliances with those who reject God constitute a form of spiritual complicity — the Chronicler uses this to critique any entanglement that compromises covenant loyalty
adults.option_b: The rebuke is purely political about treaty obligations to the house of David
adults.option_c: Jehu is only concerned with Jehoshaphat's military strategy
adults.option_d: The rebuke is about specific terms of the alliance, not its existence
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 19:2 — "Shouldest thou help the ungodly, and love them that hate the LORD?" establishing the principle of covenant-based foreign policy.

adults.question: What does the phrase "good things found in thee" show about the Chronicler's approach to moral evaluation of kings?
adults.option_a: The Chronicler evaluates kings by their overall pattern of seeking God, allowing that even flawed kings retain genuine merit — avoiding simplistic all-or-nothing moral verdicts
adults.option_b: The Chronicler applies a point-based system for righteousness
adults.option_c: Good things only matter if they outweigh the bad deeds in number
adults.option_d: The phrase is a diplomatic softening before the real condemnation
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 19:3 — "Nevertheless there are good things found in thee," showing the Chronicler's nuanced moral assessment of Jehoshaphat.

adults.question: What does Jehoshaphat's appointment of judges in every fortified city reveal about his administrative program?
adults.option_a: He was implementing a systematic judicial reform extending accountability and fair legal process to every city of Judah — a major institutional achievement
adults.option_b: He was primarily trying to increase tax revenue through better administration
adults.option_c: The appointment was a ceremonial honor with no practical function
adults.option_d: He was trying to reduce the power of the Levites by creating rival courts
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 19:5 — "And he set judges in the land throughout all the fenced cities of Judah, city by city."

adults.question: What was the division of jurisdiction between Amariah and Zebadiah in Jerusalem's central court?
adults.option_a: Amariah handled all matters pertaining to the LORD (religious/priestly law) while Zebadiah handled matters pertaining to the king (civil/administrative law)
adults.option_b: Both had equal jurisdiction over all cases regardless of type
adults.option_c: Amariah handled northern cases and Zebadiah handled southern cases
adults.option_d: Zebadiah handled criminal cases and Amariah handled civil cases
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 19:11 — "Amariah the chief priest is over you in all matters of the LORD; and Zebadiah the son of Ishmael, the ruler of the house of Judah, for all the king's matters."

adults.question: What was the role of the Levites specifically in the Jerusalem court system?
adults.option_a: They were to be officers before the court dealing in all controversies
adults.option_b: They were to record and archive all legal judgments
adults.option_c: They served as advocates speaking on behalf of the poor
adults.option_d: They were responsible for carrying out sentences
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 19:11 — "Also the Levites shall be officers before you. Deal courageously."

adults.question: How does Jehoshaphat's charge to the judges parallel Moses' instructions for judges in Deuteronomy?
adults.option_a: Both call for impartial judgment without respect of persons or bribery, and both ground judicial authority in God's own character as the ultimate judge of all
adults.option_b: Jehoshaphat's system is entirely different from Moses' design
adults.option_c: Jehoshaphat added requirements Moses had not included
adults.option_d: The parallel is coincidental — Jehoshaphat devised the system independently
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 19:7 — "There is no iniquity with the LORD our God, nor respect of persons, nor taking of gifts," echoing Deuteronomy 16:19: "Thou shalt not wrest judgment; thou shalt not respect persons, neither take a gift."

adults.question: What does Jehoshaphat's post-rebuke behavior — going throughout the people to bring them back to God — suggest about repentance in the Chronicler's framework?
adults.option_a: True repentance leads to active reform — Jehoshaphat does not just feel remorse but takes concrete action to rectify the spiritual condition of the nation
adults.option_b: The tour was a political move to shore up popular support after his embarrassing return from battle
adults.option_c: Jehoshaphat's repentance was purely internal and had no external expression
adults.option_d: The reform tour was mandated by the prophets as a specific penance
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 19:4 — "He went out again through the people from Beersheba to mount Ephraim, and brought them back unto the LORD God of their fathers."

adults.question: What is the significance of "the LORD shall be with the good" as the closing charge to the judges?
adults.option_a: It grounds judicial integrity in divine presence — right judgment attracts God's help, making divine partnership the reward for and companion of righteous judgment
adults.option_b: It is a motivational slogan with no theological depth
adults.option_c: It implies that corrupt judges will lose military battles
adults.option_d: It is a promise that righteous judges will be promoted in rank
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 19:11 — "Deal courageously, and the LORD shall be with the good," making divine presence contingent on right conduct.

kids.question: Which nations came against Jehoshaphat in a great army?
kids.option_a: Moab, Ammon, and the children of Seir
kids.explanation: 2 Chronicles 20:1 — "It came to pass after this also, that the children of Moab, and the children of Ammon, and with them other beside the Ammonites, came against Jehoshaphat to battle."

kids.question: What did Jehoshaphat proclaim throughout all of Judah when he heard the enemies were coming?
kids.explanation: 2 Chronicles 20:3 — "And Jehoshaphat feared, and set himself to seek the LORD, and proclaimed a fast throughout all Judah."

kids.question: Who did Jehoshaphat put at the very front of the army to go out before the soldiers?
kids.option_b: Priests with the ark of the covenant
kids.option_c: Elders carrying the law of Moses
kids.explanation: 2 Chronicles 20:21 — "And when he had consulted with the people, he appointed singers unto the LORD, and that should praise the beauty of holiness, as they went out before the army."

teens.question: What was the main point of Jehoshaphat's prayer before the congregation in the temple court?
teens.option_a: He confessed Judah had no might against the great company, their eyes were upon God, and declared "we know not what to do but our eyes are upon thee"
teens.option_b: He listed all of Israel's past victories as proof God would help again
teens.option_c: He promised to build a new altar if God gave them victory
teens.option_d: He asked God to show a miraculous sign before the battle
teens.explanation: 2 Chronicles 20:12 — "O our God, wilt thou not judge them? for we have no might against this great company that cometh against us; neither know we what to do: but our eyes are upon thee."

teens.question: Which Levite gave the prophecy "the battle is not yours, but God's"?
teens.option_a: Jahaziel the son of Zechariah
teens.option_b: Amariah the chief priest
teens.option_c: Azariah the son of Oded
teens.option_d: Hanani the seer
teens.explanation: 2 Chronicles 20:14-15 — "Then upon Jahaziel the son of Zechariah...came the Spirit of the LORD...And he said...Thus saith the LORD unto you, Be not afraid nor dismayed by reason of this great multitude; for the battle is not yours, but God's."

teens.question: What were the people of Judah to do the next day according to God's promise through Jahaziel?
teens.option_a: Go down against them, stand still, and see the salvation of the LORD — they would not need to fight
teens.option_b: Attack at dawn and God would send confusion into the enemy camp
teens.option_c: Surround the enemy from three sides while priests prayed
teens.option_d: March to the valley and God would cause an earthquake
teens.explanation: 2 Chronicles 20:17 — "Ye shall not need to fight in this battle: set yourselves, stand ye still, and see the salvation of the LORD with you...for the LORD will be with you."

teens.question: What happened among the enemies that destroyed them without Judah fighting?
teens.option_a: The LORD set ambushments against them and the enemy nations turned on one another and destroyed each other
teens.option_b: God sent fire from heaven to consume their armies
teens.option_c: A great earthquake swallowed their camp overnight
teens.option_d: Disease struck their army and they were too weak to fight
teens.explanation: 2 Chronicles 20:22-23 — "When they began to sing and to praise, the LORD set ambushments against the children of Ammon, Moab, and mount Seir...For the children of Ammon and Moab stood up against the inhabitants of mount Seir...and when they had made an end of the inhabitants of Seir, every one helped to destroy another."

teens.question: What was the valley called where Judah blessed the LORD after the battle, and why?
teens.option_a: Berachah, meaning blessing, because they blessed the LORD there
teens.option_b: Jehoshaphat, meaning the LORD judges
teens.option_c: Zephathah, where Asa had fought before
teens.option_d: Achor, meaning trouble turned to hope
teens.explanation: 2 Chronicles 20:26 — "And on the fourth day they assembled themselves in the valley of Berachah; for there they blessed the LORD: therefore the name of the same place was called, The valley of Berachah, unto this day."

adults.question: What was the historical and theological argument Jehoshaphat made about Israel's past relationship with the nations now attacking?
adults.option_a: He argued that God had commanded Israel not to invade these nations when coming from Egypt, yet they now repaid Israel with invasion — so God must judge this injustice
adults.option_b: He appealed to the Abrahamic covenant as the sole basis for protection
adults.option_c: He listed Solomon's treaties with surrounding nations that were being violated
adults.option_d: He argued that God's honor among all nations required him to act
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 20:10-11 — "And now, behold, the children of Ammon and Moab and mount Seir, whom thou wouldest not let Israel invade...behold, I say, how they reward us, to come to cast us out of thy possession."

adults.question: What specific description of God's sovereign power did Jehoshaphat invoke at the opening of his prayer?
adults.option_a: Art thou not God in heaven? and rulest not thou over all the kingdoms of the heathen? and in thine hand is there not power and might, so that none is able to withstand thee?
adults.option_b: He praised God as the creator of heaven and earth and sea
adults.option_c: He called God the covenant keeper who had never failed Israel
adults.option_d: He described God as the one who destroyed Pharaoh's army in the sea
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 20:6 — "O LORD God of our fathers, art not thou God in heaven? and rulest not thou over all the kingdoms of the heathen? and in thine hand is there not power and might, so that none is able to withstand thee?"

adults.question: What does Jehoshaphat's reference to Solomon's temple dedication in his prayer reveal about his theological reasoning?
adults.option_a: He invoked the promise God made when Solomon dedicated the temple — that if they cried to God in time of trouble in that house, he would hear and save them
adults.option_b: He used the temple's physical glory as a reason God must protect Jerusalem
adults.option_c: He reminded God that Israel had paid greatly to build the temple
adults.option_d: He argued that enemies attacking Judah were effectively attacking God's house
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 20:8-9 — "They have built thee a sanctuary therein for thy name, saying, If, when evil cometh upon us...we stand before this house...and cry unto thee in our affliction, then thou wilt hear and help."

adults.question: What was the immediate corporate response to Jahaziel's prophecy before the battle even began?
adults.option_a: Jehoshaphat bowed with his face to the ground and all Judah and Jerusalem fell before the LORD worshipping him, and the Levites praised him with a loud voice
adults.option_b: They immediately armed themselves and marched out to battle
adults.option_c: They sent messengers to offer the enemies terms of peace
adults.option_d: They fasted again through the night in preparation for battle
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 20:18-19 — "And Jehoshaphat bowed his head with his face to the ground: and all Judah and the inhabitants of Jerusalem fell before the LORD, worshipping the LORD. And the Levites...stood up to praise the LORD God of Israel with a loud voice on high."

adults.question: How much spoil did Judah find when they came to the enemy camp, and how long did it take to gather it?
adults.option_a: More spoil than they could carry — riches, raiment, and precious jewels — and it took three days to gather it
adults.option_b: They found very little because the enemy had fled taking their possessions
adults.option_c: One day's worth of goods was divided equally among the soldiers
adults.option_d: They found weapons and armour but no treasure or jewels
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 20:25 — "And when Jehoshaphat and his people came to take away the spoil of them, they found among them in abundance both riches with the dead bodies, and precious jewels, which they stripped off for themselves, more than they could carry away: and they were three days in gathering of the spoil."

adults.question: What was the effect of this miraculous victory on the surrounding kingdoms?
adults.option_a: The fear of God was on all the kingdoms of those countries when they heard the LORD fought against the enemies of Israel
adults.option_b: All surrounding nations immediately submitted tribute to Jehoshaphat
adults.option_c: Egypt sent an embassy offering alliance after hearing the news
adults.option_d: The kingdom of northern Israel was humbled and offered peace
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 20:29 — "And the fear of God was on all the kingdoms of those countries, when they had heard that the LORD fought against the enemies of Israel."

adults.question: What was the failure at the end of the chapter involving Jehoshaphat and Ahaziah's joint shipping venture?
adults.option_a: He joined with Ahaziah king of Israel who did wickedly to make ships to go to Tarshish and Eliezer prophesied the ships would be broken — and they were
adults.option_b: He sent ships to Egypt against God's command and they sank in a storm
adults.option_c: He built a navy that sailed into pagan harbors and was condemned
adults.option_d: He used temple treasury funds to pay for the fleet without permission
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 20:35-37 — "And after this did Jehoshaphat king of Judah join himself with Ahaziah king of Israel, who did very wickedly...And Eliezer...prophesied against Jehoshaphat, saying, Because thou hast joined thyself with Ahaziah, the LORD hath broken thy works. And the ships were broken, that they were not able to go to Tarshish."

adults.question: What does chapter 20 establish as the supreme example of faith in Chronicles, and how does it function literarily in the book?
adults.option_a: It is the paradigmatic holy war narrative — radical dependence on God, corporate prayer, prophetic word, worship before battle, and miraculous victory — serving as the high point of Jehoshaphat's reign and a template for trusting God
adults.option_b: It is primarily a political account showing Judah's military recovery after Ramoth-gilead
adults.option_c: The chapter functions mainly as a warning about the dangers of the valley of Berachah
adults.option_d: The Chronicler uses it to demonstrate that music is the central act of temple worship
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 20:17 — "Ye shall not need to fight in this battle: set yourselves, stand ye still, and see the salvation of the LORD," making passive trust in God the central theological statement.

kids.question: Who became king of Judah after Jehoshaphat?
kids.explanation: 2 Chronicles 21:1 — "Now Jehoshaphat slept with his fathers, and was buried with his fathers in the city of David. And Jehoram his son reigned in his stead."

kids.question: What terrible thing did Jehoram do to his brothers when he became king?
kids.option_a: He killed all his brothers with the sword
kids.option_b: He sent them away to other countries
kids.option_c: He put them all in prison
kids.option_d: He took away all their wealth
kids.explanation: 2 Chronicles 21:4 — "Now when Jehoram was risen up to the kingdom of his father, he strengthened himself, and slew all his brethren with the sword."

kids.question: Which great prophet sent Jehoram a letter warning him of God's judgment?
kids.explanation: 2 Chronicles 21:12 — "And there came a writing to him from Elijah the prophet."

teens.question: What was the main reason Jehoram walked in evil ways according to the text?
teens.option_a: He had the daughter of Ahab as his wife who influenced him
teens.option_b: He was corrupted by the priests of Baal in Jerusalem
teens.option_c: His evil counselors led him astray from the beginning
teens.option_d: He was bitter about his father giving gifts to other sons
teens.explanation: 2 Chronicles 21:6 — "And he walked in the way of the kings of Israel, like as did the house of Ahab: for he had the daughter of Ahab to wife: and he wrought that which was evil in the eyes of the LORD."

teens.question: Why did God not destroy Judah despite Jehoram's great wickedness?
teens.option_a: Because of the covenant God had made with David to give a light to him and to his sons always
teens.option_b: Because the people of Judah themselves remained faithful to God
teens.option_c: Because Jehoshaphat had been so exceptionally righteous
teens.option_d: Because the temple still stood as a perpetual sign of God's presence
teens.explanation: 2 Chronicles 21:7 — "Howbeit the LORD would not destroy the house of David, because of the covenant that he had made with David, and as he promised to give a light to him and to his sons for ever."

teens.question: What nations revolted against Judah during Jehoram's reign?
teens.option_a: Edom revolted from under his hand and Libnah revolted also
teens.option_b: Moab and Ammon rose up against him
teens.option_c: Philistia and Arabia attacked Jerusalem
teens.option_d: Syria and Assyria both invaded at once
teens.explanation: 2 Chronicles 21:8,10 — "In his days the Edomites revolted from under the dominion of Judah...Also did Libnah revolt at the same time from under his hand."

teens.question: What did Elijah's letter say would happen to Jehoram personally as punishment?
teens.option_a: He would be smitten with a great plague and his bowels would fall out by reason of the sickness day by day
teens.option_b: He would be taken captive to Babylon
teens.option_c: He would be killed in battle by the Philistines
teens.option_d: A great fire would consume his palace in Jerusalem
teens.explanation: 2 Chronicles 21:14-15 — "Behold, with a great plague will the LORD smite thy people...And thou shalt have great sickness by disease of thy bowels, until thy bowels fall out by reason of the sickness day by day."

teens.question: How did Jehoram die and what dishonor was shown at his burial?
teens.option_a: He died of a bowel disease and his people made no burning for him — he departed without being desired and was not buried in the sepulchres of the kings
teens.option_b: He was killed in battle and buried with great honors
teens.option_c: He was assassinated by his own servants and buried secretly
teens.option_d: He died of old age in peace and was buried in the city of David
teens.explanation: 2 Chronicles 21:19-20 — "His bowels fell out by reason of his sickness: so he died of sore diseases...his people made no burning for him...and he departed without being desired."

adults.question: What high places did Jehoram build and what spiritual effect did they have on the nation?
adults.option_a: He made high places in the mountains of Judah and caused the inhabitants of Jerusalem to commit fornication and compelled Judah thereto
adults.option_b: He rebuilt the temples that Asa had destroyed decades earlier
adults.option_c: He introduced Baal worship into the Jerusalem temple itself
adults.option_d: He set up golden calves like those at Bethel and Dan in the north
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 21:11 — "Moreover he made high places in the mountains of Judah, and caused the inhabitants of Jerusalem to commit fornication, and compelled Judah thereto."

adults.question: What was the content of Elijah's letter regarding Jehoram's religious sins before naming the punishment?
adults.option_a: He charged Jehoram with not walking in the ways of Jehoshaphat or Asa but walking like the house of Ahab, and killing his brothers who were better than him
adults.option_b: Elijah primarily charged him with building the high places throughout the land
adults.option_c: The letter focused on his mistreatment of the Levites and priests
adults.option_d: Elijah condemned his treaty with the Philistines and Arabians
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 21:12-13 — "Thus saith the LORD God of David thy father, Because thou hast not walked in the ways of Jehoshaphat thy father, nor in the ways of Asa king of Judah...and also hast slain thy brethren of thy father's house."

adults.question: What Philistine and Arabian attack occurred during Jehoram's reign and what did they take?
adults.option_a: They came against Judah and carried away all the substance found in the king's house and his sons and his wives — only Jehoahaz the youngest son was left
adults.option_b: They attacked two border cities and carried away livestock only
adults.option_c: They besieged Jerusalem for six months and were paid off with tribute
adults.option_d: They raided southern Judah and returned without entering Jerusalem
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 21:16-17 — "Moreover the LORD stirred up against Jehoram the spirit of the Philistines, and of the Arabians...And they came up into Judah, and brake into it, and carried away all the substance that was found in the king's house, and his sons also, and his wives."

adults.question: What does the phrase "he departed without being desired" communicate about Jehoram's legacy?
adults.option_a: It is a uniquely harsh epitaph — his reign was so destructive and despised that even his death provoked no mourning, the opposite of the honored royal burial
adults.option_b: It means he died in exile away from his people and capital
adults.option_c: It refers to the fact that no one asked for his body to be returned
adults.option_d: It is a formula the Chronicler uses for all wicked kings without distinction
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 21:20 — "Thirty and two years old was he when he began to reign, and he reigned in Jerusalem eight years, and departed without being desired: notwithstanding they buried him in the city of David, but not in the sepulchres of the kings."

adults.question: What was the significance of not burying Jehoram in the sepulchres of the kings?
adults.option_a: It was a public act of condemnation that separated him from the honored line of Davidic kings and reinforced his reputation as a failed king
adults.option_b: There was simply no room remaining in the royal tombs
adults.option_c: The priests refused to allow burial in sacred ground because of his disease
adults.option_d: His body was ceremonially unclean due to the nature of his bowel disease
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 21:20 — "They buried him in the city of David, but not in the sepulchres of the kings," contrasting with the honored burials of faithful kings like Asa and Jehoshaphat.

adults.question: How does the Davidic covenant function in chapter 21 as a restraint on God's judgment of the entire dynasty?
adults.option_a: God chooses not to destroy the house of David entirely despite Jehoram's wickedness because of the eternal covenant — showing that God's promises outlast human faithlessness, though they do not prevent personal judgment
adults.option_b: The covenant guarantees prosperity to all Davidic kings regardless of their behavior
adults.option_c: The covenant only protects the land of Israel, not the king personally
adults.option_d: God delays judgment here but eventually cancels the Davidic covenant entirely
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 21:7 — "Howbeit the LORD would not destroy the house of David, because of the covenant that he had made with David," showing covenant mercy operating alongside retributive justice.

adults.question: What does Elijah's letter to Jehoram suggest about the extent of prophetic ministry in Chronicles?
adults.option_a: It shows prophets operating across kingdom boundaries through written correspondence — Elijah's letter to a Judahite king demonstrates that prophetic authority transcends political divisions
adults.option_b: Elijah had no authority over Judah and the letter was a personal opinion only
adults.option_c: The letter demonstrates that written prophecy was considered less authoritative than spoken prophecy
adults.option_d: Elijah could only write because he was no longer able to travel to Jerusalem in person
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 21:12 — "And there came a writing to him from Elijah the prophet, saying, Thus saith the LORD God of David thy father."

adults.question: How does Jehoram's reign serve as a negative foil to his father Jehoshaphat's in the Chronicler's narrative design?
adults.option_a: Every element is inverted: Jehoshaphat sought God, was honored, and died beloved; Jehoram forsook God, killed his brothers, suffered enemy raids, disease, and died unmourned — illustrating the Chronicler's two-ways theology
adults.option_b: The Chronicler presents them as equally wicked kings despite their different reputations
adults.option_c: Jehoram's story is simply a historical filler between the more important reigns
adults.option_d: The contrast is only about military success rather than spiritual character
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 21:6 compared to 17:3 — Jehoram "walked in the way of the kings of Israel" while Jehoshaphat "walked in the first ways of his father David."

kids.question: Who became king of Judah after Jehoram, because the Arabians had killed all the other sons?
kids.explanation: 2 Chronicles 22:1 — "And the inhabitants of Jerusalem made Ahaziah his youngest son king in his stead: for the band of men that came with the Arabians to the camp had slain all the eldest."

kids.question: Who seized the throne of Judah after Ahaziah was killed?
kids.explanation: 2 Chronicles 22:10 — "But when Athaliah the mother of Ahaziah saw that her son was dead, she arose and destroyed all the seed royal of the house of Judah."

kids.question: Who hid the baby prince Joash from Athaliah so she could not kill him?
kids.option_a: Jehoshabeath the daughter of the king and wife of Jehoiada the priest
kids.option_c: A Levite servant in the temple
kids.option_d: The royal nurse of the palace
kids.explanation: 2 Chronicles 22:11 — "But Jehoshabeath, the daughter of the king, took Joash the son of Ahaziah, and stole him from among the king's sons that were slain, and put him and his nurse in a bedchamber."

teens.question: Who was the chief counselor of Ahaziah's downfall according to the Chronicler?
teens.option_a: His mother Athaliah who was his counsellor to do wickedly
teens.option_b: The priests of Baal who surrounded the court
teens.option_c: The elders of Israel who had abandoned God
teens.option_d: Jehu who had previously been his ally
teens.explanation: 2 Chronicles 22:3 — "He also walked in the ways of the house of Ahab: for his mother was his counsellor to do wickedly."

teens.question: How did Ahaziah die — who killed him and where?
teens.option_a: Jehu killed him when he went to see wounded Joram king of Israel — he was found hiding in Samaria and brought to Jehu who slew him
teens.option_b: He was killed in battle against the Syrians at Ramoth-gilead
teens.option_c: The Philistines killed him on a raid into Judah
teens.option_d: He died of the same disease as his father Jehoram
teens.explanation: 2 Chronicles 22:9 — "And he sought Ahaziah: and they caught him, (for he was hid in Samaria,) and brought him to Jehu: and when they had slain him, they buried him."

teens.question: Why did they bury Ahaziah with honor even though he was wicked?
teens.option_a: Because they said he was the son of Jehoshaphat who sought the LORD with all his heart
teens.option_b: Because Jehoiada the priest interceded for his burial
teens.option_c: Because no one was willing to leave a king's body unburied
teens.option_d: Because his mother Athaliah demanded a royal funeral
teens.explanation: 2 Chronicles 22:9 — "And he sought Ahaziah...and brought him to Jehu: and when they had slain him, they buried him: Because, said they, he is the son of Jehoshaphat, who sought the LORD with all his heart."

teens.question: Where did Jehoshabeath hide the infant Joash for six years?
teens.option_a: In the house of God
teens.option_b: In the king's wine cellar
teens.option_c: In a cave outside Jerusalem
teens.option_d: In the house of a faithful Levite outside the city
teens.explanation: 2 Chronicles 22:12 — "And he was with them hid in the house of God six years: and Athaliah reigned over the land."

teens.question: What had happened to Ahaziah's power base that left no one with strength to hold his kingdom together?
teens.option_a: His destruction of the house of Ahab meant there was no one of the house of Ahaziah strong enough to retain the kingdom
teens.option_b: The army had been destroyed by Jehu's coup in Israel
teens.option_c: The treasury was empty after his father's reign
teens.option_d: The Levites had withdrawn their support from the royal house
teens.explanation: 2 Chronicles 22:9 — "So the house of Ahaziah had no power to keep still the kingdom."

adults.question: What was the theological explanation the Chronicler gave for Ahaziah's downfall?
adults.option_a: His destruction was of God since he had joined with Joram against Jehu whom the LORD had anointed to cut off the house of Ahab
adults.option_b: He was simply caught in the wrong place at the wrong time during Jehu's revolt
adults.option_c: His mother's counsel was the sole cause of his death
adults.option_d: God punished him because he had not repaired the temple
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 22:7 — "And the destruction of Ahaziah was of God by coming to Joram: for when he was come, he went out with Jehoram against Jehu the son of Nimshi, whom the LORD had anointed to cut off the house of Ahab."

adults.question: What does the Chronicler mean by saying Ahaziah's counselors were "the house of Ahab" — what theological point is being made?
adults.option_a: Ahaziah was so thoroughly under the influence of Ahab's evil dynasty through his mother that their counsel destroyed him — showing how corruption spreads through family alliances with the ungodly
adults.option_b: The phrase simply means he hired Israelite advisors for foreign policy
adults.option_c: The Chronicler is condemning the northern kingdom's entire administrative tradition
adults.option_d: It refers specifically to Jezebel's influence which continued through Athaliah
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 22:4 — "Wherefore he did evil in the sight of the LORD like the house of Ahab: for they were his counsellors after the death of his father to his destruction."

adults.question: What does Athaliah's action of destroying "all the seed royal of the house of Judah" represent as a threat in the Chronicler's framework?
adults.option_a: It nearly extinguished the Davidic line entirely — the one child hidden in the temple represents the thread by which God's covenant promise to David survived
adults.option_b: It was simply a political power grab with no theological significance
adults.option_c: It showed the weakness of the Davidic dynasty at that period
adults.option_d: Athaliah was fulfilling a judgment God had decreed on Jehoram's line
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 22:10 — "But when Athaliah the mother of Ahaziah saw that her son was dead, she arose and destroyed all the seed royal of the house of Judah," nearly ending the Davidic line.

adults.question: What is the significance of Jehoshabeath's identity as both the king's daughter and wife of the priest Jehoiada?
adults.option_a: Her dual position gave her both access to the royal children and the sanctuary of the temple — her marriage to the chief priest was the providential arrangement that allowed Joash to be hidden safely
adults.option_b: She is mentioned only to establish her legal right to act on behalf of the royal family
adults.option_c: Her priestly household connection is incidental to the narrative
adults.option_d: The Chronicler includes her name only because she was a woman of unusual bravery
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 22:11 — "But Jehoshabeath, the daughter of the king, took Joash...and put him and his nurse in a bedchamber. So Jehoshabeath, the daughter of king Jehoram, the wife of Jehoiada the priest...hid him from Athaliah."

adults.question: What does the six-year period of Athaliah's reign represent theologically in the Chronicler's narrative?
adults.option_a: It represents the darkest moment for the Davidic covenant — a usurping queen from Ahab's family on David's throne with the legitimate heir hidden — yet God preserves his promise even in this crisis
adults.option_b: It was a relatively stable period of political administration for Judah
adults.option_c: The six years represent a test of the Levites' faithfulness to the temple service
adults.option_d: It is a fulfillment of the curse Elijah pronounced on the house of Ahab
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 22:12 — "And he was with them hid in the house of God six years: and Athaliah reigned over the land," with the true king hidden even as the usurper sat on the throne.

adults.question: How does the account of Ahaziah's reign demonstrate the Chronicler's ongoing theme about alliances with the northern kingdom?
adults.option_a: Ahaziah's alliance with Joram of Israel and his dependence on Ahab's household for counsel leads directly to his destruction — reinforcing the pattern established with Jehoshaphat that aligning with the ungodly brings judgment
adults.option_b: The Chronicler shows that some northern alliances are acceptable if made for good reasons
adults.option_c: Ahaziah's problem was military rather than spiritual in the Chronicler's view
adults.option_d: The theme of northern alliances is only significant for kings who also build high places
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 22:7 — "The destruction of Ahaziah was of God by coming to Joram," linking his fate directly to his alliance with Israel's wicked king.

adults.question: How does the Chronicler's account of chapter 22 differ from the account in 2 Kings regarding Ahaziah's age?
adults.option_a: Chronicles says Ahaziah was forty-two years old but Kings says twenty-two — the Chronicles figure is likely a textual error since his father Jehoram died at forty
adults.option_b: Both accounts agree on Ahaziah's exact age of twenty-two
adults.option_c: Chronicles omits his age entirely while Kings gives the figure
adults.option_d: The discrepancy is explained by different methods of counting regnal years
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 22:2 — "Forty and two years old was Ahaziah when he began to reign," compared to 2 Kings 8:26 — "Two and twenty years old was Ahaziah when he began to reign."

adults.question: What does God's providential preservation of Joash through Jehoshabeath's courageous act reveal about divine sovereignty in the Chronicler's theology?
adults.option_a: God works through human courage and family loyalty to preserve his covenant purposes even when those purposes appear on the verge of total human destruction — providence operates through ordinary actors
adults.option_b: The rescue shows that the Davidic line was secure in itself regardless of human danger
adults.option_c: The Chronicler presents the rescue as primarily a human achievement requiring no divine action
adults.option_d: God's preservation of Joash was primarily about the temple rather than the Davidic covenant
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 22:11-12 — Jehoshabeath's act of hiding Joash in the house of God for six years preserving the covenant seed, showing God's purposes working through human faithfulness.

kids.question: Who organized the plan to make young Joash the rightful king?
kids.explanation: 2 Chronicles 23:1 — "And in the seventh year Jehoiada strengthened himself, and took the captains of hundreds, Azariah the son of Jeroham...and they went about in Judah."

kids.question: What happened to Athaliah when she heard the trumpets and shouts of the people crowning Joash king?
kids.option_a: She cried "Treason, Treason!" and was taken out of the temple and killed
kids.option_b: She fled to Egypt to escape
kids.option_d: She hid in the palace inner rooms
kids.explanation: 2 Chronicles 23:13,15 — "And when Athaliah heard the noise of the people running and praising the king, she came to the people...And she rent her clothes, and said, Treason, Treason...And they laid hands on her; and when she was come to the entering of the horse gate by the king's house, they slew her there."

kids.question: What did the people do to the temple of Baal and its priest after Joash was crowned?
kids.option_a: They broke it down and killed the priest Mattan before the altars
kids.option_c: They turned it into a storehouse for the temple
kids.explanation: 2 Chronicles 23:17 — "Then all the people went to the house of Baal, and brake it down, and brake his altars and his images in pieces, and slew Mattan the priest of Baal before the altars."

teens.question: What covenant did Jehoiada make when he crowned Joash, and between whom?
teens.option_a: A covenant between himself, the people, and the king that they would be the LORD's people
teens.option_b: A treaty between Judah and the northern tribes to reunite the kingdom
teens.option_c: A covenant between the new king and the army commanders
teens.option_d: A promise between the Levites and the priests to guard the temple
teens.explanation: 2 Chronicles 23:3 — "And all the congregation made a covenant with the king in the house of God. And he said unto them, Behold, the king's son shall reign, as the LORD hath said of the sons of David."

teens.question: What was the role of the Levites and the captains of hundreds in the temple during the coronation ceremony?
teens.option_a: The Levites surrounded the king with weapons, anyone entering the temple would be killed, and the priests handled the regular temple service
teens.option_b: They all stood outside the temple gates to guard the exits
teens.option_c: They formed two lines leading from the palace to the temple
teens.option_d: They stood on the walls of Jerusalem to signal the city
teens.explanation: 2 Chronicles 23:7 — "And the Levites shall compass the king round about, every man with his weapons in his hand; and whosoever else cometh into the house, he shall be put to death: but be ye with the king when he cometh in, and when he goeth out."

teens.question: What specific items were given to Joash when he was crowned?
teens.option_a: The crown and the testimony (the law) were put upon him
teens.option_b: A golden sceptre and the royal robes of David
teens.option_c: The sword of the LORD and the anointing oil
teens.option_d: A signet ring and the key of the house of David
teens.explanation: 2 Chronicles 23:11 — "Then they brought out the king's son, and put upon him the crown, and gave him the testimony, and made him king."

teens.question: What covenant did Jehoiada make after Athaliah was killed and the temple of Baal destroyed?
teens.option_a: A covenant between himself, all the people, and the king that they would be the LORD's people
teens.option_b: A treaty with the surrounding nations to recognize Joash as king
teens.option_c: A covenant to never again allow a foreign queen on the throne
teens.option_d: An agreement to hold an annual feast commemorating the restoration
teens.explanation: 2 Chronicles 23:16 — "And Jehoiada made a covenant between him, and between all the people, and between the king, that they should be the LORD's people."

teens.question: What offices did Jehoiada restore and reorganize in the temple after the coup?
teens.option_a: He appointed the offices of the house of the LORD under the Levitical priests whom David had divided, with singers and porters — as David had commanded
teens.option_b: He replaced all the former priests with new ones loyal to him
teens.option_c: He gave the Levites new duties not prescribed in the law of Moses
teens.option_d: He restored only the morning and evening offerings which had lapsed
teens.explanation: 2 Chronicles 23:18 — "Also Jehoiada appointed the offices of the house of the LORD by the hand of the priests the Levites, whom David had distributed in the house of the LORD, to offer the burnt offerings of the LORD, as it is written in the law of Moses."

adults.question: What was the precise military and political plan Jehoiada used to execute the coup, involving the Sabbath rotation of gatekeepers?
adults.option_a: He arranged that those coming on and going off the Sabbath duty should both remain — tripling the guard — with one third at the king's house, a third at the gate of the foundation, and a third at the gate behind the guard
adults.option_b: He waited until all the guards were changed simultaneously and struck in that moment
adults.option_c: He bribed the commanders of the army to support Joash
adults.option_d: He used the Passover celebration as cover for the gathering of armed men
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 23:4-5 — "This is the thing that ye shall do; A third part of you entering on the sabbath, of the priests and of the Levites, shall be porters of the doors; And a third part shall be at the king's house; and a third part at the gate of the foundation."

adults.question: What was the theological basis Jehoiada gave for the coup — how did he justify the action?
adults.option_a: Behold, the king's son shall reign as the LORD hath said of the sons of David — the coup was not rebellion but restoration of the divinely mandated Davidic succession
adults.option_b: He argued that Athaliah was a foreigner and therefore had no right to reign
adults.option_c: He presented it as fulfilling Elijah's prophecy against the house of Ahab
adults.option_d: He invoked the law of Moses which forbade women from ruling
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 23:3 — "And all the congregation made a covenant with the king in the house of God. And he said unto them, Behold, the king's son shall reign, as the LORD hath said of the sons of David."

adults.question: What does the giving of the testimony (the law) alongside the crown reveal about the Chronicler's concept of Davidic kingship?
adults.option_a: It shows that legitimate Davidic kingship is covenantally defined — the king receives divine law alongside royal authority, making his rule subject to and shaped by God's word from the moment of coronation
adults.option_b: The testimony was simply a ceremonial object with no practical significance
adults.option_c: The law was given to remind the young king to study it later when he was older
adults.option_d: It was a standard coronation ceremony that all kings underwent automatically
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 23:11 — "Then they brought out the king's son, and put upon him the crown, and gave him the testimony, and made him king," placing law and crown together as inseparable.

adults.question: What was the crowd's response when Joash was crowned and anointed — what was the atmosphere?
adults.option_a: They said "God save the king" and the people rejoiced and the city was quiet after Athaliah was slain with the sword
adults.option_b: They wept with joy but remained reverent and silent in the temple
adults.option_c: They shouted battle cries and marched to destroy the Baal temple immediately
adults.option_d: They praised God with instruments for three days continuously
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 23:11,13,21 — "And they said, God save the king...and all the people of the land rejoiced...So all the people of the land rejoiced, and the city was quiet, after that they had slain Athaliah with the sword."

adults.question: What does Jehoiada's careful instruction that only the priests and ministering Levites could enter the temple during the coronation reveal about his theology of sacred space?
adults.option_a: He maintained the distinction of sacred and common space even in a political crisis — the coup did not override the holiness code, showing that right worship and right action must coexist
adults.option_b: He was simply trying to limit the number of armed men in one place for security reasons
adults.option_c: The restriction on entry was purely a military security measure with no religious meaning
adults.option_d: He was protecting the secrecy of the plan from those who might warn Athaliah
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 23:6 — "But let none come into the house of the LORD, save the priests, and they that minister of the Levites; they shall go in, for they are holy: but all the people shall keep the watch of the LORD."

adults.question: What does the destruction of the Baal temple and killing of its priest Mattan represent in the Chronicler's reform narrative?
adults.option_a: It shows the coup was not merely political but covenantal — the restoration of Davidic rule necessarily entailed the elimination of Baal worship that Athaliah had promoted under the influence of Ahab's house
adults.option_b: The destruction was an act of popular mob vengeance with no theological direction
adults.option_c: Jehoiada destroyed the Baal temple primarily to remove a political rival institution
adults.option_d: The Baal temple was destroyed only because it had been built on royal property
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 23:17 — "Then all the people went to the house of Baal, and brake it down, and brake his altars and his images in pieces, and slew Mattan the priest of Baal before the altars."

adults.question: How does Jehoiada's restoration of the Davidic order of temple worship (citing David's arrangement) reinforce the Chronicler's broader theological program?
adults.option_a: The Chronicler consistently presents David's arrangement of temple worship as the normative standard — its restoration after Athaliah's usurpation confirms the coup as a return to legitimate divine order
adults.option_b: The citation of David is simply an antiquarian note without theological weight
adults.option_c: The Chronicler is primarily interested in Jehoiada's administrative skill
adults.option_d: The reference to David is used to legitimize Jehoiada's personal authority
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 23:18 — "Jehoiada appointed the offices of the house of the LORD...as David had distributed in the house of the LORD...as it is written in the law of Moses, with rejoicing and with singing, as it was ordained by David."

adults.question: What does the statement "all the people of the land rejoiced and the city was quiet" after the coup suggest about the nature of Athaliah's reign?
adults.option_a: It implies her rule was experienced as oppressive and illegitimate by the people — the restoration of the Davidic heir brought genuine relief and joy, not just political change
adults.option_b: The city's quietness was merely the absence of military conflict after the fighting ended
adults.option_c: The people's joy was primarily relief at having any king rather than a specific attachment to the Davidic line
adults.option_d: The quiet meant the people were indifferent to who ruled as long as order was maintained
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 23:21 — "So all the people of the land rejoiced, and the city was quiet, after that they had slain Athaliah with the sword," contrasting the usurper's removal with the restoration of rightful covenant kingship.

kids.question: How old was Joash when he became king and how long did he reign in Jerusalem?
kids.option_a: He was seven years old when he began to reign and he reigned forty years in Jerusalem
kids.option_b: He was twelve years old and reigned thirty years
kids.option_c: He was five years old and reigned twenty-five years
kids.option_d: He was ten years old and reigned forty-five years
kids.explanation: 2 Chronicles 24:1 — "Joash was seven years old when he began to reign, and he reigned forty years in Jerusalem."

kids.question: What did Joash put outside the temple gate to collect money for repairs?
kids.explanation: 2 Chronicles 24:8 — "And at the king's commandment they made a chest, and set it without at the gate of the house of the LORD."

kids.question: Who was stoned to death for telling the people they had forsaken the LORD?
kids.option_a: Zechariah the son of Jehoiada the priest
kids.explanation: 2 Chronicles 24:20-21 — "And the Spirit of God came upon Zechariah the son of Jehoiada the priest...And they conspired against him, and stoned him with stones at the commandment of the king."

teens.question: What was the state of the temple that Joash wanted to repair?
teens.option_a: It had been broken up and the dedicated things of the house of the LORD had Athaliah's sons used for Baalim
teens.option_b: The walls had been damaged in Jehu's invasion of Jerusalem
teens.option_c: The roof had collapsed during an earthquake in Jehoshaphat's time
teens.option_d: The bronze pillars had been stolen by the Egyptians under Shishak
teens.explanation: 2 Chronicles 24:7 — "For the sons of Athaliah, that wicked woman, had broken up the house of God; and also all the dedicated things of the house of the LORD did they bestow upon Baalim."

teens.question: What did the chest for temple repairs contain when it was brought to the king by the Levites?
teens.option_a: Much money — and the king and Jehoiada's scribe counted it and hired masons and carpenters to repair the house of the LORD
teens.option_b: Empty offerings of grain instead of the required silver
teens.option_c: Gold and silver from the northern tribes who had contributed
teens.option_d: Only the offerings of the priests but none from the people
teens.explanation: 2 Chronicles 24:11 — "Now it came to pass, that at what time the chest was brought unto the king's office by the hand of the Levites...when they saw that there was much money, the king's scribe and the high priest's officer came and emptied the chest."

teens.question: What happened to Joash after Jehoiada the priest died at the age of 130?
teens.option_a: The princes of Judah came and made obeisance to him and he hearkened to them and forsook the house of the LORD to serve groves and idols
teens.option_b: He became even more devoted to the temple service on his own
teens.option_c: He appointed his own son as priest in Jehoiada's place
teens.option_d: He continued all the reforms without Jehoiada's guidance
teens.explanation: 2 Chronicles 24:17-18 — "Now after the death of Jehoiada came the princes of Judah, and made obeisance to the king...And they left the house of the LORD God of their fathers, and served groves and idols."

teens.question: What were the dying words of Zechariah son of Jehoiada as he was being stoned?
teens.option_a: The LORD look upon it, and require it
teens.option_b: God will avenge my blood upon you all
teens.option_c: Forgive them Father for they know not what they do
teens.option_d: May the LORD not forget this evil deed
teens.explanation: 2 Chronicles 24:22 — "And when he died, he said, The LORD look upon it, and require it."

teens.question: How was Joash punished for his wickedness against Zechariah and the prophets?
teens.option_a: A small army of Syrians came and destroyed the princes and the people; Joash was left sick and his servants conspired against him and slew him in his bed
teens.option_b: He was defeated by a great army from Egypt
teens.option_c: God sent a plague that killed his children
teens.option_d: Edom invaded and captured Jerusalem's treasures
teens.explanation: 2 Chronicles 24:23-25 — "And it came to pass at the end of the year, that the host of Syria came up against him...And they left him in great diseases. And his own servants conspired against him...and slew him on his bed, and he died."

adults.question: What was the administrative procedure Joash established for the temple repair collection, and why had the Levites been slow to act?
adults.option_a: He commanded a chest be set outside the gate and a proclamation made in Judah and Jerusalem; the Levites had not been hasty in the work of gathering the money
adults.option_b: He instituted a special Sabbath tax on all landowners in Judah
adults.option_c: He required the temple priests to personally solicit donations from every household
adults.option_d: He used only the existing temple treasury without collecting new funds
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 24:8-10 — "And at the king's commandment they made a chest, and set it without at the gate of the house of the LORD...And all the princes and all the people rejoiced, and brought in, and cast into the chest, until they had made an end."

adults.question: What does the text say was done with the money collected — what was the priority established for its use?
adults.option_a: They used it to hire masons and carpenters to repair the house of the LORD, and also wrought iron and brass to mend the house; after the repairs were done the residue was used for temple vessels
adults.option_b: All the money went directly to the priests for their maintenance
adults.option_c: Half was used for building and half for temple furnishings immediately
adults.option_d: The money was given to the king first and he distributed it to the craftsmen
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 24:12-14 — "And they set it right...and hired masons and carpenters to repair the house of the LORD...And when they had finished it, they brought the rest of the money before the king and Jehoiada, whereof were made vessels of gold and silver."

adults.question: What was the honor given to Jehoiada at his death that was unprecedented for a non-king?
adults.option_a: He was buried among the kings in the city of David because he had done good in Israel both toward God and toward his house
adults.option_b: He was given a state funeral with the full mourning period of thirty days
adults.option_c: A monument was erected to him at the temple gate in his memory
adults.option_d: His name was inscribed in the book of the kings of Judah
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 24:16 — "And they buried him in the city of David among the kings, because he had done good in Israel, both toward God, and toward his house."

adults.question: What does the speed of Joash's apostasy after Jehoiada's death reveal about the nature of his earlier faithfulness?
adults.option_a: His faithfulness was largely dependent on Jehoiada's mentorship rather than deeply rooted personal conviction — when the godly influence was removed, the princes of Judah's flattery led him immediately into idolatry
adults.option_b: His apostasy shows that all kings eventually fail no matter how well they begin
adults.option_c: The princes had been secretly corrupting Joash for years before Jehoiada died
adults.option_d: His fall was caused by political pressure from the northern kingdom rather than internal failure
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 24:2,18 — "Joash did that which was right...all the days of Jehoiada the priest...And they left the house of the LORD God of their fathers, and served groves and idols," showing the conditional nature of his goodness.

adults.question: What made Joash's killing of Zechariah particularly heinous according to the narrative context?
adults.option_a: Zechariah was the son of Jehoiada who had saved Joash's life and been his guardian — Joash forgot the kindness of Jehoiada his father and killed his son, showing ingratitude compounding the crime
adults.option_b: Zechariah was killed in the temple court itself which was sacred ground
adults.option_c: Zechariah was an elderly man too weak to flee or defend himself
adults.option_d: Zechariah had been a personal friend to Joash from childhood
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 24:22 — "Thus Joash the king remembered not the kindness which Jehoiada his father had done to him, but slew his son."

adults.question: How does the Chronicler use the Syrian invasion to illustrate divine retribution in chapter 24?
adults.option_a: A small Syrian army defeated a much larger Judahite force because Judah had forsaken the LORD — the military defeat is explicitly linked to their abandonment of God as immediate divine judgment
adults.option_b: The Syrian victory was due to superior military tactics rather than divine judgment
adults.option_c: The defeat is presented as politically significant but without theological comment
adults.option_d: God used the invasion to punish the princes rather than Joash personally
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 24:24 — "For the army of the Syrians came with a small company of men, and the LORD delivered a very great host into their hand, because they had forsaken the LORD God of their fathers."

adults.question: What does Jesus' reference to "Zacharias son of Barachias" in Matthew 23:35 suggest about how the stoning of Zechariah was remembered in Jewish tradition?
adults.option_a: Jesus cited Zechariah as the last righteous martyr killed in the Hebrew scriptures (from Abel at the beginning to Zechariah at the end of the Hebrew canon), making him a symbol of all prophetic blood shed by Israel
adults.option_b: Jesus was referring to a different Zechariah from a later period
adults.option_c: The reference in Matthew is an error because the father's name differs between Matthew and Chronicles
adults.option_d: Zechariah's death had been forgotten and Jesus was restoring the memory of it
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 24:20-22 — Zechariah son of Jehoiada was stoned in the temple court, referenced by Jesus in Matthew 23:35 as the bookend of righteous martyrdom in the Hebrew scriptures.

adults.question: How does the arc of Joash's reign in chapter 24 — from hiding in the temple as an infant, to repairing it, to killing God's prophet in its court — function as a theological statement about the human heart?
adults.option_a: It shows that even those who begin well and accomplish great things for God can fall catastrophically when they choose human approval (the princes' flattery) over divine faithfulness — external piety cannot substitute for inward devotion
adults.option_b: The arc is simply a historical account without intentional theological shaping
adults.option_c: It shows that all Davidic kings were fundamentally corrupt from the beginning
adults.option_d: The Chronicler uses Joash to argue that only priests, not kings, can be trusted with spiritual leadership
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 24:2 — "Joash did that which was right...all the days of Jehoiada," and verse 18 — "they left the house of the LORD," showing how quickly covenantal faithfulness can unravel without sustained personal commitment.

kids.question: Who was the king of Judah in this chapter?
kids.explanation: 2 Chronicles 25:1 — "Amaziah was twenty and five years old when he began to reign, and he reigned twenty and nine years in Jerusalem."

kids.question: What people did Amaziah defeat in battle?
kids.explanation: 2 Chronicles 25:11 — "And Amaziah strengthened himself, and led forth his people, and went to the valley of salt, and smote of the children of Seir ten thousand."

kids.question: After defeating Edom, what did Amaziah foolishly bring back and worship?
kids.option_a: The gods of the children of Seir
kids.explanation: 2 Chronicles 25:14 — "Now it came to pass, after that Amaziah was come from the slaughter of the Edomites, that he brought the gods of the children of Seir, and set them up to be his gods, and bowed down himself before them."

teens.question: What did the man of God tell Amaziah about the hundred talents of silver he had paid to hire soldiers from Israel?
teens.option_a: That God was not with Israel and Amaziah should send them home, for God was able to give him much more than that
teens.option_b: That he should use the soldiers since he had already paid for them
teens.option_c: That he should keep them as a reserve force
teens.option_d: That the silver would be repaid out of the spoil of Edom
teens.explanation: 2 Chronicles 25:9 — "And Amaziah said to the man of God, But what shall we do for the hundred talents which I have given to the army of Israel? And the man of God answered, The LORD is able to give thee much more than this."

teens.question: What did the dismissed Israelite soldiers do as they returned home angry?
teens.option_a: They fell upon the cities of Judah and smote three thousand and took much spoil
teens.option_b: They ambushed the Edomite army
teens.option_c: They attacked the caravan roads
teens.option_d: They complained to the king of Israel
teens.explanation: 2 Chronicles 25:13 — "But the soldiers of the army which Amaziah sent back...fell upon the cities of Judah...and smote three thousand of them, and took much spoil."

teens.question: How did Amaziah challenge Joash king of Israel, and what was Joash's response using a parable?
teens.option_a: Amaziah challenged him to battle; Joash replied with a parable of a thistle asking a cedar to give his daughter in marriage, warning Amaziah not to stir up trouble
teens.option_b: Amaziah demanded tribute; Joash laughed and sent it
teens.option_c: Amaziah asked for an alliance; Joash agreed and they met at Samaria
teens.option_d: Amaziah invaded first; Joash counter-attacked without warning
teens.explanation: 2 Chronicles 25:18 — "And Joash king of Israel sent to Amaziah king of Judah, saying, The thistle that was in Lebanon sent to the cedar that was in Lebanon, saying, Give thy daughter to my son to wife."

teens.question: What happened when Amaziah would not listen to Joash's warning and the battle was fought at Beth-shemesh?
teens.option_a: Judah was put to the worse before Israel, and Amaziah king of Judah was taken prisoner
teens.option_b: Judah defeated Israel and Joash fled
teens.option_c: Both armies retreated without a decisive result
teens.option_d: Amaziah escaped but his army was captured
teens.explanation: 2 Chronicles 25:22 — "And Judah was put to the worse before Israel, and they fled every man to his tent."

teens.question: How did Amaziah's reign end?
teens.option_a: He was killed at Lachish after fleeing there from a conspiracy in Jerusalem
teens.option_b: He died peacefully in Jerusalem after a long reign
teens.option_c: He was taken captive to Babylon
teens.option_d: He was struck with leprosy like Uzziah
teens.explanation: 2 Chronicles 25:27-28 — "They sent to Lachish after him, and slew him there...and they buried him with his fathers in the city of Judah."

adults.question: What qualification does the Chronicler give for Amaziah's character at the outset, and what does this phrase suggest about the limits of his devotion?
adults.option_a: He did that which was right in the sight of the LORD, but not with a perfect heart
adults.option_b: He was wholly faithful but lacked military skill
adults.option_c: He kept the law perfectly but was proud
adults.option_d: He was righteous in his youth but fell away in old age
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 25:2 — "And he did that which was right in the sight of the LORD, but not with a perfect heart."

adults.question: How did Amaziah handle the men who had murdered his father, and what law did he cite in doing so?
adults.option_a: He killed only the murderers but not their children, as written in the law of Moses that fathers shall not die for their children
adults.option_b: He executed the entire families of the conspirators
adults.option_c: He exiled the murderers to the wilderness
adults.option_d: He forgave them according to his priestly advisors' counsel
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 25:4 — "But he slew not their children, but did as it is written in the law in the book of Moses, where the LORD commanded, saying, The fathers shall not die for the children."

adults.question: How many Edomites were killed in the valley of salt and at the cliff, and what was done at the cliff?
adults.option_a: Ten thousand were slain in the valley of salt, and ten thousand were cast down from the cliff so that they all were broken in pieces
adults.option_b: Twenty thousand slain in battle and five thousand taken captive
adults.option_c: Thirty thousand slain with the sword alone
adults.option_d: Fifteen thousand slain and the rest enslaved
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 25:11-12 — "And Amaziah...smote of the children of Seir ten thousand. And other ten thousand left alive did the children of Judah carry away captive, and brought them unto the top of the rock, and cast them down from the top of the rock, that they all were broken in pieces."

adults.question: When the prophet warned Amaziah about worshipping Edomite gods, what was Amaziah's arrogant reply, and what did the prophet say in return?
adults.option_a: Amaziah asked who made the prophet a counsellor to the king; the prophet said he knew God had determined to destroy Amaziah because he had done this
adults.option_b: Amaziah imprisoned the prophet immediately
adults.option_c: Amaziah repented and destroyed the idols
adults.option_d: Amaziah ignored the prophet without speaking
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 25:16 — "Then the prophet forbare, and said, I know that God hath determined to destroy thee, because thou hast done this, and hast not hearkened unto my counsel."

adults.question: What specific damage did Joash do to Jerusalem after defeating Amaziah?
adults.option_a: He broke down four hundred cubits of the wall of Jerusalem from the gate of Ephraim to the corner gate, took all the gold, silver, and vessels in the house of God, and took hostages
adults.option_b: He burned the temple and carried off only the priests
adults.option_c: He took only the king's treasury and left the city intact
adults.option_d: He demolished the palace but spared the temple
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 25:23-24 — "And Joash...broke down the wall of Jerusalem from the gate of Ephraim to the corner gate, four hundred cubits. And he took all the gold and the silver, and all the vessels...and returned to Samaria."

adults.question: What theological explanation does the Chronicler give for Amaziah's refusal to heed Joash's parable warning?
adults.option_a: It came of God, that he might deliver them into the hand of their enemies, because they sought after the gods of Edom
adults.option_b: Amaziah was proud from his victory over Edom
adults.option_c: His advisors had convinced him Israel was weak
adults.option_d: He misread the political situation and thought Joash was bluffing
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 25:20 — "But Amaziah would not hear; for it came of God, that he might deliver them into the hand of their enemies, because they sought after the gods of Edom."

adults.question: How many men of valour did Amaziah muster from Judah and Benjamin for his Edomite campaign?
adults.option_a: Three hundred thousand choice men, able to go forth to war, that could handle spear and shield
adults.option_b: One hundred thousand from Judah alone
adults.option_c: Five hundred thousand from all twelve tribes
adults.option_d: Two hundred thousand with one hundred thousand hired from Israel
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 25:5 — "He gathered Judah together, and made them captains over thousands, and captains over hundreds...and found them three hundred thousand choice men, able to go forth to war."

adults.question: What was the sum Amaziah paid to hire men of valour from Israel, and why was this a spiritually costly decision?
adults.option_a: A hundred talents of silver; it was costly because God was not with Israel and it showed Amaziah's lack of faith in God's sufficiency alone
adults.option_b: A thousand talents of gold; it depleted Judah's treasury fatally
adults.option_c: Ten talents of silver; a small sum showing contempt for Israel
adults.option_d: Fifty talents of silver; acceptable because it was a defensive alliance
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 25:6 — "He hired also an hundred thousand mighty men of valour out of Israel for an hundred talents of silver."

kids.question: Who was the king of Judah who became a leper?
kids.explanation: 2 Chronicles 26:19 — "Then Uzziah was wroth, and had a censer in his hand to burn incense: and while he was wroth with the priests, the leprosy even rose up in his forehead before the priests in the house of the LORD."

kids.question: Why was Uzziah struck with leprosy?
kids.option_a: He went into the temple to burn incense, which only priests were allowed to do
kids.option_d: He worshipped idols in the high places
kids.explanation: 2 Chronicles 26:16 — "But when he was strong, his heart was lifted up to his destruction: for he transgressed against the LORD his God, and went into the temple of the LORD to burn incense upon the altar of incense."

kids.question: As long as Uzziah sought the LORD, who helped him to prosper?
kids.option_c: The priests prayed for him daily
kids.option_d: His father Amaziah taught him wisdom
kids.explanation: 2 Chronicles 26:5 — "And he sought God in the days of Zechariah, who had understanding in the visions of God: and as long as he sought the LORD, God made him to prosper."

teens.question: What prophet instructed Uzziah and helped him seek God in his early years?
teens.option_a: Zechariah, who had understanding in the visions of God
teens.option_b: Isaiah the son of Amoz
teens.option_c: Amos the herdsman from Tekoa
teens.option_d: Micah of Moresheth
teens.explanation: 2 Chronicles 26:5 — "And he sought God in the days of Zechariah, who had understanding in the visions of God."

teens.question: What were three areas in which Uzziah's military power was strengthened?
teens.option_a: He had an army of 307,500 soldiers, built towers in Jerusalem and the wilderness, and invented engines of war for the towers
teens.option_b: He conquered Egypt, Philistia, and Edom
teens.option_c: He built a navy, fortified Judah's borders, and trained cavalry
teens.option_d: He hired foreign mercenaries, built siege weapons, and dug water channels
teens.explanation: 2 Chronicles 26:12-15 — "The whole number of the chief of the fathers of the mighty men of valour were two thousand and six hundred...And the number of all their host was three hundred thousand and seven thousand and five hundred...And he made in Jerusalem engines, invented by cunning men, to be on the towers."

teens.question: What three groups did Uzziah defeat or subdue during his reign?
teens.option_a: The Philistines, the Arabians at Gur-baal, and the Mehunim
teens.option_b: The Egyptians, the Moabites, and the Edomites
teens.option_c: The Assyrians, the Syrians, and the Ammonites
teens.option_d: The Amalekites, the Hivites, and the Perizzites
teens.explanation: 2 Chronicles 26:7 — "And God helped him against the Philistines, and against the Arabians that dwelt in Gur-baal, and the Mehunim."

teens.question: What happened when Uzziah entered the temple to burn incense and the priests confronted him?
teens.option_a: Uzziah was wroth with the priests, and leprosy rose in his forehead before them in the house of the LORD
teens.option_b: He repented and left immediately without consequence
teens.option_c: The priests allowed him to continue since he was king
teens.option_d: Fire fell from heaven and consumed the altar
teens.explanation: 2 Chronicles 26:19 — "Then Uzziah was wroth, and had a censer in his hand to burn incense: and while he was wroth with the priests, the leprosy even rose up in his forehead before the priests."

teens.question: Where did Uzziah live after he was struck with leprosy, and who governed in his place?
teens.option_a: He dwelt in a several house, being a leper, and Jotham his son was over the king's house, judging the people
teens.option_b: He was sent to a leper colony outside Judah
teens.option_c: He went to Egypt for healing
teens.option_d: He remained in the palace but was hidden from public view
teens.explanation: 2 Chronicles 26:21 — "And Uzziah the king was a leper unto the day of his death, and dwelt in a several house, being a leper; for he was cut off from the house of the LORD: and Jotham his son was over the king's house, judging the people of the land."

adults.question: What does the phrase "his heart was lifted up to his destruction" tell us theologically about the relationship between prosperity and pride in the Chronicler's view?
adults.option_a: That God-given success became the very occasion of Uzziah's fall, teaching that strength and pride lead to transgression without continual dependence on God
adults.option_b: That Uzziah was inherently wicked from the beginning
adults.option_c: That material success is itself a spiritual danger to be avoided
adults.option_d: That God withdrew his blessing once the temple was built
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 26:16 — "But when he was strong, his heart was lifted up to his destruction: for he transgressed against the LORD his God."

adults.question: How many priests withstood Uzziah when he entered the temple, and what did they declare to him?
adults.option_a: Fourscore priests of valour, who said it appertaineth not unto thee, Uzziah, to burn incense unto the LORD, but to the priests the sons of Aaron that are consecrated to burn incense
adults.option_b: Twelve chief priests representing the twelve tribes
adults.option_c: Only the high priest Azariah alone confronted him
adults.option_d: Seven priests as required by the law of Moses
