adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 26:17-18 — "And Azariah the priest went in after him, and with him fourscore priests of the LORD, that were valiant men: And they withstood Uzziah the king, and said unto him...It appertaineth not unto thee, Uzziah, to burn incense."

adults.question: What territories did Uzziah build cities in after breaking down Philistine walls, and what tribute did the Ammonites pay?
adults.option_a: He built cities about Ashdod and in Philistia, and the Ammonites gave gifts, his name spreading to Egypt
adults.option_b: He rebuilt only Jerusalem's walls
adults.option_c: He conquered only the coastal plain and demanded no tribute
adults.option_d: He built only military outposts in the Negev
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 26:6-8 — "And he went forth and warred against the Philistines, and brake down the wall of Gath, and the wall of Jabneh, and the wall of Ashdod, and built cities about Ashdod...And the Ammonites gave gifts to Uzziah: and his name spread abroad even to the entering in of Egypt."

adults.question: What agricultural and pastoral activities does the Chronicler mention to show Uzziah's love of the land?
adults.option_a: He had husbandmen and vinedressers in the mountains and in Carmel, for he loved husbandry, and also much cattle in the lowlands
adults.option_b: He built irrigation canals throughout Judah
adults.option_c: He imported grain from Egypt to fill storehouses
adults.option_d: He planted forests and orchards around Jerusalem
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 26:10 — "Also he built towers in the desert, and digged many wells: for he had much cattle, both in the low country, and in the plains: husbandmen also, and vine dressers in the mountains, and in Carmel: for he loved husbandry."

adults.question: What specific equipment are the "engines invented by cunning men" described as being used for, and where were they placed?
adults.option_a: They were placed on towers and on bulwarks to shoot arrows and great stones, and Uzziah's name spread abroad because of his great strength
adults.option_b: They were siege machines used to attack enemy cities
adults.option_c: They were agricultural devices to assist in harvest
adults.option_d: They were water-lifting machines for the desert wells
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 26:15 — "And he made in Jerusalem engines, invented by cunning men, to be on the towers and upon the bulwarks, to shoot arrows and great stones withal: and his name spread far abroad."

adults.question: What was Uzziah's age at accession, the length of his reign, and the name of the historian who recorded his acts?
adults.option_a: He was sixteen when he began to reign, reigned fifty-two years, and his acts were written by Isaiah the prophet the son of Amoz
adults.option_b: He was twenty-five, reigned forty years, written by Jeremiah
adults.option_c: He was eighteen, reigned forty-five years, written by Ezra
adults.option_d: He was twenty, reigned thirty years, written by Nathan
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 26:1,3,22 — "Uzziah, who was sixteen years old...reigned fifty and two years in Jerusalem...Now the rest of the acts of Uzziah, first and last, did Isaiah the prophet, the son of Amoz, write."

adults.question: What happened to Uzziah when he died as a leper, and how was his burial affected by his condition?
adults.option_a: He was buried in the field of the burial which belonged to the kings, for they said he is a leper, not in the royal sepulchres
adults.option_b: He was buried with full honours in the royal tombs
adults.option_c: He was buried outside the city in an unmarked grave
adults.option_d: He was buried at Gibeon by the priests
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 26:23 — "So Uzziah slept with his fathers, and they buried him with his fathers in the field of the burial which belonged to the kings; for they said, He is a leper."

adults.question: What does the Chronicler's pairing of "God made him to prosper" (v.5) and "his heart was lifted up to his destruction" (v.16) reveal about the spiritual structure of his reign?
adults.option_a: It reveals a pattern the Chronicler uses repeatedly: faithfulness brings blessing, but success without continued humility leads to pride and judgment — a central theme of Chronicles
adults.option_b: It reveals that God's blessings are always temporary and must be earned daily
adults.option_c: It reveals Uzziah was weak-willed and easily corrupted
adults.option_d: It reveals the priests had too much power over the king
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 26:5,16 — The Chronicler deliberately frames Uzziah's reign around these two verses to teach that seeking God is the only safeguard against the corruption of success.

kids.question: Who was king of Judah in this chapter?
kids.explanation: 2 Chronicles 27:1 — "Jotham was twenty and five years old when he began to reign, and he reigned sixteen years in Jerusalem."

kids.question: What gate did Jotham build on the temple mount?
kids.option_a: The upper gate of the house of the LORD
kids.option_b: The water gate of the city
kids.option_c: The sheep gate by the brook
kids.explanation: 2 Chronicles 27:3 — "He built the high gate of the house of the LORD, and on the wall of Ophel he built much."

kids.question: Jotham became mighty because he did what?
kids.option_a: Prepared his ways before the LORD his God
kids.option_b: Built the largest army in Judah's history
kids.option_c: Made an alliance with the king of Egypt
kids.option_d: Destroyed all the high places in the land
kids.explanation: 2 Chronicles 27:6 — "So Jotham became mighty, because he prepared his ways before the LORD his God."

teens.question: What mistake that his father Uzziah made did Jotham carefully avoid?
teens.option_a: He entered not into the temple of the LORD, unlike his father who intruded on the priests' duties
teens.option_b: He never made war against neighbouring nations
teens.option_c: He never levied taxes on the people
teens.option_d: He never hired foreign craftsmen
teens.explanation: 2 Chronicles 27:2 — "And he did that which was right in the sight of the LORD, according to all that his father Uzziah did: howbeit he entered not into the temple of the LORD."

teens.question: What nations did Jotham defeat or subdue during his reign?
teens.option_a: The Ammonites, from whom he received tribute for three years
teens.option_b: The Philistines and the Arabians
teens.option_c: The Edomites and the Syrians
teens.option_d: The Moabites and the Midianites
teens.explanation: 2 Chronicles 27:5 — "He fought also with the king of the Ammonites, and prevailed against them. And the children of Ammon gave him the same year an hundred talents of silver."

teens.question: What was the annual tribute Jotham received from the Ammonites for three years?
teens.option_a: A hundred talents of silver, ten thousand measures of wheat, and ten thousand of barley
teens.option_b: Two hundred talents of gold each year
teens.option_c: Fifty talents of silver and twenty thousand sheep
teens.option_d: A thousand talents of silver the first year only
teens.explanation: 2 Chronicles 27:5 — "And the children of Ammon gave him the same year an hundred talents of silver, and ten thousand measures of wheat, and ten thousand of barley. So much did the children of Ammon pay him, both the second year, and the third."

teens.question: What building projects did Jotham undertake besides the upper gate?
teens.option_a: He built much on the wall of Ophel, built cities in the mountains of Judah, and built castles and towers in the forests
teens.option_b: He rebuilt the entire Jerusalem wall that Joash had broken down
teens.option_c: He constructed a new palace and royal treasury
teens.option_d: He built a new altar for the temple court
teens.explanation: 2 Chronicles 27:3-4 — "He built the high gate of the house of the LORD, and on the wall of Ophel he built much. Moreover he built cities in the mountains of Judah, and in the forests he built castles and towers."

teens.question: What was the moral condition of the people of Judah during Jotham's reign despite his own righteousness?
teens.option_a: The people did yet corruptly
teens.option_b: The people were faithful and worshipped only the LORD
teens.option_c: The people followed Jotham's example completely
teens.option_d: The people were divided between two opinions
teens.explanation: 2 Chronicles 27:2 — "And the people did yet corruptly."

adults.question: What does the brief but positive account of Jotham suggest about the Chronicler's evaluative criteria for kings?
adults.option_a: That consistent faithfulness and preparation of one's ways before God — even without dramatic reform — constitutes a good reign worthy of divine blessing
adults.option_b: That military success is the primary measure of a godly king
adults.option_c: That building projects demonstrate God's favour upon a reign
adults.option_d: That tribute from foreign nations signals divine approval
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 27:6 — "So Jotham became mighty, because he prepared his ways before the LORD his God," showing internal faithfulness as the foundation of external blessing.

adults.question: How does the Chronicler's note that "the people did yet corruptly" function within his theological framework?
adults.option_a: It shows that a king's personal righteousness does not automatically transform the people, and that national corruption persists independently of royal piety
adults.option_b: It suggests Jotham was secretly wicked
adults.option_c: It explains why God eventually punished Jotham
adults.option_d: It shows the Levites were failing in their teaching duties
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 27:2 — "And the people did yet corruptly," — a rare instance where the Chronicler separates kingly virtue from national spiritual condition.

adults.question: What was Jotham's age at accession and the total length of his reign?
adults.option_a: He was twenty-five years old when he began to reign and reigned sixteen years in Jerusalem
adults.option_b: He was twenty years old and reigned twenty years
adults.option_c: He was eighteen and reigned twelve years
adults.option_d: He was thirty and reigned twenty-five years
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 27:1 — "Jotham was twenty and five years old when he began to reign, and he reigned sixteen years in Jerusalem."

adults.question: What was his mother's name and her father's identity?
adults.option_a: His mother's name was Jerushah the daughter of Zadok
adults.option_b: His mother's name was Athaliah the daughter of Ahab
adults.option_c: His mother's name was Abijah the daughter of Zechariah
adults.option_d: His mother's name was Meshullemeth the daughter of Haruz
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 27:1 — "His mother's name also was Jerushah, the daughter of Zadok."

adults.question: Where does the Chronicler direct readers who want to learn more about Jotham's acts and wars?
adults.option_a: To the book of the kings of Israel and Judah
adults.option_b: To the writings of the prophet Isaiah
adults.option_c: To the records of the priests at Jerusalem
adults.option_d: To the chronicles of the kings of Judah
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 27:7 — "Now the rest of the acts of Jotham, and all his wars, and his ways, lo, they are written in the book of the kings of Israel and Judah."

adults.question: What theological significance does the Chronicler attach to Jotham's building of the upper gate of the temple?
adults.option_a: It reflects his respect for the sanctity of the temple and his desire to honour God's house without presuming priestly privileges as his father had done
adults.option_b: It was a purely defensive military measure
adults.option_c: It demonstrated his wealth and power to surrounding nations
adults.option_d: It fulfilled a prophecy made in the days of Solomon
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 27:3 — "He built the high gate of the house of the LORD," which the Chronicler contrasts implicitly with Uzziah's unlawful entry into the same temple.

adults.question: How does the shortness of Jotham's chapter compared to those of Hezekiah and Josiah reflect the Chronicler's literary strategy?
adults.option_a: The Chronicler gives proportionate space to reigns proportionate to their significance as reform movements — Jotham was faithful but not a major reformer
adults.option_b: The Chronicler had fewer sources for Jotham
adults.option_c: Jotham's reign was deliberately minimized to contrast with evil Ahaz
adults.option_d: Jotham's records were lost in the Babylonian exile
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 27:9 — "So Jotham slept with his fathers, and they buried him in the city of David," a quiet, dignified conclusion matching his quiet, faithful reign.

adults.question: How does Jotham's succession pattern compare with the pattern of other kings in Chronicles, and what does it reveal about dynastic stability during periods of faithfulness?
adults.option_a: His son Ahaz reigned in his stead, showing that faithfulness in one generation does not guarantee it in the next — a recurring Chronicler warning
adults.option_b: Jotham's son continued his reforms and exceeded them
adults.option_c: The succession was disputed and nearly broke the Davidic line
adults.option_d: Jotham reigned jointly with Ahaz for ten years
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 27:9 — "And Ahaz his son reigned in his stead," — Ahaz immediately reversed all his father's godly ways, a sobering sequel to Jotham's faithful reign.

kids.question: King Ahaz of Judah did evil things. What did he make and worship?
kids.option_b: A golden calf like Jeroboam made
kids.option_c: An idol of Dagon the Philistine god
kids.option_d: A bronze serpent like Moses made
kids.explanation: 2 Chronicles 28:2 — "For he walked in the ways of the kings of Israel, and made also molten images for Baalim."

kids.question: Who was the prophet that stopped the Israelite soldiers from making slaves of the people of Judah they had captured?
kids.explanation: 2 Chronicles 28:9 — "But a prophet of the LORD was there, whose name was Oded: and he went out before the host that came to Samaria, and said unto them, Behold, because the LORD God of your fathers was wroth with Judah, he hath delivered them into your hand."

kids.question: When Ahaz was in trouble, who did he ask for help instead of trusting God?
kids.explanation: 2 Chronicles 28:16 — "At that time did king Ahaz send unto the kings of Assyria to help him."

teens.question: What terrible sin did Ahaz commit involving his own children?
teens.option_a: He burnt his children in the fire, after the abominations of the heathen
teens.option_b: He sold his children into slavery to Phoenicia
teens.option_c: He dedicated his sons to idol priests as servants
teens.option_d: He forced his children to marry foreign kings' daughters
teens.explanation: 2 Chronicles 28:3 — "Moreover he burnt incense in the valley of the son of Hinnom, and burnt his children in the fire, after the abominations of the heathen whom the LORD had cast out before the children of Israel."

teens.question: By how many men did Israel smite Judah in one day during the Syro-Ephraimite war?
teens.option_a: A hundred and twenty thousand valiant men in one day
teens.option_b: Fifty thousand in the first battle
teens.option_c: Two hundred thousand over three days
teens.option_d: Thirty thousand in a single ambush
teens.explanation: 2 Chronicles 28:6 — "For Pekah the son of Remaliah slew in Judah an hundred and twenty thousand in one day, which were all valiant men; because they had forsaken the LORD God of their fathers."

teens.question: What did the Israelite men of Ephraim do after Oded the prophet rebuked them about the captives?
teens.option_a: They rose up, took the captives, clothed them from the spoil, and brought them to Jericho, the city of palm trees
teens.option_b: They set the captives free in the wilderness
teens.option_c: They returned the captives to Jerusalem immediately
teens.option_d: They divided the captives among the Levite cities
teens.explanation: 2 Chronicles 28:14-15 — "So the armed men left the captives and the spoil before the princes and all the congregation. And the men which were expressed by name rose up, and took the captives, and with the spoil clothed all that were naked among them...and brought them to Jericho."

teens.question: What foreign powers attacked Judah simultaneously while Ahaz was under pressure from Israel and Syria?
teens.option_a: The Edomites smote Judah and carried away captives, and the Philistines invaded the cities of the low country and the south of Judah
teens.option_b: The Moabites and Ammonites invaded from the east
teens.option_c: The Egyptians raided the Negev
teens.option_d: The Assyrians attacked Jerusalem directly
teens.explanation: 2 Chronicles 28:17-18 — "For again the Edomites had come and smitten Judah, and carried away captives. The Philistines also had invaded the cities of the low country, and of the south of Judah."

teens.question: What did Ahaz do with the treasures of the temple when he appealed to Assyria for help?
teens.option_a: He took a portion out of the house of the LORD, and out of the house of the king, and of the princes, and gave it to the king of Assyria — but he helped him not
teens.option_b: He melted down all the bronze furnishings
teens.option_c: He offered the temple itself as a gift to Assyria
teens.option_d: He transferred the ark to Assyria as a pledge
teens.explanation: 2 Chronicles 28:21 — "For Ahaz took away a portion out of the house of the LORD, and out of the house of the king, and of the princes, and gave it unto the king of Assyria: but he helped him not."

adults.question: What specific theological reason does the Chronicler give for Judah's defeat by Israel, and what does this reveal about his view of military outcomes?
adults.option_a: Because they had forsaken the LORD God of their fathers; showing that military defeat is always the consequence of spiritual apostasy in the Chronicler's framework
adults.option_b: The defeat was due to Ahaz's inferior military tactics
adults.option_c: The defeat was God's will to reunite the kingdom
adults.option_d: The defeat proved Israel's army was always stronger
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 28:6 — "For Pekah...slew in Judah an hundred and twenty thousand in one day, which were all valiant men; because they had forsaken the LORD God of their fathers."

adults.question: What was the name of the Israelite who killed the king's son, the governor of the house, and the king's second in command in the battle against Judah?
adults.option_a: Zichri, a mighty man of Ephraim, killed Maaseiah the king's son, and Azrikam and Elkanah that was next to the king
adults.option_b: Pekah the king himself killed all three
adults.option_c: Oded the prophet's son killed them in battle
adults.option_d: An unnamed captain from the tribe of Dan
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 28:7 — "And Zichri, a mighty man of Ephraim, slew Maaseiah the king's son, and Azrikam the governor of the house, and Elkanah that was next to the king."

adults.question: What was the key argument Oded used to persuade Israel not to enslave the captives of Judah?
adults.option_a: That Israel themselves had sins against God, and if they enslaved Judah they would add to their own trespass, and there was fierce wrath against Israel already
adults.option_b: That it was illegal under Mosaic law to enslave kinsmen
adults.option_c: That the captives were too weak to be useful as slaves
adults.option_d: That the surrounding nations would mock Israel for enslaving brothers
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 28:10-11 — "And now ye purpose to keep under the children of Judah and Jerusalem for bondmen and bondwomen unto you: but are there not with you, even with you, sins against the LORD your God? Now hear me therefore, and deliver the captives again."

adults.question: What does the Chronicler's account of Ahaz's response to trouble reveal about the spiritual logic of his reign?
adults.option_a: In his time of distress he trespassed yet more against the LORD, showing that hardship without repentance leads to deeper apostasy rather than return to God
adults.option_b: Ahaz was repentant but did not know how to seek God properly
adults.option_c: Ahaz's advisors prevented him from turning to the LORD
adults.option_d: Ahaz sought God but God refused to hear him because of Manasseh's sins
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 28:22 — "And in the time of his distress did he trespass yet more against the LORD: this is that king Ahaz."

adults.question: What did Ahaz do with the altars of the gods of Damascus, and what was his stated rationale?
adults.option_a: He sacrificed to the gods of Damascus which smote him, saying because the gods of the kings of Syria help them I will sacrifice to them that they may help me
adults.option_b: He destroyed the Damascene altars to prove his loyalty to God
adults.option_c: He sent the altars to Assyria as tribute
adults.option_d: He set up the altars as trophies of his victory
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 28:23 — "For he sacrificed unto the gods of Damascus, which smote him: and he said, Because the gods of the kings of Syria help them, therefore will I sacrifice to them, that they may help me."

adults.question: What did Ahaz do to the vessels of the house of God, the temple doors, and the altars at the end of his reign?
adults.option_a: He cut in pieces the vessels of the house of God, shut up the doors of the house of the LORD, and made altars in every corner of Jerusalem and high places in every city of Judah
adults.option_b: He melted the vessels to make coins
adults.option_c: He sent the vessels to Assyria as tribute
adults.option_d: He buried the holy vessels to protect them from the Assyrians
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 28:24-25 — "And Ahaz gathered together the vessels of the house of God, and cut in pieces the vessels...and he shut up the doors of the house of the LORD...And in every several city of Judah he made high places to burn incense unto other gods."

adults.question: What princes of Ephraim by name stood against those returning from battle to insist the captives be released?
adults.option_a: Azariah the son of Johanan, Berechiah the son of Meshillemoth, Jehizkiah the son of Shallum, and Amasa the son of Hadlai
adults.option_b: Oded and his four sons
adults.option_c: The chief Levites of Samaria
adults.option_d: The elders of the gate of Samaria
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 28:12 — "Then certain of the heads of the children of Ephraim, Azariah the son of Johanan, Berechiah the son of Meshillemoth, and Jehizkiah the son of Shallum, and Amasa the son of Hadlai, stood up against them that came from the war."

adults.question: How many cities did the Philistines take from Judah during Ahaz's reign, and what does the Chronicler say was the cause?
adults.option_a: They took Beth-shemesh, Aijalon, Gederoth, Shocho, Timnah, Gimzo, and their villages; the LORD brought Judah low because of Ahaz king of Israel who made Judah naked and transgressed sore
adults.option_b: They took only three border cities
adults.option_c: They took twenty cities and enslaved the population
adults.option_d: They took Lachish and the surrounding towns only
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 28:18-19 — "The Philistines also had invaded the cities of the low country...For the LORD brought Judah low because of Ahaz king of Israel; for he made Judah naked, and transgressed sore against the LORD."

kids.question: King Hezekiah opened and repaired what building in his first year as king?
kids.option_a: The doors of the house of the LORD
kids.explanation: 2 Chronicles 29:3 — "He in the first year of his reign, in the first month, opened the doors of the house of the LORD, and repaired them."

kids.question: Who did Hezekiah gather together to help cleanse the temple?
kids.explanation: 2 Chronicles 29:4 — "And he brought in the priests and the Levites, and gathered them together into the east street."

kids.question: After the temple was cleansed, what did Hezekiah command the Levites to do with musical instruments?
kids.option_a: To praise the LORD with the words of David and Asaph
kids.option_b: To teach the people to sing new songs
kids.option_c: To play music for the king's coronation
kids.option_d: To make new instruments from cedar wood
kids.explanation: 2 Chronicles 29:30 — "Moreover Hezekiah the king and the princes commanded the Levites to sing praise unto the LORD with the words of David, and of Asaph the seer."

teens.question: What did Hezekiah say to the Levites about why Judah was in its current miserable condition?
teens.option_a: Our fathers have trespassed and done evil in God's eyes, forsaken him, turned their faces away, shut the temple doors, and quenched the lamps — therefore the wrath of the LORD is upon Judah
teens.option_b: Our fathers were defeated in battle because they did not train soldiers well
teens.option_c: Our fathers offended the Assyrian king and brought his army upon us
teens.option_d: Our fathers failed to pay tithes and God removed his protection
teens.explanation: 2 Chronicles 29:6-8 — "For our fathers have trespassed, and done that which was evil in the eyes of the LORD our God, and have forsaken him...Wherefore the wrath of the LORD was upon Judah and Jerusalem."

teens.question: How many days did it take the priests and Levites to sanctify the house of the LORD and carry the uncleanness out?
teens.option_a: Eight days sanctifying the porch and the house, then eight more days the altar and the holy vessels — sixteen days total
teens.option_b: Seven days as commanded in the law of Moses
teens.option_c: Three days for the inner sanctuary
teens.option_d: One month for the entire temple compound
teens.explanation: 2 Chronicles 29:17 — "Now they began on the first day of the first month to sanctify, and on the eighth day of the month came they to the porch of the LORD: so they sanctified the house of the LORD in eight days; and in the sixteenth day of the first month they made an end."

teens.question: What did Hezekiah say was his purpose in making a covenant with the LORD?
teens.option_a: That the fierce wrath of the LORD might turn away from Israel
teens.option_b: To secure victory against Assyria
teens.option_c: To obtain God's blessing on the harvest
teens.option_d: To establish his dynasty on the throne forever
teens.explanation: 2 Chronicles 29:10 — "Now it is in mine heart to make a covenant with the LORD God of Israel, that his fierce wrath may turn away from us."

teens.question: How many burnt offerings did the congregation bring for the dedication, and what was special about their number?
teens.option_a: Seven bullocks, seven rams, seven lambs, seven he goats as a sin offering — the number seven for each type
teens.option_b: Twelve of each type representing the twelve tribes
teens.option_c: A hundred of each type showing great abundance
teens.option_d: One for each Levite who served in the temple
teens.explanation: 2 Chronicles 29:21 — "And they brought seven bullocks, and seven rams, and seven lambs, and seven he goats, for a sin offering for the kingdom, and for the sanctuary, and for Judah."

teens.question: What did all the congregation do when the burnt offering began and the Levites sang?
teens.option_a: They bowed themselves and worshipped, and the singers sang and the trumpeters sounded
teens.option_b: They shouted with a loud voice and ran to the altar
teens.option_c: They prostrated themselves silently before the holy place
teens.option_d: They returned to their homes to celebrate the feast
teens.explanation: 2 Chronicles 29:28 — "And all the congregation worshipped, and the singers sang, and the trumpeters sounded: and all this continued until the burnt offering was finished."

adults.question: What does Hezekiah's declaration "it is in mine heart" reveal about the Chronicler's view of true reform?
adults.option_a: It is fundamentally personal — a king's deliberate inward decision to seek God precedes and drives institutional reform, as the Chronicler shows throughout Chronicles
adults.option_b: It is primarily institutional — the temple system must be restored before personal faith can function
adults.option_c: It is mainly legal — correct observance of the law automatically produces revival
adults.option_d: It is political — Hezekiah wanted to consolidate power through the temple establishment
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 29:10 — "Now it is in mine heart to make a covenant with the LORD God of Israel," placing personal resolve at the root of national renewal.

adults.question: What specific unclean items did the priests carry out of the sanctuary, and where did the Levites take them?
adults.option_a: All the uncleanness found in the temple of the LORD into the court, and the Levites carried it out to the brook Kidron — a place associated with disposing of idolatrous objects in other reformations
adults.option_b: They burned everything on the altar of burnt offering
adults.option_c: They buried unclean items under the temple mount
adults.option_d: They threw them into the Dead Sea
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 29:16 — "And the priests went into the inner part of the house of the LORD, to cleanse it, and brought out all the uncleanness...And the Levites took it, to carry it out abroad into the brook Kidron."

adults.question: What was the role of the Levites when there were not enough sanctified priests, and what commendation does the text give them?
adults.option_a: The Levites killed the sin offerings because there were not enough sanctified priests — the Levites were more upright in heart to sanctify themselves than the priests
adults.option_b: The Levites composed new psalms while the priests performed the sacrifices
adults.option_c: The Levites taught the people while the priests played music
adults.option_d: The Levites received the offering money and the priests distributed it
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 29:34 — "But the priests were too few...wherefore their brethren the Levites did help them...for the Levites were more upright in heart to sanctify themselves than the priests."

adults.question: How many bulls, rams, lambs, oxen, and sheep were offered in the great burnt offering and consecrated things?
adults.option_a: Seventy bullocks, a hundred rams, two hundred lambs for burnt offering; six hundred oxen and three thousand sheep for holy things
adults.option_b: Seven bullocks, seven rams, seven lambs, seven goats only
adults.option_c: A hundred bulls and a thousand lambs
adults.option_d: Twelve of each animal representing the twelve tribes
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 29:32-33 — "And the number of the burnt offerings, which the congregation brought, was threescore and ten bullocks, an hundred rams, and two hundred lambs...And the consecrated things were six hundred oxen and three thousand sheep."

adults.question: What instruments did Hezekiah command the Levites to use, and what was their scriptural precedent?
adults.option_a: Cymbals, psalteries, and harps according to the commandment of David and Gad and Nathan the king's prophet, for the commandment was by the LORD through his prophets
adults.option_b: Only the trumpets as prescribed in Numbers
adults.option_c: Harps and lyres only as David had used
adults.option_d: Seven trumpets as used at Jericho
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 29:25 — "And he set the Levites in the house of the LORD with cymbals, with psalteries, and with harps, according to the commandment of David, and of Gad the king's seer, and Nathan the prophet: for so was the commandment of the LORD by his prophets."

adults.question: What theological significance does Hezekiah's speech attribute to the exile of their kinsmen?
adults.option_a: It was God's judgment — a sword, captivity, spoil, and derision — because the trespass of their fathers who forsook God brought the wrath of the LORD upon Judah and Jerusalem
adults.option_b: It was a natural consequence of political weakness
adults.option_c: It was a test to purify the faithful remnant
adults.option_d: It was caused by the sins of foreign nations against Israel
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 29:8-9 — "Wherefore the wrath of the LORD was upon Judah and Jerusalem, and he hath delivered them to trouble, to astonishment, and to hissing, as ye see with your eyes. For, lo, our fathers have fallen by the sword."

adults.question: What phrase does Hezekiah use to describe the temple's proper function, and what does this reveal about his theology of the sanctuary?
adults.option_a: He calls it a place to stand before God, to serve him, and to burn incense — showing the temple's purpose is ongoing ministry and intercession on behalf of the nation
adults.option_b: He calls it a treasury for the nation's wealth
adults.option_c: He calls it a palace for the king of heaven
adults.option_d: He calls it a school for teaching the law
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 29:11 — "My sons, be not now negligent: for the LORD hath chosen you to stand before him, to serve him, and that ye should minister unto him, and burn incense."

adults.question: What was the immediate congregational response to the rededication, and what does the Chronicler emphasize about the speed of the reform?
adults.option_a: All the congregation rejoiced, and Hezekiah rejoiced because the thing was done suddenly — God had prepared the people for such rapid transformation
adults.option_b: The people celebrated for seven days before the rededication was complete
adults.option_c: The congregation was divided and some refused to participate
adults.option_d: Only the priests and Levites rejoiced while the people waited outside
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 29:36 — "And Hezekiah rejoiced, and all the people, that God had prepared the people: for the thing was done suddenly."

kids.question: What special feast did Hezekiah invite all Israel and Judah to celebrate in Jerusalem?
kids.option_a: The passover unto the LORD God of Israel
kids.explanation: 2 Chronicles 30:1 — "And Hezekiah sent to all Israel and Judah, and wrote letters also to Ephraim and Manasseh, that they should come to the house of the LORD at Jerusalem, to keep the passover unto the LORD God of Israel."

kids.question: Why was the Passover held in the second month instead of the first month?
kids.option_a: Because the priests had not sanctified themselves in time and the people had not gathered to Jerusalem
kids.option_b: Because the king was sick and could not attend
kids.option_c: Because the temple was not yet cleaned
kids.option_d: Because it rained heavily in the first month
kids.explanation: 2 Chronicles 30:3 — "For they could not keep it at that time, because the priests had not sanctified themselves sufficiently, neither had the people gathered themselves together to Jerusalem."

kids.question: How many extra days did the people celebrate because they were so happy?
kids.option_a: Another seven days they kept it with gladness
kids.explanation: 2 Chronicles 30:23 — "And the whole assembly took counsel to keep other seven days: and they kept other seven days with gladness."

teens.question: What did the couriers proclaim as they went through Israel with Hezekiah's letters?
teens.option_a: Ye children of Israel, turn again unto the LORD God of Abraham, Isaac, and Israel, and he will return to the remnant of you that are escaped out of the hand of the kings of Assyria
teens.option_b: Come to Jerusalem and pay your taxes to King Hezekiah
teens.option_c: The temple has been rebuilt — come and see its glory
teens.option_d: Hezekiah has defeated Assyria — come celebrate the victory
teens.explanation: 2 Chronicles 30:6 — "So the posts went with the letters from the king and his princes throughout all Israel and Judah...saying, Ye children of Israel, turn again unto the LORD God of Abraham, Isaac, and Israel."

teens.question: How did most of the northern tribes respond to the invitation couriers?
teens.option_a: They laughed them to scorn and mocked them
teens.option_b: They gladly received the invitation and came to Jerusalem
teens.option_c: They sent a message asking for more time to prepare
teens.option_d: They consulted their own priests and refused politely
teens.explanation: 2 Chronicles 30:10 — "So the posts passed from city to city through the country of Ephraim and Manasseh even unto Zebulun: but they laughed them to scorn, and mocked them."

teens.question: What did Hezekiah pray for the many people who ate the Passover without the required purification?
teens.option_a: He prayed that the good LORD would pardon every one that prepareth his heart to seek God, though not purified according to the purification of the sanctuary
teens.option_b: He asked the priests to perform purification ceremonies overnight
teens.option_c: He requested that God delay the feast another month
teens.option_d: He offered extra sin offerings on their behalf
teens.explanation: 2 Chronicles 30:18-19 — "But Hezekiah prayed for them, saying, The good LORD pardon every one That prepareth his heart to seek God, the LORD God of his fathers, though he be not cleansed according to the purification of the sanctuary."

teens.question: Which northern tribes actually humbled themselves and came to Jerusalem for the Passover?
teens.option_a: Divers of Asher, Manasseh, and Zebulun humbled themselves and came to Jerusalem
teens.option_b: Only the tribe of Benjamin came from the north
teens.option_c: The tribes of Dan and Naphtali came
teens.option_d: None from the north came — only Judah attended
teens.explanation: 2 Chronicles 30:11 — "Nevertheless divers of Asher and Manasseh and of Zebulun humbled themselves, and came to Jerusalem."

teens.question: What did the people do to the altars and incense altars in Jerusalem during the feast?
teens.option_a: They arose and took away the altars that were in Jerusalem, and all the altars for incense, and cast them into the brook Kidron
teens.option_b: They left them for the priests to dismantle later
teens.option_c: They burned them with fire outside the city
teens.option_d: They buried them under the temple foundations
teens.explanation: 2 Chronicles 30:14 — "And they arose and took away the altars that were in Jerusalem, and all the altars for incense took they away, and cast them into the brook Kidron."

adults.question: What does the Chronicler's description of Hezekiah's Passover as unprecedented since Solomon reveal about its theological significance?
adults.option_a: That Hezekiah's reform represented a restoration of the Solomonic ideal — a united Israel gathered at the Jerusalem sanctuary — making it the greatest act of national reunification since the divided kingdom
adults.option_b: That Hezekiah was greater than Solomon in all ways
adults.option_c: That previous Passovers had been celebrated incorrectly
adults.option_d: That the northern tribes had never celebrated Passover
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 30:26 — "So there was great joy in Jerusalem: for since the time of Solomon the son of David king of Israel there was not the like in Jerusalem."

adults.question: What was the theological argument in Hezekiah's letter for why even the northern remnant should attend the Passover?
adults.option_a: That if they returned to the LORD, their brethren and children would find compassion before their captors and the LORD — gracious and merciful — would not turn away if they returned to him
adults.option_b: That Hezekiah as king of Judah had legal authority over all twelve tribes
adults.option_c: That the temple was the only valid place of worship and ignoring it was sin
adults.option_d: That a political alliance with Judah was their best protection against Assyria
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 30:9 — "For if ye turn again unto the LORD...then your brethren and your children shall find compassion before them that lead them captive...for the LORD your God is gracious and merciful, and will not turn away his face from you, if ye return unto him."

adults.question: How does God respond to Hezekiah's prayer for those who ate the Passover without purification, and what principle does this establish?
adults.option_a: The LORD hearkened to Hezekiah and healed the people — establishing that sincere preparation of heart takes precedence over ritual purity when circumstances prevent full observance
adults.option_b: God required additional sacrifices before accepting the worship
adults.option_c: God accepted only those who were properly purified
adults.option_d: God was silent and no sign was given
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 30:20 — "And the LORD hearkened to Hezekiah, and healed the people."

adults.question: What is the significance of the great assembly's spontaneous decision to keep an additional seven days of celebration?
adults.option_a: It shows the genuineness of the spiritual revival — the people themselves desired to extend their worship without royal compulsion, supplied joyfully by Hezekiah and his officials
adults.option_b: It shows Hezekiah had planned the extended feast from the beginning
adults.option_c: It shows the people were reluctant to return to their work
adults.option_d: It shows the priests needed extra time to finish the sacrifices
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 30:23-24 — "And the whole assembly took counsel to keep other seven days: and they kept other seven days with gladness. For Hezekiah king of Judah did give to the congregation a thousand bullocks and seven thousand sheep."

adults.question: What did Hezekiah and the princes contribute to sustain the extended seven-day feast?
adults.option_a: Hezekiah gave a thousand bullocks and seven thousand sheep, and the princes gave a thousand bullocks and ten thousand sheep, and a great number of priests sanctified themselves
adults.option_b: Only Hezekiah contributed out of the royal treasury
adults.option_c: The Levites gave from the tithe storehouses
adults.option_d: Wealthy merchants donated on behalf of the congregation
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 30:24 — "For Hezekiah king of Judah did give to the congregation a thousand bullocks and seven thousand sheep; and the princes gave to the congregation a thousand bullocks and ten thousand sheep."

adults.question: What was the role of the Levites in teaching the people during the feast, and how does this show the dual function of temple worship?
adults.option_a: The Levites praised the LORD day by day, and Hezekiah spoke comfortably to all the Levites that taught the good knowledge of the LORD — showing worship and instruction as inseparable
adults.option_b: The Levites only performed sacrifices and did not teach
adults.option_c: Teaching happened separately in the synagogues
adults.option_d: Only the high priest addressed the people
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 30:22 — "And Hezekiah spake comfortably unto all the Levites that taught the good knowledge of the LORD: and they did eat throughout the feast seven days, offering peace offerings."

adults.question: How does the Chronicler describe the prayer during the feast ascending to God?
adults.option_a: Their voice was heard and their prayer came up to his holy dwelling place, even unto heaven — showing that corporate worship of a reunited people has special power before God
adults.option_b: Only Hezekiah's private prayer was recorded as heard
adults.option_c: The priests' formal prayers alone ascended to heaven
adults.option_d: The people prayed individually and God heard each separately
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 30:27 — "Then the priests the Levites arose and blessed the people: and their voice was heard, and their prayer came up to his holy dwelling place, even unto heaven."

adults.question: What does the Passover's celebration in the second month rather than the first month reveal about the relationship between law and grace in Chronicles?
adults.option_a: That God's mercy allows legitimate adjustments to ritual law when sincere repentance and seeking of God motivates the participants — the spirit of the law can fulfil its intent even with procedural variation
adults.option_b: That the Mosaic law was no longer binding after the division of the kingdom
adults.option_c: That Hezekiah had authority to rewrite the law as king
adults.option_d: That the second month Passover was an entirely different feast
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 30:2-3 — "For the king had taken counsel...to keep the passover in the second month. For they could not keep it at that time, because the priests had not sanctified themselves," showing pastoral accommodation within covenant faithfulness.

kids.question: After the feast, what did the people break down and destroy throughout Israel and Judah?
kids.option_a: The images and the groves and the high places and the altars
kids.option_d: The bronze gates of the cities
kids.explanation: 2 Chronicles 31:1 — "Now when all this was finished, all Israel that were present went out to the cities of Judah, and brake the images in pieces, and cut down the groves, and threw down the high places and the altars out of all Judah and Benjamin."

kids.question: What did Hezekiah ask the people to bring to support the priests and Levites?
kids.option_a: The portion of the priests and the Levites, and the firstfruits and tithes
kids.option_b: Silver and gold for rebuilding the temple
kids.option_d: Cedar wood for new temple furniture
kids.explanation: 2 Chronicles 31:4 — "Moreover he commanded the people that dwelt in Jerusalem to give the portion of the priests and the Levites, that they might be encouraged in the law of the LORD."

kids.question: What was Hezekiah said to do in every work for the house of God and in the law?
kids.option_a: He did it with all his heart, and prospered
kids.option_b: He was the wisest king since Solomon
kids.option_c: He was the most generous king who ever lived
kids.option_d: He was a great warrior who never lost a battle
kids.explanation: 2 Chronicles 31:21 — "And in every work that he began in the service of the house of God, and in the law, and in the commandments, to seek his God, he did it with all his heart, and prospered."

teens.question: How did Hezekiah reorganize the priests and Levites regarding their duties?
teens.option_a: He appointed the courses of the priests and Levites after their courses, every man according to his service, for burnt offerings and peace offerings, to minister and to give thanks and to praise
teens.option_b: He reduced the number of Levites to save money
teens.option_c: He assigned the Levites only to teaching and not to sacrifice
teens.option_d: He gave the priests roles in civil government
teens.explanation: 2 Chronicles 31:2 — "And Hezekiah appointed the courses of the priests and the Levites after their courses, every man according to his service, the priests and Levites for burnt offerings and for peace offerings, to minister, and to give thanks, and to praise."

teens.question: What did the people bring in abundance after Hezekiah's command about tithes?
teens.option_a: The firstfruits of corn, wine, oil, honey, and all the increase of the field, and the tithe of all things, laid in heaps
teens.option_b: Only a small portion because of the harvest failure
teens.option_c: Exactly the legal minimum required by Moses
teens.option_d: Silver equivalent to a tithe of all produce
teens.explanation: 2 Chronicles 31:5 — "And as soon as the commandment came abroad, the children of Israel brought in abundance the firstfruits of corn, wine, and oil, and honey, and of all the increase of the field."

teens.question: Who was put in charge of the chambers where the contributions and tithes were stored?
teens.option_a: Cononiah the Levite was ruler, and Shimei his brother was the next
teens.option_b: Azariah the chief priest oversaw all storerooms
teens.option_c: Hezekiah's personal treasurer controlled the storerooms
teens.option_d: The elders of each tribe managed their own storerooms
teens.explanation: 2 Chronicles 31:12-13 — "And brought in the offerings and the tithes and the dedicated things faithfully: over which Cononiah the Levite was ruler, and Shimei his brother was the next."

teens.question: What did Azariah the chief priest tell Hezekiah when he asked about the heaps of firstfruits?
teens.option_a: Since the people began to bring the offerings into the house of the LORD, we have had enough to eat and have left plenty — for the LORD hath blessed his people; and that which is left is this great store
teens.option_b: He said the storerooms were nearly empty and more was needed
teens.option_c: He said the Levites had been keeping too large a share for themselves
teens.option_d: He said the heaps were from three years of accumulated tithes
teens.explanation: 2 Chronicles 31:10 — "And Azariah the chief priest of the house of Zadok answered him, and said, Since the people began to bring the offerings into the house of the LORD, we have had enough to eat, and have left plenty: for the LORD hath blessed his people."

teens.question: Who received portions from the tithes besides the male Levites on duty?
teens.option_a: All males from three years old and upward, and all that were reckoned by genealogy among the Levites — their sons and daughters
teens.option_b: Only the Levites themselves, not their families
teens.option_c: Only adult male Levites on active duty
teens.option_d: Levites in the cities who farmed their own land
teens.explanation: 2 Chronicles 31:16-17 — "Beside their genealogy of males, from three years old and upward, even unto every one that entereth into the house of the LORD...And to the genealogy of the Levites."

adults.question: What does the thoroughness of the destruction of high places in verse 1 — covering even Ephraim and Manasseh — reveal about Hezekiah's reform?
adults.option_a: It reveals Hezekiah attempted a pan-Israelite reform reaching into the former northern kingdom, aligning with his Passover invitation and showing his vision of a reunited covenant people
adults.option_b: It reveals he had political ambitions over the north
adults.option_c: It reveals the high places in the north were particularly offensive to Judeans
adults.option_d: It reveals the northern tribes had invited Judean forces to help them
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 31:1 — "All Israel...went out to the cities of Judah, and brake the images in pieces...throughout all Judah and Benjamin, in Ephraim also and Manasseh."

adults.question: What does the detail that Hezekiah gave from his own possessions for the morning and evening burnt offerings show about his reform?
adults.option_a: It shows he put his own wealth behind the worship restoration rather than merely commanding others — true leadership requires personal sacrifice
adults.option_b: It shows he was wealthy beyond measure and could afford any expense
adults.option_c: It shows the temple treasury had been completely emptied by Ahaz
adults.option_d: It shows he did not trust the Levites to manage funds properly
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 31:3 — "He appointed also the king's portion of his substance for the burnt offerings, to wit, for the morning and evening burnt offerings, and the burnt offerings for the sabbaths, and for the new moons, and for the set feasts, as it is written in the law of the LORD."

adults.question: How does the Chronicler's emphasis on genealogy in the distribution of Levitical provisions reflect a broader concern in Chronicles?
adults.option_a: Chronicles repeatedly emphasizes genealogy as the basis of legitimate service and inheritance — proper genealogical registration ensured correct distribution and prevented fraud
adults.option_b: Genealogy was purely administrative with no theological significance
adults.option_c: Genealogy was only relevant for the Davidic dynasty
adults.option_d: Genealogy was a recent innovation introduced by Hezekiah
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 31:17-18 — "Both to the genealogy of the priests by the house of their fathers, and the Levites from twenty years old and upward...And to the genealogy of all their little ones, their wives, and their sons and their daughters."

adults.question: What officials were appointed to administer the chambers and the distribution of tithes under Cononiah?
adults.option_a: Hezekiah commanded to prepare chambers in the house of the LORD; Cononiah the Levite ruled over them with Shimei his brother as deputy, and ten others appointed under him
adults.option_b: The city elders distributed portions without appointed officers
adults.option_c: The chief priest alone distributed from Jerusalem
adults.option_d: The princes of each tribe managed distribution in their territory
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 31:11-13 — "Then Hezekiah commanded to prepare chambers in the house of the LORD...And brought in the offerings...faithfully: over which Cononiah the Levite was ruler, and Shimei his brother was the next. And Jehiel...and Azariah...and Nahath, and Asahel, and Jerimoth...were overseers."

adults.question: What is the significance of the Chronicler noting that Hezekiah did this "in every city of Judah" and not just Jerusalem?
adults.option_a: It shows his reform was not merely a Jerusalem phenomenon but a genuine national transformation reaching every part of the kingdom — thoroughness the Chronicler prizes in true revival
adults.option_b: It shows Hezekiah needed tax revenue from every city
adults.option_c: It shows the Levites were spread too thinly across the land
adults.option_d: It shows the law required temple cities in every region
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 31:19 — "Also of the sons of Aaron the priests, which were in the fields of the suburbs of their cities, in every several city, the men that were expressed by name, to give portions to all the males among the priests."

adults.question: What is the Chronicler's overall summary verdict on Hezekiah, and what three qualities does it cite?
adults.option_a: He wrought that which was good and right and truth before the LORD his God — combining moral goodness, covenant righteousness, and fidelity to truth as a triple standard
adults.option_b: He was the greatest military king since David
adults.option_c: He was faithful in wealth but not in worship
adults.option_d: He was good in his youth but failed in old age
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 31:20 — "And thus did Hezekiah throughout all Judah, and wrought that which was good and right and truth before the LORD his God."

adults.question: How does the abundance of the tithes function theologically in the Chronicler's narrative of chapter 31?
adults.option_a: The overflowing heaps demonstrate the principle that seeking God first leads to material abundance — a direct application of the covenant blessing principle rooted in Deuteronomy
adults.option_b: The tithes are mentioned only as an administrative detail
adults.option_c: The abundance shows the people feared punishment for withholding
adults.option_d: The tithes demonstrate Israel's superior agriculture compared to surrounding nations
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 31:10 — "The LORD hath blessed his people; and that which is left is this great store," showing the tithes as evidence of covenant blessing flowing from faithfulness.

adults.question: What name does the text give to the chief priest of Hezekiah's time, and to what family line does it connect him?
adults.option_a: Azariah the chief priest of the house of Zadok — connecting the priesthood to the Zadokite line that had served since Solomon's dedication of the temple
adults.option_b: Jehoiada of the line of Aaron
adults.option_c: Hilkiah the son of Shallum
adults.option_d: Urijah of the house of Eleazar
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 31:10 — "And Azariah the chief priest of the house of Zadok answered him," — the Zadokite line being the priestly family favoured by Solomon at the very start of the temple era.

kids.question: What great enemy king came to attack Jerusalem in Hezekiah's days?
kids.explanation: 2 Chronicles 32:1 — "After these things, and the establishment thereof, Sennacherib king of Assyria came, and entered into Judah."

kids.question: What did God send to destroy the Assyrian army at Jerusalem?
kids.option_a: An angel who cut off all the mighty men of valour
kids.option_d: A flood that swept away the army
kids.explanation: 2 Chronicles 32:21 — "And the LORD sent an angel, which cut off all the mighty men of valour, and the leaders and captains in the camp of the king of Assyria."

kids.question: After God delivered Jerusalem, Hezekiah became proud. What did he do to make things right?
kids.option_a: He humbled himself for the pride of his heart, so the wrath of the LORD came not upon them
kids.option_b: He offered a hundred bulls as a sacrifice
kids.option_c: He gave all his wealth to the temple treasury
kids.option_d: He asked Isaiah to pray for him
kids.explanation: 2 Chronicles 32:26 — "Notwithstanding Hezekiah humbled himself for the pride of his heart, both he and the inhabitants of Jerusalem, so that the wrath of the LORD came not upon them in the days of Hezekiah."

teens.question: What water project did Hezekiah carry out to deny the Assyrian army access to water?
teens.option_a: He stopped all the fountains and the brook that ran through the midst of the land, saying why should the kings of Assyria find much water?
teens.option_b: He built a great dam across the Jordan
teens.option_c: He poisoned the wells outside Jerusalem
teens.option_d: He diverted a canal into Jerusalem from the north
teens.explanation: 2 Chronicles 32:3-4 — "He took counsel with his princes and his mighty men to stop the waters of the fountains which were without the city...So there was gathered much people together, who stopped all the fountains, and the brook that ran through the midst of the land."

teens.question: What did Sennacherib's servants say to the people of Jerusalem about the God of Hezekiah?
teens.option_a: They spoke against the God of Jerusalem as against the gods of the nations — no god of any nation had delivered his people from Assyria, and neither would the God of Jerusalem
teens.option_b: They promised riches if the city surrendered peacefully
teens.option_c: They said Hezekiah had lied to them about God's power
teens.option_d: They warned that the Assyrian army was undefeatable
teens.explanation: 2 Chronicles 32:19 — "And they spake against the God of Jerusalem, as against the gods of the people of the earth, which were the work of the hands of man."

teens.question: What happened to Sennacherib when he returned to Assyria after his defeat at Jerusalem?
teens.option_a: He went into the house of his god, and some of his own sons smote him there with the sword
teens.option_b: He died of shame and grief within a year
teens.option_c: He was deposed by his army commanders
teens.option_d: He attacked Egypt and was defeated there too
teens.explanation: 2 Chronicles 32:21 — "And he returned with shame of face to his own land. And when he was come into the house of his god, they that came forth of his own bowels slew him there with the sword."

teens.question: What illness did Hezekiah suffer and what did God do regarding the Babylonian ambassadors who came to inquire?
teens.option_a: He was sick unto death and God healed him; God left him concerning the Babylonian ambassadors to try him, that he might know all that was in his heart
teens.option_b: He broke his leg in battle and the prophet healed him
teens.option_c: He was poisoned by Assyrian spies and recovered miraculously
teens.option_d: He had a fever and the people fasted three days for him
teens.explanation: 2 Chronicles 32:24,31 — "In those days Hezekiah was sick to the death...Howbeit in the business of the ambassadors of the princes of Babylon...God left him, to try him, that he might know all that was in his heart."

teens.question: What great wealth and honour did Hezekiah accumulate, showing God's blessing upon him?
teens.option_a: He had exceeding much riches and honour, and made treasuries for silver, gold, precious stones, spices, shields, and pleasant jewels, and all nations brought gifts to him
teens.option_b: He had a powerful army that conquered all surrounding nations
teens.option_c: He received tribute only from Philistia and Edom
teens.option_d: He built the largest palace ever seen in Judah
teens.explanation: 2 Chronicles 32:27-28 — "And Hezekiah had exceeding much riches and honour: and he made himself treasuries for silver, and for gold, and for precious stones...stalls for all manner of beasts, and cotes for flocks. Moreover he provided him cities, and possessions of flocks and herds in abundance."

adults.question: What is the theological significance of the phrase "after these things and the establishment thereof" that opens chapter 32?
adults.option_a: It signals the Chronicler's teaching that faithfulness does not exempt one from trial but makes God's deliverance available — Hezekiah's reforms were tested by the most severe crisis of his reign
adults.option_b: It indicates there was a long gap between the reforms and the invasion
adults.option_c: It shows Sennacherib chose to attack because Hezekiah was weak
adults.option_d: It is a standard literary formula with no theological meaning
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 32:1 — "After these things, and the establishment thereof, Sennacherib king of Assyria came," — the Chronicler places the invasion immediately after the reforms to teach trust under trial.

adults.question: What speech did Hezekiah give to encourage the people, and what is its theological core?
adults.option_a: He said be strong and courageous, be not afraid for the king of Assyria — for there be more with us than with him: with him is an arm of flesh, but with us is the LORD our God to help us and to fight our battles
adults.option_b: He promised victory based on Judah's superior army
adults.option_c: He declared he would personally lead the charge and God would follow
adults.option_d: He ordered everyone to fast and pray without speaking
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 32:7-8 — "Be strong and courageous, be not afraid nor dismayed for the king of Assyria...for there be more with us than with him: With him is an arm of flesh; but with us is the LORD our God to help us, and to fight our battles."

adults.question: What specific claim did Sennacherib's letter make about the God of Hezekiah compared to the gods of other nations?
adults.option_a: That the God of Hezekiah was like the gods of the nations destroyed by Assyria — therefore he would not deliver Jerusalem out of his hand
adults.option_b: That Hezekiah had offended his own God by removing the high places
adults.option_c: That God had told Sennacherib to destroy Jerusalem
adults.option_d: That no god had ever successfully defended a besieged city
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 32:15 — "Now therefore let not Hezekiah deceive you...for no god of any nation or kingdom was able to deliver his people out of mine hand, and out of the hand of my fathers: how much less shall your God deliver you out of mine hand?"

adults.question: How did Hezekiah and Isaiah respond to Sennacherib's blasphemy, combining human action with divine dependence?
adults.option_a: Hezekiah the king and Isaiah the prophet the son of Amoz prayed and cried to heaven, and God sent an angel — prayer was the human contribution and divine intervention was God's response
adults.option_b: Hezekiah relied entirely on his army without prayer
adults.option_c: Isaiah alone prayed while Hezekiah made military preparations only
adults.option_d: They appealed to Egypt for military assistance first
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 32:20-21 — "And for this cause Hezekiah the king, and the prophet Isaiah the son of Amoz, prayed and cried to heaven. And the LORD sent an angel."

adults.question: What does the detail that God left Hezekiah "to try him" regarding the Babylonian ambassadors reveal about divine testing in Chronicles?
adults.option_a: That God withdraws felt presence as a test of whether the heart is truly devoted — prosperity and recovery can become occasions for spiritual failure as much as for gratitude
adults.option_b: That God was displeased with Hezekiah for receiving foreign ambassadors
adults.option_c: That the Babylonian embassy was a political trap Hezekiah should have avoided
adults.option_d: That God was testing whether Hezekiah would share military secrets
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 32:31 — "God left him, to try him, that he might know all that was in his heart."

adults.question: How does the Chronicler handle Hezekiah's illness and the sign of the sundial compared to the parallel account in Kings?
adults.option_a: The Chronicler mentions the sign and recovery briefly, directing readers to the vision of Isaiah the prophet for full details — a cross-reference technique showing awareness of other canonical sources
adults.option_b: The Chronicler gives a longer account than Kings does
adults.option_c: The Chronicler denies the sundial miracle occurred
adults.option_d: The Chronicler replaces the sundial with the tunnel as the key miracle
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 32:24 — "In those days Hezekiah was sick to the death, and prayed unto the LORD: and he spake unto him, and he gave him a sign," with full details found in Isaiah 38.

adults.question: What does Hezekiah's construction of the water conduit to bring Gihon spring water into the city represent strategically and spiritually?
adults.option_a: It represents preparation and faith working together — he secured the city's water supply against siege while trusting God, showing that prudent preparation and prayer are complementary not contradictory
adults.option_b: It shows he trusted engineering more than God
adults.option_c: It was purely a defensive measure with no spiritual dimension
adults.option_d: It was completed after the siege as a memorial to God's deliverance
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 32:30 — "This same Hezekiah also stopped the upper watercourse of Gihon, and brought it straight down to the west side of the city of David."

adults.question: How does the Chronicler's treatment of Hezekiah's pride differ from that of Uzziah's pride, and what does this reveal about repentance?
adults.option_a: Hezekiah humbled himself so the wrath came not upon them in his days — unlike Uzziah who persisted in pride and was permanently judged; repentance makes the critical difference in the Chronicler's theology
adults.option_b: Both were punished equally and permanently
adults.option_c: Hezekiah's pride was not as serious as Uzziah's
adults.option_d: Hezekiah was never proud — the text is speaking of the people alone
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 32:26 — "Notwithstanding Hezekiah humbled himself for the pride of his heart, both he and the inhabitants of Jerusalem, so that the wrath of the LORD came not upon them in the days of Hezekiah."

kids.question: King Manasseh of Judah did many wicked things. What did he rebuild that Hezekiah had destroyed?
kids.explanation: 2 Chronicles 33:3 — "For he built again the high places which Hezekiah his father had broken down."

kids.question: Where was Manasseh taken as a prisoner when God judged him?
kids.explanation: 2 Chronicles 33:11 — "Wherefore the LORD brought upon them the captains of the host of the king of Assyria, which took Manasseh among the thorns, and bound him with fetters, and carried him to Babylon."

kids.question: After Manasseh was taken captive, what did he do that made God bring him back to Jerusalem?
kids.option_a: He humbled himself greatly before the God of his fathers and prayed to God
kids.option_b: He paid a large ransom to the Assyrians
kids.option_c: He promised to rebuild the temple
kids.option_d: He wrote a letter begging God for forgiveness
kids.explanation: 2 Chronicles 33:12-13 — "And when he was in affliction, he besought the LORD his God, and humbled himself greatly before the God of his fathers, And prayed unto him: and he was intreated of him, and heard his supplication, and brought him again to Jerusalem."

teens.question: What specific abominations did Manasseh commit in the house of God itself?
teens.option_a: He built altars for all the host of heaven in the two courts of the house of the LORD, and set a carved image in the house of God
teens.option_b: He burned the temple doors and removed the ark
teens.option_c: He replaced the golden lampstand with an idol of Baal
teens.option_d: He turned the inner sanctuary into a treasury for idols
teens.explanation: 2 Chronicles 33:4-5,7 — "Also he built altars in the house of the LORD...And he built altars for all the host of heaven in the two courts of the house of the LORD...And he set a carved image, the idol which he had made, in the house of God."

teens.question: How does the Chronicler characterize the scale of Manasseh's wickedness compared to the nations God had destroyed?
teens.option_a: Manasseh made Judah and the inhabitants of Jerusalem to err and do worse than the heathen whom the LORD had destroyed before the children of Israel
teens.option_b: Manasseh was only slightly more wicked than Ahaz
teens.option_c: Manasseh's sins were fewer than Jeroboam's
teens.option_d: Manasseh sinned only in his youth and improved with age
teens.explanation: 2 Chronicles 33:9 — "So Manasseh made Judah and the inhabitants of Jerusalem to err, and to do worse than the heathen, whom the LORD had destroyed before the children of Israel."

teens.question: After Manasseh's restoration what did he do to the temple and the city?
teens.option_a: He took away the strange gods and the idol out of the house of the LORD, repaired the altar of the LORD, commanded Judah to serve the LORD, and built an outer wall for the city of David
teens.option_b: He demolished the temple and built a new one
teens.option_c: He restored only the outer courts but kept the inner shrine for idols
teens.option_d: He gave all the temple gold to the Assyrian king as tribute
teens.explanation: 2 Chronicles 33:15-16 — "And he took away the strange gods, and the idol out of the house of the LORD...And he repaired the altar of the LORD, and sacrificed thereon peace offerings and thank offerings, and commanded Judah to serve the LORD God of Israel."

teens.question: What was the conduct of Manasseh's son Amon who succeeded him?
teens.option_a: Amon trespassed more and more and did evil in the sight of the LORD as his father Manasseh had done, but unlike Manasseh he humbled not himself before the LORD
teens.option_b: Amon reversed all Manasseh's evil and followed Hezekiah's reforms
teens.option_c: Amon was assassinated before he could do either good or evil
teens.option_d: Amon ruled wisely but was weak militarily
teens.explanation: 2 Chronicles 33:22-23 — "But he did that which was evil in the sight of the LORD, as did Manasseh his father...And humbled not himself before the LORD, as Manasseh his father had humbled himself; but Amon trespassed more and more."

teens.question: What was the fate of Amon and who succeeded him?
teens.option_a: His servants conspired against him and slew him in his own house, and the people of the land made Josiah his son king in his stead
teens.option_b: Amon died of a sickness after only six months as king
teens.option_c: The Assyrians captured Amon and he died in captivity
teens.option_d: Amon was killed in battle against the Philistines
teens.explanation: 2 Chronicles 33:24-25 — "And his servants conspired against him, and slew him in his own house. But the people of the land slew all them that had conspired against king Amon; and the people of the land made Josiah his son king in his stead."

adults.question: What does the Chronicler's inclusion of Manasseh's repentance and restoration reveal about his theological purpose, given that Kings omits this narrative?
adults.option_a: It demonstrates the Chronicler's central retribution principle: repentance — even after extreme wickedness — brings divine forgiveness and restoration, showing God's mercy overrides the severity of sin
adults.option_b: It shows the Chronicler had access to sources Kings did not have
adults.option_c: It shows the Chronicler was writing propaganda for a later king
adults.option_d: It shows Manasseh's wickedness was exaggerated by the Kings author
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 33:13 — "And prayed unto him: and he was intreated of him, and heard his supplication, and brought him again to Jerusalem into his kingdom. Then Manasseh knew that the LORD he was God."

adults.question: What was the specific mechanism of Manasseh's capture — how does the text describe the manner of his arrest?
adults.option_a: The captains of the king of Assyria took Manasseh among the thorns, and bound him with fetters, and carried him to Babylon
adults.option_b: He surrendered voluntarily to avoid a siege of Jerusalem
adults.option_c: He was captured in battle while leading his army
adults.option_d: He was betrayed by his own nobles to the Assyrians
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 33:11 — "Wherefore the LORD brought upon them the captains of the host of the king of Assyria, which took Manasseh among the thorns, and bound him with fetters, and carried him to Babylon."

adults.question: What does "Manasseh knew that the LORD he was God" reveal as the purpose of his suffering, and how does this relate to the Chronicler's use of affliction?
adults.option_a: The Chronicler presents affliction as God's instrument for producing true knowledge of God — captivity broke Manasseh's idolatrous self-sufficiency and brought him to genuine acknowledgment of the LORD
adults.option_b: The suffering was purely punitive with no redemptive purpose
adults.option_c: Manasseh's knowledge was merely intellectual, not transforming
adults.option_d: The restoration was a political arrangement with no spiritual significance
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 33:13 — "Then Manasseh knew that the LORD he was God," — the climactic statement showing the redemptive purpose of his chastisement.

adults.question: Why does the Chronicler note that the people still sacrificed in the high places after Manasseh's restoration, even though his personal repentance was genuine?
adults.option_a: To show that a leader's personal reform, even when genuine, cannot fully undo the deep-rooted idolatry his earlier example embedded in the people — leadership's negative influence outlasts repentance
adults.option_b: To show Manasseh's repentance was insincere
adults.option_c: To show the high places were legal worship sites at this period
adults.option_d: To show the Levites had failed to teach the people properly
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 33:17 — "Nevertheless the people did sacrifice still in the high places, yet unto the LORD their God only."

adults.question: What building work did the restored Manasseh carry out that showed his changed priorities?
adults.option_a: He built an outer wall to the city of David on the west side of Gihon in the valley and raised it up to Ophel, and put captains of war in all the fenced cities of Judah
adults.option_b: He rebuilt the entire temple from the ground up
adults.option_c: He constructed a new palace for the priests
adults.option_d: He built forty altars for the LORD throughout Judah
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 33:14 — "Now after this he built a wall without the city of David, on the west side of Gihon, in the valley, even to the entering in at the fish gate, and compassed about Ophel, and raised it up a very great height."

adults.question: What was the source cited for the prayer of Manasseh and the account of his humbling?
adults.option_a: The words of the seers who spake to him in the name of the LORD God of Israel, written in the book of the kings of Israel; also his prayer is among the sayings of the Hozai
adults.option_b: Only the general chronicle of Kings
adults.option_c: The writings of the prophet Micah
adults.option_d: The records kept by the Levitical priests
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 33:18-19 — "Now the rest of the acts of Manasseh, and his prayer unto his God...written in the book of the kings of Israel. His prayer also...behold, they are written among the sayings of the seers."

adults.question: How old was Manasseh when he began to reign, how long did he reign, and what does this make his reign in terms of length among Judah's kings?
adults.option_a: He was twelve years old and reigned fifty-five years in Jerusalem — the longest reign of any king of Judah
adults.option_b: He was twenty and reigned forty years
adults.option_c: He was sixteen and reigned forty-five years
adults.option_d: He was eight and reigned thirty-one years
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 33:1 — "Manasseh was twelve years old when he began to reign, and he reigned fifty and five years in Jerusalem," making his the longest reign recorded for any king of Judah.

adults.question: What does the contrast between Manasseh's repentance bearing fruit and Amon's lack of repentance reveal about the Chronicler's view of inherited religion?
adults.option_a: It shows that each generation must personally seek God — a father's genuine repentance cannot substitute for a son's own turning to God; Amon had the example before him but chose not to follow it
adults.option_b: It shows that sons always repeat their fathers' sins
adults.option_c: It shows that Amon was too young to understand repentance
adults.option_d: It shows Manasseh's repentance was not genuine after all
adults.explanation: 2 Chronicles 33:23 — "And humbled not himself before the LORD, as Manasseh his father had humbled himself; but Amon trespassed more and more," — personal accountability before God is non-transferable.

kids.question: How old was Josiah when he became king?
kids.explanation: 2 Chronicles 34:1 — "Josiah was eight years old when he began to reign, and he reigned in Jerusalem one and thirty years."

kids.question: What important book was found in the temple when Josiah was repairing it?
kids.option_a: The book of the law of the LORD given by Moses
kids.option_b: The book of the prophecies of Isaiah
kids.option_c: The book of the psalms of David
kids.option_d: The book of the genealogies of the priests
kids.explanation: 2 Chronicles 34:14-15 — "And when they brought out the money that was brought into the house of the LORD, Hilkiah the priest found a book of the law of the LORD given by Moses. And Hilkiah answered and said to Shaphan the scribe, I have found the book of the law in the house of the LORD."

kids.question: What did Josiah do when the book of the law was read to him?
kids.option_a: He rent his clothes because of the wrath of the LORD poured out on them for not keeping the law
kids.option_b: He had it copied and sent to every city
kids.option_c: He read it out loud to all the people immediately
kids.option_d: He placed it in the ark of the covenant
kids.explanation: 2 Chronicles 34:19 — "And it came to pass, when the king had heard the words of the law, that he rent his clothes."

teens.question: At what age did Josiah begin to seek the God of David his father, and what did he do in his twelfth year?
teens.option_a: In his eighth year as king, being sixteen, he began to seek God; in his twelfth year he began to purge Judah and Jerusalem of the high places, groves, carved images, and molten images
teens.option_b: At age eight he began to seek God; at twelve he built the temple
teens.option_c: At age eighteen he began to seek God; at twenty-two he purged Judah
teens.option_d: At age sixteen he began to seek God; at twenty he cleansed the temple
